title: The score decreases when the number of statements decrease
body:
### Steps to reproduce

Use the following example to get a negative score:
```
from typing import Optional

a, b = 1

a.test
```

Notice that removing the unused import will result in a lower score.

### Current behavior

The removal of the unused import will result in a lower score.

### Expected behavior

Removing a fault should result in an increased score. Unfortunately we rely on the number of statements as a factor which will affect the result in the aforementioned example as the number of statements decreased after fixing the issue.

title: False negative when a non-trivial assignment always evaluates to True
body:
<!--
  Hi there! Thank you for wanting to make pylint better.

  Before you submit this, make sure that this feature wasn't
  already requested or if it is not already implemented in the master branch.
-->

### Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe

Compare the following snippets:

```python
b = "asd"
a = "a" or False
```

and:

```python
b = "asd"
a = "a" in b or False
```

I actually came across a codebase where `in b` was forgotten, which resulted in a variable always evaluating to True, causing a bug. It would be great to `pylint` warn that a non-trivial expression always evaluates to True.

### Describe the solution you'd like

In case of a boolean assignment other than `a = True`, especially if other variables are mentioned, throw a warning that says that the statement will always be true.

title: log-and-reraise antipattern linter
body:
### Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe
I don't know how common it is, but I've several times seen the anti-pattern of catching an exception, logging its traceback then re-raising it.

This usually leads to duplicated tracebacks and lots of noise as the exception is either going to be logged again by something higher up the stack, or it's going to fall out of the system and get printed normally.

### Describe the solution you'd like
A lint error if an exception handler uses `.exception()` or `<any logging method>(..., exc_info=True)` then:
* re-raises the exception as-is
* raises a new exception without suppressing chaining (`raise <exc> from None`)

In Python 3, logging then raising `from None` is OK to avoid losing the current traceback when chaining is undesirable, otherwise the traceback being logged; if the lint is also built for Python 2, new exceptions are not chained so it's also fine.

title: Branching mistake leading to unused variable: to be detected
body:
Hello,

The following code
1. defines a filter which depends of an `if` condition
2. should apply the filter on a data set
3. ... but only applies the filter on one branch of the `if`, instead of the common branch:

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python
"""lorem ipsum"""

import numpy as np


def get(greater):
    """lorem ipsum"""
    ret = np.arange(10)
    if greater:
        f = ret > 5
        ret = ret[f].copy()  # The bug is here ...
    else:
        f = ret <= 5  # f is not used - to be detected => pylint feature request (W0602?)
    # ... the line of the bug shoud be here instead
    return ret


if __name__ == "__main__":
    print(get(True))
    print(get(False))
```

It would be great to have a warning issued in such case. I think that `unused-variable (W0612)` would be accurate.

Thanks a lot for your help and support.

```bash
astroid 2.4.2
Python 3.8.5 (default, Sep  3 2020, 21:29:08) [MSC v.1916 64 bit (AMD64)]

> tree .
.
├──bug
│  ├── __init__.py
│  └── module.py
└──run_bug.py
```
with `bug/__init__.py`:
```python
from .module import *

__all__ = module.__all__
```
and `bug/module.py`:
```python
__all__ = ['func']

def func() -> None:
    print('works')

```
and `run_bug.py`:
```python
from bug import func

func()

```
code works:
```bash
> python -c "import bug ; bug.func()"
works
```
and this also works:
```bash
> python run_bug.py
works
```
and bug is here:
```bash
> pylint bug run_bug --disable=C
************* Module bug
bug\__init__.py:3:10: E1101: Class 'module' has no '__all__' member (no-member)
bug\__init__.py:3:10: E0602: Undefined variable 'module' (undefined-variable)
```
Problem is that when `*` is imported from `bug.module`, `module` becomes imported to `bug`, but PyLint thinks that it doesn't.
All I wanted is to delegate filling of `__all__` to submodules, like in [asyncio](https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/master/Lib/asyncio/__init__.py).
Explanation is [here](https://youtu.be/0oTh1CXRaQ0?t=1599).

title: Spurious `no-member` errors with modules named `builtins` on py3
body:
<!--
  Hi there! Thank you for discovering and submitting an issue.

  Before you submit this, make sure that the issue doesn't already exist
  or if it is not closed.

  Is your issue fixed on the preview release?: pip install pylint astroid --pre -U

-->

pylint gives incorrect `no-member` errors when checking a file that does a `from`-import of a module named "builtins" in python3 and then accesses any name that are not attributes of `dict` types. (My guess is that this also would have happened in python2 with modules named `__builtin__`, but that's much less likely to happen in IRL code than a module called `foopkg.builtins`.)

This does not occur when doing `import foopkg.builtins as builtins`, only when doing `from foopkg import builtins`. It does not matter whether the module is aliased to some other name (eg, `... as builtins_mod`).

### Steps to reproduce
Commands to set up a test case:

```
mkdir subpkg
touch subpkg/__init__.py
echo "foo = object()" > subpkg/builtins.py
echo "foo = object()" > subpkg/any_other_module_name.py
cat << EOF > no_error_any_other_module.py
from subpkg import any_other_module_name as builtins_mod
bar = builtins_mod.foo
EOF
cat << EOF > no_error_import_as.py
import subpkg.builtins as builtins_mod
bar = builtins_mod.foo
EOF
cat << EOF > gives_error_from_import.py
from subpkg import builtins as builtins_mod
bar = builtins_mod.foo # will have incorrect no-member
baz = builtins_mod.pop # this *should* be a no-member error, but doesn't get noticed.
EOF
```

Pylint run & output:
```
$ pylint --version
pylint 2.4.4
astroid 2.3.3
Python 3.7.6 (default, Jan  2 2020, 11:24:00)
[GCC 8.3.0]
$ pylint -E --rcfile=/dev/null gives_error_from_import.py no_error_any_other_module.py no_error_import_as.py
************* Module gives_error_from_import
gives_error_from_import.py:2:6: E1101: Instance of 'dict' has no 'foo' member (no-member)
```

This does not occur with pylint 1.6.5, but does occur with 1.7.6 (with python3.6, 1.7.6 does not work with python3.7 (#2241)). This makes it similar to #1606, but I don't think this is a duplicate of that issue - this occurs with any (even trivial, as shown above) modules named `builtins`, and is unrelated to anything weird that `future.builtins` is doing, so the fix suggested in that issue wouldn't resolve this.

I have set up a test case in https://github.com/AdamG/pylint/commit/f2e701938bcd6bedc966941d5c81d66c006feb34 that is currently failing, but should work as a regression test once the underlying issue is fixed.

### Current behavior

When doing a from-import, gives an error on access of `builtins_mod.foo`, even though it exists in that module.

The error message always indicates that pylint thinks that the `builtins` object is a `dict`, which points to [checkers.utils.is_builtin()](https://github.com/PyCQA/pylint/blob/d128c276435c379325ad89d0680f889e16364d1f/pylint/checkers/utils.py#L278-L290) being somehow involved. I tried a few things in VariablesChecker and TypeChecker, but nothing seemed to make a difference; i'm not familiar enough with this codebase/astroid to get much further.

### Expected behavior
It should not give the incorrect no-member error. It certainly shouldn't conditionally occur based on the name of the imported module.

### pylint --version output

see above

title: Incorrect E1101 (no-member) using SQLAlchemy @hybrid_property
body:
<!--

### Steps to reproduce
1. Reproducing code [wrong_no_member.txt](https://github.com/PyCQA/pylint/files/4037739/wrong_no_member.txt)

```
from sqlalchemy import Column, Boolean
from sqlalchemy.ext.declarative import declarative_base
from sqlalchemy.ext.hybrid import hybrid_property
from sqlalchemy.schema import PrimaryKeyConstraint

Base = declarative_base()


class Table(Base):
    __tablename__ = 'table'
    __table_args__ = (PrimaryKeyConstraint('column1'),)
    _column1 = Column('column1', Boolean)
    column2 = Column('column2', Boolean)

    @hybrid_property
    def column1(self):
        return self._column1

    @column1.setter
    def column1(self, column1):
        self._column1 = column1


Table.column1.op('*')
Table.column2.op('*')
```
2. Just run as `pylint wrong_no_member.py`
### Current behavior
Complains about `column1`, `wrong_no_member.py:24:0: E1101: Method 'column1' has no 'op' member (no-member)`

No errors

```
$ pylint --version
pylint 2.4.4

Python 3.6.6 (default, May 14 2019, 11:57:55)
[GCC 7.4.0]
```

There's a few other issues in this repo and the astroid repo about various issues with properties, https://github.com/PyCQA/pylint/issues/1061 is probably the closest.

title: Support pragma rule "parameters" for narrower disables
body:
### Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe
Given the classic situation of a function that will be called with two args, but I only use one:

```python3
def func(arg, arg2):
    return arg + 1
```
...I can either use `_` or `disable=unused-argument` to suppress the error. `_` is reasonable for this case, but the disable is too broad: it ignores _any_ unused arguments, not just the one I actually intended to ignore. This becomes more of an issue if the caller uses keyword args:

```python3
def func(*, arg, arg2):
    return arg + 1

func(arg=1, arg2=2)
```

Since the `arg2` name is part of the interface `func` needs to provide, I'm *forced* to use `disable=unused-argument` and accept the risk that a future breaking change will not be caught by pylint.

### Describe the solution you'd like
I've been searching for this for like 20 minutes, and only found [one comment](https://github.com/PyCQA/pylint/issues/216#issuecomment-163174108) that talks about it (I certainly could just be using bad search terms). With that proposed feature, I could just do:

```python3
def func(*, arg, arg2):
    # pylint: disable=unused-argument(arg2)
    return arg + 1

func(arg=1, arg2=2)
```
...and avoid the broad disable. "Explicit is better than implicit", as they say :)

There are probably several rules that would benefit from this, in addition to `unused-argument`. Certainly `unused-variable`, `unused-import`, etc, but also `redefined-builtin`, etc... likely most or all the `pylint.checkers.variables` rules.

title: Autoformatter integration: Stop no line-too-long errors that your formatter cannot fix, etc.
body:
One feature we've implemented internally on top of pylint at Google is to have line-too-long warnings be silenced if running an autoformatter on the code cannot fix the length of those lines.  Our autoformatting is built around [yapf](https://pypi.org/project/yapf/).

The core of the logic we use for getting the data to make this decision is this (snipped out and contributed here from our existing implementation):

```python
try:
    old_contents, _, _ = yapflib.yapf_api.ReadFile(filename)
    style_config = ('{{based_on_style: chromium, '
                    'indent_width: {}, '
                    'split_before_named_assigns: True, '
                    'column_limit: {}}}'.format(indent_width, max_line_length))
    new_contents, _ = yapf_api.FormatCode(
            old_contents, style_config=style_config, verify=False)
    operations_group = difflib.SequenceMatcher(
            None, old_contents.splitlines(), new_contents.splitlines())
except Exception:  # pylint: disable=broad-except
    # formatters are not perfect and can explode, don't block lint on that.
    # TODO log the error.
    operations_group = []

# We have a function just return operations_group above; this loop to check it for
# line-to-long is another function called by the fixer making that API call:
for i1, i2, old_longest_line, new_longest_line in operations_group:
    # 'line' is 1-indexed, the diff offsets are 0-indexed.
    if i1 < line <= i2 and old_longest_line > new_longest_line:
        return False  # the original line would have been made shorter.
return True  # the formatter can fix the line, emit the lint warning.
```

So that findings can process the diff to see if their warning would go away via formatting, as that example loop does.

We memoize the formatting computation so that each individual lint finding that wants to check formatting results to see if the formatter could do anything with their area of the code in question can retrieve that diff to find out without redoing the often expensive yapf_lib.FormatCode operation (as that operates internally on the entire file).

I assume this could also be implemented on top of [black](https://pypi.org/project/black/).  It made sense to use the same formatter we use throughout the codebase (probably wise for any project - consistency between tooling is key to productivity).  Figuring out how to make which formatting engine to use selectable or pluggable for anyone who wants such a feature implemented in the main pylint project itself would be worthwhile.  I expect the API call pattern to be similar regardless.  H/T to @wes-turner for pointing out that this may be of interest in upstream pylint - our current implementation is tied to too many other internal details right now - not something to simply make a PR out of _yet_, but I'm filing this so that the demonstrably useful concept is at least public.

**Upsides**: There is much more than just this line-to-long squelching you can do with autoformatter integration.  line-too-long was just the start for us.  We've also been able to have entire classes of lint warnings also emit suggested edits local to the warning that feed into code review and/or editor integrations to make magic buttons to just apply the individual suggested edit directly.  saving developers and reviewers time.

_Potential downsides_: Supporting this would add an additional, possibly optional, dependency from pylint on yapf, black, or whatnot.  If those deps are considered too heavyweight, I'd imagine this as not necessarily being declared in the default deps of the pylint package, but with conditional imports gating the feature being active.

title: False warning when combining parameters in numpy style doctring
body:
<!--
  Hi there! Thank you for discovering and submitting an issue.

  Before you submit this, make sure that the issue doesn't already exist
  or if it is not closed.

  Is your issue fixed on the preview release?: pip install pylint astroid --pre -U

-->

### Steps to reproduce
With the following example code i encounter warnings that should not pop up.
It does not seem to be fixed in the preview release. I'm using the command line option load-plugins=pylint.extensions.docparams.

```python
"""Test module to show a suspected bug"""

def testpass(valx, valy, valz):
    """Test method that pylint is okay with

    Parameters
    ----------
    valx : int
        description of valx
    valy : int
        description of valy
    valz : int
        description of valz

    Return
    ------
    int
        sum of valx, valy, valz
    """
    return valx + valy + valz

def testpass2(valx, valy, valz):
    """Test method that pylint is okay with

    Parameters
    ----------
    valx, valy, valz : int
        description of valx, valy, valz

    Return
    ------
    int
        sum of valx, valy, valz
    """
    return valx + valy + valz

def testfail(valx, valy, valz, factor):
    """Test method that pylint is NOT okay with

    Parameters
    ----------
    valx, valy, valz : int
        description of valx, valy, valz
    factor: int
        description of factor

    Return
    ------
    int
        sum of valx, valy, valz multiplied by factor
    """
    return (valx + valy + valz) * factor

```
### Current behavior
The following warnings show up:
* "valx, valy, valz" missing in parameter documentation; pyling(missing-param-doc) [37, 1]
* "valx, valy, valz" missing in parameter type documentation; pyling(missing-param-doc) [37, 1]

### Expected behavior
No warnings should show up, as described on https://numpydoc.readthedocs.io/en/latest/format.html
"When two or more input parameters have exactly the same type, shape and description, they can be combined"

### pylint --version output
Instead of `pylint --version output` the command `python -m pylint --version` was used due to system setup. As I don't know if there are any hidden differences I decided to mention that.

pylint 2.4.4
astroid 2.3.3
Python 3.7.0 (v3.7.0:1bf9cc5093, Jun 27 2018, 04:59:51) [MSC v.1914 64 bit (AMD64)]

title: using-constant-test triggers on a property when using unknown property decorators
body:
The `using-constant-test` warning shows up on uses of things that pylint does not understand are properties when it could not inspect the decorator implementation.

### Reproduction

Given the following code, run pylint (2.4.4 in my case) on it.  Note the comment.

#### module.py

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python3
"""Module."""

# A PyPI package - run pylint in a venv _without_ this installed!
import cached_property


class Klass:
    """Klass."""

    def __init__(self, arg):
        self.arg = arg

    def meth(self):
        if self._condition:
            print('truth')

    @cached_property.cached_property
    def _condition(self):
        return bool(self.arg)


def main():
    k = Klass(1)
    k.meth()


if __name__ == '__main__':
    main()
```

Assuming you follow the comment in the code and make sure you do not have cached_property installed within the environment you are running pylint in, you'll get the following in the errors:

```
$ pylint module.py
************* Module module
...
module.py:15:8: W0125: Using a conditional statement with a constant value (using-constant-test)
...
```

This is true even if you add `cached_property.cached_property` to your pylintrc's `[BASIC]` `property-classes` list.

It appears that `property-classes` is not used for the `using-constant-test` error check, only some other naming related ones.  We should have functions or methods defined with a decorator that appears in `property-classes` not fail this check.

I'm not familiar enough with the code to understand where the logic that leads to a built-in property declaration working while this one does not.

title: False negative for uninitialized variable usage in negative comprehensions
body:
<!--

### Steps to reproduce
(moved from #3487)

```python3
b = [x for x in range(10) if y % 2 == 0 for y in range(x)]
```

### Current behavior
```
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Your code has been rated at 10.00/10 (previous run: 10.00/10, +0.00)
```

### Expected behavior
Semantically, the comprehension should be roughly equivalent to the following:
```python3
for x in range(10):
    if y % 2 == 0:
        for y in range(x):
            x
```
As such, the usage of y in the if statement is invalid, and should be reported as usage before assignment.

### pylint --version output
```
pylint 2.4.4
astroid 2.3.3
Python 3.7.6 (default, Dec 30 2019, 19:38:28) 
[Clang 11.0.0 (clang-1100.0.33.16)]
```

title: Disable certain messages by default
body:
Following on from the discussion on #746, this is a draft proposal to start disabling certain messages by default. Furthermore, to make it easier to run pylint side by side with flake8, we want to document which messages are duplicated in flake8 and possible make them easier to disable as a group.

The following table contains every pylint message excluding those from plugins and py3k checks. For each message it is noted whether it is a style check or a functional check (by functional I mean it validates some sort of run time behaviour of the code being linted or checks for programmer mistakes) or other (when it's technically a style check but I don't think we would want to disable it when a user opts out of style checks, or if the message doesn't fit in the other categories), whether it duplicates a message in flake8, and **my opinion** on whether I think it should be disabled by default so that we can use it as a discussion point. I normally prefer the linter to be quite strict so it might be worth gathering user feedback on this. Another option would be to give messages levels of strictness and you pick a level to lint at.

title: no-member reported when accessing a @property of parent class
body:
I'm not sure if this bug is in `pylint`, `astroid` or `WTForms`. I am having trouble reproducing it with similar code to what is in `WTForms`.

### Steps to reproduce
1. Install `WTForms` and create a sub-class of the `Form` class which accesses `self.data` (call it `BadForm` for argument's sake).
2. Run pylint against the class

`self.data` in this case is a reference [to a method](https://github.com/wtforms/wtforms/blob/master/src/wtforms/form.py#L137) which has been decorated with `@property` on the `wtforms.form.BaseForm` class (from which `wtforms.form.Form` inherits).

### Current behavior
The following error is reported: `E1101: Instance of 'BadForm' has no 'data' member (no-member)`

### Expected behavior
No `no-member` error is reported.

### pylint --version output
```
astroid==2.4.0
isort==4.3.21
lazy-object-proxy==1.4.3
MarkupSafe==1.1.1
mccabe==0.6.1
pylint==2.5.0
six==1.14.0
toml==0.10.0
typed-ast==1.4.1
wrapt==1.12.1
WTForms==2.3.1
```
Below is some code demonstrating the problem, and my attempts to reproduce it without using `WTForms` directly:
```python
"""Demonstrate false positive for no-member."""
from wtforms.form import BaseForm, FormMeta, Form
from wtforms.meta import DefaultMeta


# pylint: disable=too-few-public-methods,missing-function-docstring,missing-class-docstring,too-many-arguments


# This demonstrates the exact error I am seeing:
class BadForm(Form):
    """A class demonstrating the issue that appears when inheriting from `Form`."""

    def do_something_with_data(self):
        return self.data


# This following two classes demonstrate the @property access working without an error.
class ClassWithProperty:
    """A class with a property."""

    @property
    def a_property(self):
        return "this works"


class AccessPropertyOk(ClassWithProperty):
    """A class that accesses a property on its parent."""

    def do_something(self):
        return self.a_property


# It doesn't seem to have anything to do with how many classes deep the inheritance chain is:
class IsItBecauseOfSeveralClassesInTheHierarchy(AccessPropertyOk):

    def something_else(self):
        return self.a_property


# The error doesn't appear when inheriting from the direct parent of `wtforms.forms.Form`:
class BaseFormChild(BaseForm):

    def do_something_with_data(self):
        return self.data


# Ok, so lets try re-implementing the hierarchy from `WTForms`. This
# is basically what is in `wtforms.forms.Form`
class FormWithMetaclassAndMeta(BaseForm, metaclass=FormMeta):

    Meta = DefaultMeta

    def do_something_with_data(self):
        return self.data


# I don't understand why this does not report an error. I can't see
# how this differs from `BadForm` in any material way:
class ChildForm(FormWithMetaclassAndMeta):

    def something(self):
        return self.data
```

title: syntax-error incorectly detected inside a type-comment with python 3.8
body:
### Steps to reproduce

"requirements.txt" file for the code-example below:

```
astroid==2.4.0
attrs==19.3.0
autopep8==1.5.2
isort==4.3.21
lazy-object-proxy==1.4.3
mccabe==0.6.1
mypy==0.770
mypy-extensions==0.4.3
psutil==5.7.0
psycopg2-binary==2.8.5
pycodestyle==2.5.0
pylint==2.5.0
six==1.14.0
SQLAlchemy==1.3.16
sqlalchemy-stubs==0.3
toml==0.10.0
typed-ast==1.4.1
typing-extensions==3.7.4.2
wrapt==1.12.1
```

Create a file with the following content:

```py
# pylint: disable=too-few-public-methods, missing-docstring
from typing import Set

from sqlalchemy import Column, ForeignKeyConstraint, String
from sqlalchemy.ext.declarative import declarative_base
from sqlalchemy.orm import relationship

Base = declarative_base()


class Device(Base):  # type: ignore
    __tablename__ = 'device'
    hostname = Column(String, primary_key=True)
    ports = relationship(  # type: Set[Port]
        "Port",
        back_populates="device",
        collection_class=set
    )


class Port(Base):  # type: ignore
    __tablename__ = 'port'
    __table_args__ = (
        ForeignKeyConstraint(
            ['hostname'],
            ['device.hostname'],
            ondelete='CASCADE',
            onupdate='CASCADE',
        ),
    )
    hostname = Column(String, primary_key=True)
    label = Column(String, primary_key=True)
    device = relationship(  # type: Device
        "Device",
        back_populates="ports",
        uselist=False,
    )
```

Run pylint on that file.

### Current behavior

pylint currently raises a syntax-error on line 14:

```
************* Module model
samemory/model.py:14:36: E0001: invalid syntax (<unknown>, line 14) (syntax-error)
```

### Expected behavior

No syntax error should be raised

### pylint --version output

title: import-self / no-member not emitted for modules named after stdlib modules
body:
pylint seems to incorrectly detect a self-import on a module that is named after a stdlib module.

### Background

`pytest` has at least two modules, that have the same name as modules from the stdlib: 
 * `_pytest.logging`  (imports `logging`)
 * `_pytest.warnings`  (imports `warnings`)

### Steps to reproduce
1. Create a file `/tmp/project/logging.py` with the following content

```
import logging
print(logging.CRITICAL)
```
2. Run pylint on this file `(cd /tmp && pylint project/logging.py)`

```
************* Module logging
project/logging.py:1:0: C0114: Missing module docstring (missing-module-docstring)
project/logging.py:1:0: W0406: Module import itself (import-self)
project/logging.py:4:6: E1101: Module 'logging' has no 'CRITICAL' member (no-member)
```

3. Run python `(cd /tmp && python project/logging.py)`
It outputs
```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "project/logging.py", line 1, in <module>
    import logging
  File "/tmp/project/logging.py", line 3, in <module>
    logging.CRITICAL
AttributeError: module 'logging' has no attribute 'CRITICAL'
```
4. Test that the import works `(cd /tmp && python -m project.logging)`
It outputs
`50`

### Expected behavior

Since importing the module using `python -m` works, pylint should IMO also not complain about self-imports.

### pylint --version output

```
pylint 2.6.0-dev1
astroid 2.5.0
Python 3.8.2 | packaged by conda-forge | (default, Mar 23 2020, 18:16:37) 
[GCC 7.3.0]
```

title: called code side effect
body:
Spin off from bug #3611 to reduce noise.

### Steps to reproduce
1. fresh virtualenv using python's `venv`
2. `pip install pylint flask pyserial`
3. `grut.py` at root of venv directory:
```python
import flask
import serial


def initialize():
    print(flask.Blueprint)
    #print(flask.Config.from_pyfile)
    #print(flask.Config.from_json)
    try:
        serial.Serial()
    except serial.SerialException as exc:
        if isinstance(exc.strerror, str) and "could not open port" in exc.strerror:
            print(exc)
            return
        raise
```
4.
```console
$ pylint -E grut.py
************ Module grut
grut.py:12:70: E1135: Value 'exc.strerror' doesn't support membership test (unsupported-membership-test)
```
5. in `grut.py`, uncomment the 2nd or 3rd `print()` instead of the 1st one
6. error goes away

### Open questions

1. I'm not sure what the "correct" pylint behavior should be. To me, `OSError.strerror` is a `str` so the membership test is a false negative. It could also be `None` but the `isinstance()` gets us in path where we know for sure `in` is going to be allowed.
2. The really tricky bug here is that the order of code analysis changes pylint output, which sounds like a real bug.

### Useful information (I think…)

1. `serial.SerialException` inherits from `IOError`
   - https://github.com/pyserial/pyserial/blob/v3.4/serial/serialutil.py#L90
2. `IOError` is an "alias" (as per the CPython code) of `OSError`
   - https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/v3.7.7/Objects/exceptions.c#L2590
3. the flask code which gets pylint confused reraises `IOError` exceptions with a modified `strerror` attribute:
   - https://github.com/pallets/flask/blob/1.1.2/src/flask/config.py#L136 (`from_pyfile`)
   - https://github.com/pallets/flask/blob/1.1.2/src/flask/config.py#L200 (`from_json`)
4. the link with bug #3611 was that I found this bug through different module names on the command line, managed to narrow it down to 2 files and then reduced it to a single test case file

### pylint --version output
```console
$ pylint --version
pylint 2.5.2
astroid 2.4.1
Python 3.7.7 (default, Apr 22 2020, 23:22:50) 
[GCC 9.3.0]
```

title: False negative ``bare-except`` if the except re-raises
body:
bare-except doesn't trigger if the except body directly contains a raise call.

pylint 2.4.4
astroid 2.3.3
Python 3.7.3+ (heads/3.7:e64d21b187, Apr 23 2019, 10:57:08)

```python
def foo(arg):
    try:
        pass
    except: # doesn't trigger bare-except
        raise Exception()
```
```python
def foo(arg):
    try:
        pass
    except: # doesn't trigger bare-except
        if arg:
            return
        raise Exception()
```
```python
def foo(arg):
    try:
        pass
    except: # does trigger bare-except
        if not arg:
            raise Exception()
        return
```

title: kwargs used by decorator being flagged as unused.
body:
After upgrading from pylint 2.4.4 to 2.5.2, we started getting unused-argument errors with kwargs that are sent to decorators for Marshmallow.  What is weird is that it is not consistent.  In my codebase, I encountered these and added this to the disabled list where I was seeing the lint error to ignore it.  However there are many other places in the codebase that we use Marshmallow decorators that did not error for me, and other developers are seeing errors on some (but not all) of those.

### Steps to reproduce
1. Create and lint a Marshmallow Schema that utilizes decorators like validates_schema
```
from marshmallow import fields, Schema, validate, validates_schema, ValidationError

class TestSchema(Schema):
    test_url = fields.Str(required=True, validate=validate.Length(max=1000))

    @validates_schema
    def validate_test_url(self, data, **kwargs):
        if data['test_url'] and not data['test_url'].startswith('https://test.example.com'):
            raise ValidationError('URL must start with {}'.format('https://test.example.com'))
```


### Current behavior
`[W0613(unused-argument), TestSchema.validate_test_url] Unused argument 'kwargs'`


### Expected behavior
Passes without error

### pylint --version output
pylint 1.9.5, 
astroid 1.6.6
Python 2.7.17 (default, Apr 15 2020, 17:20:14) 
[GCC 7.5.0]

title: False-positive unneeded-not when comparing dict views
body:
dict-view comparison triggers unneeded-not, despite the comparison being set-like, and therefore the "not {}.items() <= {}.items()" to "{}.items() > {}.items()" being a functionality change.

Simple example that reproduces the issue
```python
def func():
    if not {}.items() <= {}.items():
        pass
```

title: Feature request: Check use of deprecated exception classes like IOError
body:
#Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe

Python reworked exception classes a while ago (starting version 3.3) and several error classes are now just aliases for OSError, see:
https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-3151/#step-1-coalesce-exception-types

### Describe the solution you'd like

pylint could warn about the use of deprecated error classes like IOError and propose that programmers use OSError instead.

title: Could we define and add an IDE mode for Python checkers ?
body:
There are actually issues with Python checkers and the flycheck mode on Emacs due to the way checkers work.  I think this is not the job of the IDE to do magic to get things working.

Issues are mainly CWD related :
- implement a top-down walk (on `__init__.py`) to define a CWD to avoid import issues
- how to get the setup.cfg ?
- input filename check issue due to flycheck_ prefix

Emacs is only aware of the input file path.

I would suggest to " write a PEP " to define how should work a checker and implement such a mode:

- do not use the current working directory to make a decision, just ignore it
- takes an input file path and converts it to an absolute path
  then derive or find the setup files (top-down walk, $HOME, etc.)

and

- a standard JSON output
- list the setup file paths in the JSON to be able to check what is happening
- eventually define a new setup file to customize the PYTHONPATH
  else this must be done before to run Emacs

title: False positive `no-self-use` when a method defines a sub-class that itself does not use `self` in a method
body:
### Steps to reproduce
1. copy the code from below into a Python file and run pylint on it :)

### Current behavior

The following file generates a report with a false positive for `no-self-use`:

false_positive.py
```python
"""Test file."""
# pylint: disable=missing-class-docstring,missing-function-docstring,too-few-public-methods
import unittest
from class_generator import ExternalClassGenerator

class MyTest(unittest.TestCase):

    def test_one(self, value):

        with self.assertRaises(TypeError):
            class _MySubClass(ExternalClassGenerator(value)):

                def my_sub_method(self):
                    return value
```

Report:
```log
************* Module false_positive
false_positive.py:13:16: R0201: Method could be a function (no-self-use)
false_positive.py:8:4: R0201: Method could be a function (no-self-use)
```

The `self` for the method `test_one` on line 8 is used in line 10
The `self` for the method `my_sub_method` (inside the sub-class) on line 13 is indeed a correct `no-self-use`

**Both** errors disappear if I do one of the following:
* Change the inner class to just:
  ```python
            class _MySubClass(ExternalClassGenerator(value)):
                pass
  ```
* Use `self` in the `my_sub_method` method

### Expected behavior

Only `my_sub_method` should cause a `no-self-use`

### pylint --version output
```
pylint 2.5.2
astroid 2.4.1
Python 3.8.2 (default, Apr 27 2020, 15:53:34) 
[GCC 9.3.0]
```

title: False negative ``dangerous-default-value`` when ``typing.NamedTuple`` uses mutable defaults
body:
## Problem

Giving mutable default arguments to `typing.NamedTuple` can cause unexpected behaviour.
```python
from typing import List, NamedTuple

class LibStats(NamedTuple):
    avgs: List[float] = []
    stds: List[float] = []

lib_a = LibStats()
lib_b = LibStats()

lib_a.avgs.append([1.])
# this assert fails, because list is shared between the instances
assert lib_a.avgs != lib_b.avgs
```

The same occurs when [providing a dictionary as a default argument](https://stackoverflow.com/q/59205528/1079075).

## Expected Behaviour

`pylint` should warn about this code, similar to how it currently warn about mutable objects being used as default arguments in function definitions.

### Additional context
pylint version: 2.5.3

title: Make floating point numbers look like numbers, not abbreviations
body:
Python is liberal about what a float looks like, humans have more problems. A leading or trailing . is easy to miss, and with the wrong font or size, can look like a ,, impeding readability (and the text immediately before "impeding" is an example of that).

### Describe the solution you'd like
Require floating point numbers to have leading and trailing digits (that is, there must always be a digit on both sides of the '.')

BAD:  1., 2., .3, 4., 5.1

GOOD: 1.0, 2.0, 0.3, 4.0, 5.1


### Additional context
The Python syntax is unambiguous, but humans are not compilers :-)

title: do not report too-few-public-methods for `attr.s`, `attr.dataclass`, `typing.NamedTuple` and similar.
body:
do not report too-few-public-methods for `attr.s`, `attr.dataclass`, `typing.NamedTuple` and similar. 

example:

```python
import attr

@attr.dataclass(frozen=True)  # pylint-ignore: too-few-public-methods
class Foo:
    ham: str
    spam: str
```

> @uriva As mentioned by @matkoniecz in most cases the check makes sense. If your class has a single method on it, it's probably a function in disguise. You can also configure the number of public methods with [min-public-methods](http://pylint.pycqa.org/en/latest/technical_reference/features.html#design-checker-options). But there are a couple of classes for which we should not report this, as it makes perfect sense to not have too many methods or no methods at all. This includes `attrs`, `dataclasses`, `typing.NamedTuple` and similar. 

_Originally posted by @PCManticore in https://github.com/PyCQA/pylint/issues/2710#issuecomment-458157179_

title: Add different configuration for different files
body:
### Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe

This request is similar request to https://github.com/PyCQA/pylint/issues/618 (Add different configuration for different sub directories) but at the file level.

Currently, `pylint` apply the same config instinctively to all files. However some rules should be applied only to specific files.

For instance it is really frequent in test files to access private members (`assert module._some_fn()`). Currently all our tests files trigger hundreds of `protected-access` warnings.

### Describe the solution you'd like

We would like a way to overwrite specific rules for all `*_test.py` files.

For instace being able to register some `pattern="**/*_test.py"` with custom rules associated. All files which would match the pattern would use the special pylint rules.

Note: if the pattern accept directory names (`some_dir/**`), it would also solve https://github.com/PyCQA/pylint/issues/618 while being more flexible (like: `**/some_dir/**`).

title: SQLAlchemy @hybrid_property.expression has "no-self-argument"
body:
<!--
  Hi there! Thank you for discovering and submitting an issue.

  Before you submit this, make sure that the issue doesn't already exist
  or if it is not closed.

  Is your issue fixed on the preview release?: pip install pylint astroid --pre -U

-->

I'm not sure if this would be within the scope of `pylint` features, but a [similar question](https://github.com/PyCQA/pylint/issues/1061) was looked at previously, so I may as well mention the problem.

The `hybrid_property` of SQLAlchemy has an `expression` method, which acts like a classmethod in that it takes `cls` as an argument, not `self`. Pylint doesn't like this, and comes up with the `no-self-argument` warning. The documentation for it is [here](https://docs.sqlalchemy.org/en/13/orm/extensions/hybrid.html#defining-expression-behavior-distinct-from-attribute-behavior).

Here's an example:

```python
from sqlalchemy import Column, Integer, func
from sqlalchemy.ext.declarative import declarative_base
from sqlalchemy.ext.hybrid import hybrid_property

Base = declarative_base()


class Test(Base):
    __tablename__ = 'test'

    id = Column(Integer, primary_key=True)
    name = Column(String(64), nullable=False)

    @hybrid_property
    def name_lower(self):
        return self.name.lower()

    @name_lower.expression
    def name_lower(cls, value):     # this line has the issue
        return func.lower(cls.name)
```

From a look at the source code, there doesn't seem to be an obvious way to detecting that though, so it may need to be more of a hardcoded feature if implemented.

title: Have a way to require type annotations
body:
### Is your feature request related to a problem? 
Having type annotations makes the code more understandable.
Having a way to require methods have type annotations would be a great start.

### Describe the solution you'd like
An option which enforces presence of type annotations.
Where should they be present?
   - Methods: All/public only
   - Variables: all/public/constant
For methods.. What part of the signature should the enforcement be for?
  - arguments? return type? (of course self should be exempt)
Any standard set of exemptions?
  - methods with certain decorators may be exempt (eg. @override implementation of an method from parent class?) 


### Additional context
This should be opt-in, i.e. disabled by default (for backward compatibility), so individuals can enable it on a per-file basis (or a flag to pylint binary)

### Steps to reproduce

title: Recognize variable references in DataFrame.query as such
body:
### Problem description

Variable names in Pandas queries are not recognized as variable references by Pylint and produce `unused-variable` (unless used outside the Pandas query obviously).

E.g., in: `query = "somecolumn == @something"`

### Possible solution

Make Pylint recognize @-prefixed parts in strings and consider those as variable uses. This approach might lead to false positives. A more diligent approach might be to only do so when pandas is imported and/or the string in question is used in a `DataFrame.query()` call.

### Notes

* I'm surprised I didn't find anything about this in the issues, but I did have a look.
* Similar issue with `eval`?

    ```py
    from rest_framework import generics

    class SomeView(generics.RetrieveUpdateAPIView):
        def update(self, request, *args, **kwargs):  # args and kwargs are flagged as unused-arguments
            return 1
    ```

### Current behavior
- if you don't use `args` and `kwargs` arguments it will raise `unused-arguments` error
- if you change `update(self, request, *args, **kwargs)` to `update(self, request, _1, _2)` it will not raise any error

### Expected behavior
- if you don't use `args` and `kwargs` arguments it should not raise any error because the `rest_framework.mixins.UpdateModelMixin.update` method's signature is the same
- if you change `update(self, request, *args, **kwargs)` to `update(self, request, _1, _2)` it should raise `arguments-differ` error

### Current solutions
- add `rest_framework.mixins.UpdateModelMixin` to `SomeView`'s parent classes
- change `update(self, request, *args, **kwargs)` to `update(self, request, *_1, **_2)` 

### Additional
the weird thing is that pylint works exactly like I've explained if we are not overriding the `retrieve` method

```py
from rest_framework import generics

class SomeView(generics.RetrieveUpdateAPIView):
    def update(self, request, _1, _2):
        # ^^^ no error, even though it overrides the
        # rest_framework.mixins.UpdateModelMixin.update method
        # which has (self, request, *args, **kwargs) as arguments
        return 1

    def retrieve(self, request, _1, _2):
        # ^^^ arguments-differ, because it overrides the
        # rest_famework.mixins.RetrieveModelMixin.retrieve method
        # which has (self, request, *args, **kwargs) as arguments
        return 1
```

### pylint --version output
```bash
pylint 2.6.0
astroid 2.4.2
Python 3.7.8 (default, Sep 22 2020, 12:13:50)
[Clang 12.0.0 (clang-1200.0.32.2)]
```

body:

### Steps to reproduce

```python
# test.py
import functools
from typing import Callable, NamedTuple

class Module(NamedTuple):
    init: Callable
    apply: Callable

def module(module_maker):
    @functools.wraps(module_maker)
    def fabricate_module(*args, **kwargs):
        init, apply = module_maker(*args, **kwargs)
        return Module(init, apply)
    return fabricate_module

@module
def my_module():
    def my_module_init(x_in):
        return x_in
    def my_module_apply(x_in):
        return x_in
    return my_module_init, my_module_apply

mod = my_module()
INPUT_X = 0.
mod.init(INPUT_X)
mod.apply(0.)
```

title: spelling: ignore lines via regex (copyright headers)
body:
### Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe

My Python files follow the [REUSE spec](https://reuse.software/spec/) for copyright information. The standardized header in Python looks like this:
```python3
# SPDX-FileCopyrightText: 2020-2021 Stephan Lachnit <stephanlachnit@protonmail.com>
#
# SPDX-License-Identifier: EUPL-1.2
```

The spell check fails here:
```
yaiden.py:3:0: C0401: Wrong spelling of a word 'Lachnit' in a comment:
# SPDX-FileCopyrightText: 2020-2021 Stephan Lachnit <stephanlachnit@protonmail.com>
                                            ^^^^^^^
Did you mean: ''Malachite''? (wrong-spelling-in-comment)
yaiden.py:3:0: C0401: Wrong spelling of a word 'stephanlachnit' in a comment:
# SPDX-FileCopyrightText: 2020-2021 Stephan Lachnit <stephanlachnit@protonmail.com>
                                                     ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
Did you mean: ''stephanotis''? (wrong-spelling-in-comment)
yaiden.py:3:0: C0401: Wrong spelling of a word 'protonmail' in a comment:
# SPDX-FileCopyrightText: 2020-2021 Stephan Lachnit <stephanlachnit@protonmail.com>
                                                                    ^^^^^^^^^^
Did you mean: ''proton mail' or 'proton-mail' or 'protonema''? (wrong-spelling-in-comment)
```

### Describe the solution you'd like

Two common things here are problematic:
- Names
- Email addresses

There are a couple of ways to tackle this. One could obviously just add all the names and parts of the mail to the ignore list, but if you have a lot of contributors that could get long and annoying. One could also disable spellchecking for the line by adding a comment, but that kinda defeats the purpose of the REUSE spec (automatic scanning of copyright information).

For email addresses, one could ignore text that matches a regex expression like `<\S+@\S+\.\S+>` ([or something more sophisticated](http://emailregex.com/)).

For names, I don't think there is an easy solution. Instead, I think a good solution (that also works for email addresses) is to add an option to ignore a line if it matches a regex.
For example, to ignore REUSE copyright header lines, I could use: `#.*SPDX-FileCopyrightText:.*`

title: False positive used-before-assignment for return type
body:
Hello guys, here seems to be an issue with return type annotations conflicting with member names:

### Current behavior

This is a little class to reproduce the problem, the relevant (mis-)behaviour is explained in the comments:

```python
class TestPylint_return_1:

    # Hovering on 'bool' anywhere shows "bool: bool" i.e. the class member!

    def foo(self) -> bool:  # raises 'bool: bool' used-before-assignment!
        return self.bool()

    def bool(self) -> bool:
        return True
```

And this simple work-around does get rid of the warning but shows that the underlying issue remains:

```python
class TestPylintBool_return_2:

    # Hovering on 'bool' anywhere shows "bool: bool"!

    def bool(self) -> bool:
        return True

    def foo(self) -> bool:
        return self.bool()

```

Though of course at that point I have realised that the "underlying issue" may not just be with pylint...

### Expected behavior

Pylint should (at least?) recognise that that 'bool' annotating the return type is the 'bool' type and not the 'bool' member of the class, hence not give that warning.

### pylint --version output

pylint 2.6.0
astroid 2.4.2
Python 3.8.5 (default, Sep  3 2020, 21:29:08) [MSC v.1916 64 bit (AMD64)]

Thank you,

Julio

```python
"Something"
```

title: Checker to replace 'any/all(not condition)' by 'not any/all(condition)' when 'not condition' is slower than 'condition'
body:
The following function are equivalent but one is faster because it cut the execution tree at the first not instead of checking all the values.

```python
import time
from timeit import timeit


def not_all(n, step=1):
    if not all(range(-n, n, step)):
        ...


def any_not(n, step=1):
    if any(x for x in range(-n, n, step)):
        ...


assert not_all(3, 1) == any_not(3, 1)
assert not_all(3, 2) == any_not(3, 2)
assert not_all(5, 1) == any_not(5, 1)

start = time.time()
for i in range(20):
    any_not(1000000)
end = time.time()
print(end - start)
start = time.time()
for i in range(20):
    not_all(1000000)
end = time.time()
print(end - start)

print(
    timeit(
        "import numpy as np; arr = np.ones(int(1e8)); arr[3]=0; np.any(arr<=0)",
        number=10,
    )
)
print(
    timeit(
        "import numpy as np; arr = np.ones(int(1e8)); arr[3]=0; np.all(arr>0)",
        number=10,
    )
)

```

Results:
```
1.52587890625e-05
0.28366875648498535
2.1408786789979786
1.6885079700005008
```

Notice that changing from one generator to two generators with a casting to bool is slower but just changing the condition is faster.

### Describe the solution you'd like

New checker for detecting `not all(values)` and `any(not v for v in values)` to advise to convert it to the most efficient version of the check.

title: Cannot use --never-returning-functions for some functions
body:
# Version
pylint==2.7.2

# Dependency
pytest

# Steps to reproduce
Create a python file `debug.py`:
```py
import pytest

def apparently_inconsistent_returning_function(return_something: bool) -> None:
    """A function that looks like not all paths return something."""
    if return_something:
        return None
    pytest.exit("Raises an exception.")
```

```
# pylint debug.py
```
=> `R1710: Either all return statements in a function should return an expression, or none of them should. (inconsistent-return-statements)`

This message is raised as pylint does not understand that `pytest.exit` raises an exception. Using `--never-returning-functions="pytest.exit"` should fix this behavior, however it doesn't:

```
# pylint debug.py --never-returning-functions="pytest.exit"
```
=> `R1710: Either all return statements in a function should return an expression, or none of them should. (inconsistent-return-statements)`

# Expected behavior

`pylint debug.py --never-returning-functions="pytest.exit"` should not return an error for the above code.

# Actual behavior

`pylint debug.py --never-returning-functions="pytest.exit"` still gives the error "inconsistent-return-statements"

# Initial analysis

https://github.com/PyCQA/pylint/blob/master/pylint/checkers/refactoring/refactoring_checker.py#L1362

`node.func` is Attribute.exit(attrname='exit', expr=<Name.pytest l.7 at 0x7fab9184d898>)
`node.func.inferred()[0]` is "Uninferable", as a result there is no value we can set for `--never-returning-functions`  that would get rid of the error "inconsistent-return-statements".

# Workaround

```
import pytest

def pytest_exit(*args, **kwargs):
    """Call pytest.exit."""
    pytest.exit(*args, **kwargs)

def apparently_inconsistent_returning_function(return_something: bool) -> None:
    """A function that looks like not all paths return something."""
    if return_something:
        return None
    pytest_exit("Raises an exception.")
```

By wrapping pytest.exit in another function `pytest_exit`, `node.func.inferred()[0]` is now:
```
FunctionDef.pytest_exit(name='pytest_exit',
                        doc='Call pytest.exit.',
                        decorators=None,
                        args=<Arguments l.3 at 0x7f895a6b90f0>,
                        returns=None,
                        body=[<Expr l.5 at 0x7f895a6b9780>])
```

=> `node.func.inferred()[0].qname()` is "debug.pytest_exit" which we can use to get rid of the error "inconsistent-return-statements":

```
pylint debug.py --never-returning-functions="debug.pytest_exit"
```

title: Imports from module using PEP 562's __getattr__ should not raise E0611
body:
### Steps to reproduce

```python
"""
main.py
"""

from dynamic import foo, bar

print(foo)
print(bar)
```

```python
"""
dynamic.py
"""

def __getattr__(name):
    if name.startswith('x'):
        raise AttributeError
    return 42
```

### Current behavior

```
$ pylint dynamic.py main.py
************* Module main
main.py:5:0: E0611: No name 'foo' in module 'dynamic' (no-name-in-module)
main.py:5:0: E0611: No name 'bar' in module 'dynamic' (no-name-in-module)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Your code has been rated at -4.29/10 (previous run: -4.29/10, +0.00)
```

### Expected behavior

Unfortunately the current behavior ignores [PEP 562](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0562/), which officially provides a way to dynamically define module contents. `pylint` should inhibit E0611 for such modules.

(A related issue was previously logged as https://github.com/PyCQA/pylint/issues/1704.)

title: Have spellchecker ignore black/flake8/bandit directives
body:
### Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe

The spellchecker raises errors for things like `# fmt: on` (a `black` directive), `# noqa` (flake8 directive), and `# nosec` (bandit directive).

### Describe the solution you'd like

These should be ignored.  A potential implementation would be adding a filter like already exists for Sphinx directives.

Adding them to the "words to ignore" dictionary is suboptimal.  A filter could be much more restrictive and require that it appear  right next to the `#` comment symbol and not allow them to pass as false negatives of actual misspelled words in other portions of the comment.

Would you be open to a PR to address this?

title: Github actions for pylint available in github actions marketplace
body: Github actions can be defined from the repository. GitHub Action allows repo maintainers to quickly add automated pylint testing on all pull requests submitted to their repos. This will focus development efforts on a single, community-supported GitHub Action for pylint instead of a multitude of homegrown solutions with travis or custom github actions for each repository.

title: too-few-public-methods: warn only for the parent class
body:
### Steps to reproduce

Given a file `test.py`:
```python
#! /usr/bin/python3

# pylint: disable=missing-module-docstring

class Hello: # pylint: disable=too-few-public-methods
    """
    Some local class, but reproduced with external wtforms.FlaskForm, too.
    """

class World(Hello):
    """
    reported here:
    too-few-public-methods: Too few public methods (0/2)
    """
    def __init__(self):
        self.test = 0

```

### Current behavior

Result of `pylint /tmp/test.py`:
```
************* Module test
/tmp/test.py:10:0: R0903: Too few public methods (0/1) (too-few-public-methods)

------------------------------------------------------------------
Your code has been rated at 7.50/10 (previous run: 7.69/10, -0.19)
```

### Expected behavior

No error.  When we inherit from some library class, no matter how many public
methods it provides, it should be acceptable to **not** add any more public
methods.  One can e.g. only override some private method, some static class
attribute, rewrite `__init__`, ...

It isn't convenient to disable this warning for each class, and it is a pitty to
disable this for the whole project.

### pylint --version output

Result of `pylint --version` output:


```
pylint 2.6.0
astroid 2.4.2
Python 3.9.4 (default, Apr  6 2021, 00:00:00)
[GCC 11.0.1 20210324 (Red Hat 11.0.1-0)]
```

title: New checker for casting sets to lists
body:
<!--
  Hi there! Thank you for wanting to make pylint better.

  Before you submit this, make sure that this feature wasn't
  already requested or if it is not already implemented in the master branch.
-->

### Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe

Just had an issue in my code that took a while to debug, in particular because the output of the code was non-deterministic and there were no obvious places where randomness was being used. It turns out the issue stemmed from a place where a set was being cast as a list, which means the order of the list is not deterministic or consistent. Casting from an ordered type (set) into a type with order (list) should raise a warning. Order is an important characteristic of lists, but casting a set into a list means the order of the list is arbitrary.

### Describe the solution you'd like

A new warning (e.g. `casting-set-to-list`) should be raised when running pylint. A more general warning might be better, since casting to a tuple has the same effect (`casting-unordered-to-ordered` might be a better name). 

### Additional context

Casting a set to a list is a non-deterministic process, although users often assume it is:

```python
>>> s, t = set({1, 9}), set({9, 1})
>>> s == t
True
>>> s, t = list(s), list(t)
>>> s == t
False
```

title: Improper `too-many-function-args` for decorated function
body:
<!--
  Hi there! Thank you for discovering and submitting an issue.

  Before you submit this, make sure that the issue doesn't already exist
  or if it is not closed.

  Is your issue fixed on the preview release?:
    pip install pylint astroid --pre -U
-->

### Steps to reproduce

The following are the contents of `variadic.py`.

```python
# pylint:disable=missing-docstring
from __future__ import annotations
import functools
from typing import Callable, Union


def variadic(func: Callable[[int], int]):
    @functools.wraps(func)
    def wrapped(*integers: int) -> Union[int, tuple[int, ...]]:
        result = tuple(func(i) for i in integers)
        return result[0] if len(result) == 1 else result

    return wrapped


@variadic
def double(integer: int) -> int:
    return integer * 2


def main():
    doubled = double(1, 2, 3)  # false positive too-many-function-args
    print(doubled)


if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()

```

### Current behavior

Result of `pylint variadic.py`:
```
************* Module variadic
variadic.py:22:14: E1121: Too many positional arguments for function call (too-many-function-args)

------------------------------------------------------------------
Your code has been rated at 6.67/10 (previous run: 6.67/10, +0.00)
```

### Expected behavior

Line 22 shouldn't raise `too-many-function-args`, as `double` has been decorated to handle a variable number of arguments.

This is especially troublesome as I have a similar setup that uses such a function across several modules.

### pylint --version output

Result of `pylint --version` output:

```
pylint 2.7.4
astroid 2.5.3
Python 3.8.2 (default, Jun  8 2020, 10:58:26) 
[Clang 11.0.3 (clang-1103.0.32.62)]
```

title: Pylint takes the isort configuration into account only if it's directly available in its launch directory
body:
### Steps to reproduce

1. Create two python packages `apple` and `banana` that share a common namespace `fruit`. `banana` is a dependency of `apple`.

2. Create a module in `apple` with the following code:
```python
# apple-package-root/fruit/apple/statistics.py

import math # std lib

import fruit.banana.data # third party
import numpy # third party

import fruit.apple.data # first party
```

3. Sort the mentioned module with `isort` (no change)

4. Run `pylint`

### Current behavior

```
fruit/apple/statistics.py:7:0: C0412: Imports from package fruit are not grouped (ungrouped-imports)
```

### Expected behavior

No warning / error

### Explanation

In contrast to `isort` (the command-line tool), `pylint` incorrectly classifies `import fruit.banana.data` as a first party import.

This bug results from `pylint` calling `isort_driver.place_module("fruit")` (returning "first party") instead of `isort_driver.place_module("fruit.banana.data")` (would return "third party"):
https://github.com/PyCQA/pylint/blob/v2.8.2/pylint/checkers/imports.py#L723

title: Linting fails if module contains module of the same name
body:
### Steps to reproduce

Given multiple files:
```
.
`-- a/
    |-- a.py
    `-- b.py
```
Which are all empty, running `pylint a` fails:

```
$ pylint a
************* Module a
a/__init__.py:1:0: F0010: error while code parsing: Unable to load file a/__init__.py:
[Errno 2] No such file or directory: 'a/__init__.py' (parse-error)
$
```

However, if I rename `a.py`, `pylint a` succeeds:

```
$ mv a/a.py a/c.py
$ pylint a
$
```
Alternatively, I can also `touch a/__init__.py`, but that shouldn't be necessary anymore.

### Current behavior

Running `pylint a` if `a/a.py` is present fails while searching for an `__init__.py` file.

### Expected behavior

Running `pylint a` if `a/a.py` is present should succeed.

### pylint --version output

Result of `pylint --version` output:

```
pylint 3.0.0a3
astroid 2.5.6
Python 3.8.5 (default, Jan 27 2021, 15:41:15) 
[GCC 9.3.0]
```

### Additional info

This also has some side-effects in module resolution. For example, if I create another file `r.py`:

```
.
|-- a
|   |-- a.py
|   `-- b.py
`-- r.py
```

With the content:

```
from a import b
```

Running `pylint -E r` will run fine, but `pylint -E r a` will fail. Not just for module a, but for module r as well.

```
************* Module r
r.py:1:0: E0611: No name 'b' in module 'a' (no-name-in-module)
************* Module a
a/__init__.py:1:0: F0010: error while code parsing: Unable to load file a/__init__.py:
[Errno 2] No such file or directory: 'a/__init__.py' (parse-error)
```

Again, if I rename `a.py` to `c.py`, `pylint -E r a` will work perfectly.

title: Detecting OOP principles (SOLID) violations
body:
## Idea

It would be nice to have some checkers which would catch object oriented programming principle violations.[ SOLID](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/SOLID) principles usually are the go-to for writing clean, maintainable code so it would be very beneficial to catch at least some of their violations.

Even though evaluation whether a module is violating those principles can be subjective and it's hard to decide on that only by doing static code analysis, I have been writing my bachelor thesis on _Automated detection of OOP principles violation in Python_ and have gathered some proposal of how that could be checked.

## Proposal

I have already started writing code for Single Responsibility, Liskov Substitution, Interface Segregation and Dependency Inversion principle checkers and have described the types of violations they catch in this repo: https://github.com/justenau/OOP_Violations_Checker

If anyone's interested in this idea, I could provide more in depth explanation of how the metrics used in those checkers relate to actual principles validation since I investigated them during the bachelor thesis preparation.

### SRPChecker
SRP principle is violated when a class/method has more than one responsibility. Although deciding whether a module does only one job is subjective, there are some metrics which can catch some classes potentially violating the principle. Metrics proposed are Lack of Cohesion in methods (LCOM), cyclomatic complexity (CCM), amount of public methods in class and amount of class attributes. 

I have used this checker to scan some bigger projects and it did indicate classes which were actually violating SRP. So a checker which would combine all of these metrics could actually show great results.

### LSPChecker

Part of LSP principle definition says that no child class should degenerate its parent class method - this can be caught by scanning all parent class implemented methods and then checking whether the child class doesn't override them using `pass` statement or raising `NotImplementedException`.

### ISPChecker

ISP principle aims to prevent classes depending on interface methods which they don't need and that could be achieved by splitting interfaces which are not fully implemented/used by their client classes. Even though Python hasn't got built-in interface types, it's a common practice to create interfaces by having all their methods return `pass` or `NotImplementedException` (or by using `ABC` module). So the violation could be detected if a client class uses this interface-class as its base class but does not implement all of its methods.

### DIPChecker

Main idea of DIP is to use abstraction wherever possible and not have references to concrete classes. That is not really relevant for a dynamic language as Python since the type of a variable is not known until runtime and can be changed dynamically. Despite that, one more rule of DIP is that _No method should override an implemented method_ and this is quite easy to detect with Pylint and that's what I did with the DIPChecker.

## Additional notes
The proposed metrics and ways to detect the violation obviously can be altered or extended and I'd really like to hear your concerns, thoughts and suggestions about the whole idea. Even if you don't see value in having all of those checker, maybe some of them or part of their implementation could be extracted as some custom checkers.

title: Provide a way to define name to be ignored via `good-method-names` and `good-property-names`
body:
### Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe

I have encountered a use case where `invalid-name` is raised, where I believe it shouldn't.
Example class: 

```python
class User():
    @property
    def id(self): 
        return # something
 ```

In that case, because `id` is 2 characters, the name is not matched against the regex rule of 3+ snake_case characters.

### Describe the solution you'd like

Similar to `good-names` that allows override of the rules employed to match valid variable names, configurations `good-method-names` and `good-property-names` should be available. 
This would allow to list very specific cases where names should be considered valid even if they disagree with the other rules. 

The reason I feel methods and properties should have their own configurations, and not mixed all together with `good-names`, is for cases similar to my `id` example. A builtin method `id()` exists. Therefore, I do not want to allow `id` variables, but the same name as property in a class seem perfectly valid and a common practice for database models in my opinion. 

I am aware that I could override the regex for 2 characters, but I would much rather have the option to allow very specific method/property names. Setting a 2 characters regex would globally allows names like `_i`, `x_`, `aa`, etc. which is not something I want to allow. 

### Additional context

relates to #2018 and #4183 that could benefit from the same configurations

title: False positive 'requests.packages' has no 'urllib3' member (no-member)
body:
### Steps to reproduce

Given a file `a.py`:
```python
# pylint: disable=missing-docstring
```

title: Please consider warning on keyword arguments before positional unpacking arguments
body:
```python
def f(x, y):
    pass

args = [1]
f(y=2, *args)
```
This code currently passes PyLint.  Perhaps PyLint should suggest inverting the parameters:
```python
f(*args, y=2)
```
For background, please see

- [Order of positional and keyword arguments](https://mail.python.org/archives/list/python-dev@python.org/thread/I6AUYV6DVEMP7XVYVDWC62N6PK6FHX3H/)
- [Incorrect evaluation order of function arguments with *args](https://bugs.python.org/issue23316)

As Terry Reedy points out, "I think it would have been better to have stuck with 'positional before keyword'.  Can we consider deprecating something rare and easy to get wrong?"  Unfortunately, they couldn't, but I wonder if PyLint can warn the user that such code is confusing and error-prone?

title: Dangerous default argument should include "Stateful" default arguments as well
body:
### Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe

A clear and concise description of what the problem is.

When a function is defined, a function will maintain the state of the default argument. This can be dangerous if the default argument is something like `datetime.date.today()`. In this [article](https://docs.python-guide.org/writing/gotchas/), it states that:

> Python’s default arguments are evaluated once when the function is defined, not each time the function is called (like it is in say, Ruby).

Thus, if a production environment has a pod that evaluates a function once and the pod doesn't rotate for a couple days, it will continue to define that default argument incorrectly. 


### Describe the solution you'd like

It would be nice if `pylint` warns us about these dangerous default "stateful" arguments, similar to the warning about dangerous "mutable" default arguments, e.g. http://pylint-messages.wikidot.com/messages:w0102


A clear and concise description of what you want to happen.

### Additional context

Add any other context about the feature request here.

title: no-member on argparse result on Python 3.9 (only)
body:
### Steps to reproduce

Given a file `pylint_no_member.py`:
```python
#!/usr/bin/env python

"""
Demonstrates that Pylint raises no-member when accessing existing members
of an argparse result.

Required ingredients:
- This happens only on Python 3.9, but not on earlier Python versions.
- This happens only when argparse.ArgumentParser is subclassed.
- This happens only for the '--logdir' option, but not for the 'name'
  positional argument. However, in our real case with more options, it also
  happened for positional arguments.
"""

import argparse


class SilentArgumentParser(argparse.ArgumentParser):
    """
    argparse.ArgumentParser subclass that silences any errors and exit and
    just raises them as SystemExit.
    """

    def error(self, message=None):
        """Called for usage errors detected by the parser"""
        raise SystemExit(2)

    def exit(self, status=0, message=None):
        """Not sure when this is called"""
        raise SystemExit(status)


def parse_args(argv):
    """
    Parse the command line arguments.
    """
    parser = SilentArgumentParser()

    parser.add_argument('--logdir', type=str, default=None)
    parser.add_argument('name', type=str, default=None)

    parsed_args = parser.parse_args(argv)
    return parsed_args


args = parse_args(['foo'])
print(repr(args))
print(args.name)
print(args.logdir)  # Pylint issues no-member on Python 3.9
```

### Current behavior

On Python 3.9, Pylint raises no-member when accessing the `logdir` attribute from the argparse option `--logdir`:
```
$ pylint --version
pylint 2.9.3
astroid 2.6.2
Python 3.9.1 (default, Feb  1 2021, 20:41:56) 
[Clang 12.0.0 (clang-1200.0.32.29)]

$ pylint -s n -r n pylint_no_member.py 
************* Module pylint_no_member
pylint_no_member.py:49:6: E1101: Instance of 'Namespace' has no 'logdir' member (no-member)

$ ./pylint_no_member.py 
Namespace(logdir=None, name='foo')
foo
None
```

The same command on Python 3.8 (with the same Pylint version) does not raise the issue:

```
$ pylint --version
pylint 2.9.3
astroid 2.6.2
Python 3.8.7 (default, Feb  3 2021, 06:31:03) 
[Clang 12.0.0 (clang-1200.0.32.29)]

$ pylint -s n -r n pylint_no_member.py 
# no output
```

### Expected behavior

On Python 3.9, no-member should also not be raised.

title: Regression on rest_framework : Value 'serializer.data' is unsubscriptable
body:
`rest_framework.serializers.Serializer.data` is a property that returns a `ReturnDict` object. That class is a subclass of the well known `collections.OrderedDict` and is obviously subscriptable. What I notice is that the error only appears when the field we add to the serializer gets the very same name 'data'.

What might get pylint confused is that there is some metaclass magic going on when declaring a Serializer subclass. If we declare a 'data' field/attribute, it won't in the end override the original 'Serializer.data' attribute but end up within that very attribute.

Pylint 2.8.3 didn't raise any errors for this case.

### Steps to reproduce

Given a file `serializer_data_unsubscriptable.py`:
```python
import django
from django.conf import settings
from rest_framework import serializers


class DataSerializerWithError(serializers.Serializer):
    data = serializers.JSONField()
    def update(self, instance, validated_data):
        pass

    def create(self, validated_data):
        pass


class DataSerializerWithoutError(serializers.Serializer):
    payload = serializers.JSONField()
    def update(self, instance, validated_data):
        pass

    def create(self, validated_data):
        pass


def print_data():
    settings.configure()
    django.setup()

    serializer = DataSerializerWithError(data={'data': 42})
    if not serializer.is_valid():
        print("Failure!")
        return
    print(serializer.data['data'])

    serializer = DataSerializerWithoutError(data={'payload': 42})
    if not serializer.is_valid():
        print("Failure!")
        return
    print(serializer.data['payload'])
```

### Current behavior

Result of `pylint serializer_data_unsubscriptable.py`:
```
$ pylint serializer_data_unsubscriptable.py
************* Module serializer_data_unsubscriptable
serializer_data_unsubscriptable.py:32: [E1136(unsubscriptable-object), print_data] Value 'serializer.data' is unsubscriptable
```

### Expected behavior

No errors.

### pylint --version output

Result of `pylint --version` output:

```
pylint 2.9.3
astroid 2.6.2
Python 3.8.9 (default, Apr 19 2021, 08:40:21) 
[GCC 10.2.0]
```

title: Using classes "defined as generic in stubs but not at runtime" as base class triggers inherit-non-class
body:
`pylint>=2.9.0` triggers a false positive of `inherit-non-class` when using a class as base that was defined as a generic in a stub (or mypy plugin) but not at runtime when using the [recommended workaround from mypy](https://mypy.readthedocs.io/en/latest/runtime_troubles.html#using-classes-that-are-generic-in-stubs-but-not-at-runtime).

### Steps to reproduce

Consider this file:

```py
# pylint: disable=missing-module-docstring,missing-class-docstring
import typing

from django.db import models

if typing.TYPE_CHECKING:
    QuerySetBase = models.QuerySet[models.Model]
else:
    QuerySetBase = models.QuerySet


# class def will trigger: E0239: Inheriting 'QuerySetBase', which is not a class. (inherit-non-class)
class TestQuerySet(QuerySetBase):  
    pass
```

### Current behavior

`pylint>=2.9.0` triggers `inherit-non-class`, version `2.8.3` does not yet trigger that error.

It should be noted that a workaround is to invert the if/else statement like this:

```py
if not typing.TYPE_CHECKING:
    QuerySetBase = models.QuerySet
else:
    QuerySetBase = models.QuerySet[models.Model]
```

... which will not trigger the error. Still of course, something is clearly happening that is not intended.

### Expected behavior

... not trigger that error, obviously ;-)

title: Discussion around a proper implementation of control-flow in pylint
body:
### Current problem

The main limitation regarding control flow is the fact that the variable checkers rely on ``NamesConsumer``'s to_consume/consumed states, and these are updated "all-or-nothing" per variable, with additional special-case checks here and there. To give a simpler example, pylint doesn't report any error on:

```python
def function():
    x = 10
    del x
    print(x)

function()
```

### Desired solution

I don't know the history of the Variables checker, but it seems to me like pylint will keep running into issues around control flow as long as this basic approach is used. I wouldn't say that this is a limitation of the AST visitor pattern though. I think improvements could be made by doing any of (in order of increasing code complexity and running time, but also correctness IMO):

- Modifying ``NamesConsumer`` so that variables can be processed more than once.
- Using astroid's variable lookup functionality, which handles control flow better. [1]
- Building a control flow graph and then running ``VariablesChecker`` checks on the graph rather than the AST itself.

But all of these would be really significant changes so I'm not exactly advocating for them, just thought I'd mention. Personally I'm still very happy using pylint with the current functionality!

[1] Example:

```python
code = """
def function():
    x = 10
    del x
    print(x)
"""
import astroid
ast = astroid.parse(code)
n = [n for n in ast.nodes_of_class(astroid.Name) if n.name == "x"][0]
print(n.lookup('x'))  # Correctly doesn't return any AssignNames
```

### Additional context

This issue is taken entirely from a comment by @david-yz-liu [here](https://github.com/PyCQA/pylint/pull/4791#issuecomment-892076763) and will serve as the reference for discussion regarding control-flow implementation.
