title: bare-except (W0702) tricks developers into writing bugs
body:
### Current problem

_Apologies if I'm quite forthright with this one.  This particular problem has burned me pretty hard and I now suspect it has my colleagues also.  I'm aware of #3656 but that doesn't really address the problem._

Pylint is tricking developers into writing `except Exception:` where they really did mean (and most likely did want)`except:`.  The explanation of "bare-except" W0702 is:

> Catching exceptions should be as precise as possible. The type of exceptions that can be raised should be known in advance. Using a catch-all Exception instance defeats the purpose of knowing the type of error that occurred, and prohibits the use of tailored responses.

But, specifically where code was originally `except:` ... `raise` (bare-raise), there are too many cases where this description is entirely wrong.  

Indeed in my own code it's incorrect in almost every case.  That's because `except:`...`raise` is responding to the fact `try:` block did not complete and it isn't "handle" the exception at all.  Where the bare-raise is the only way out of the except block, there's almost never any kind of tailored response wanted.

The current set of pylint linting errors is tricking developers into refactoring `except:` ... `raise` into `except Exception:` ... `raise`.  **This does NOT do what the developer intended**.  If the developer's intent was to do something if-and-only-if the `try:` block did not complete, then `except Exception:` will miss some common events such as the user hitting ctrl^c at the command line.

#### Consider these two high-level examples
```python
try:
    do_something(x)
except:
    # Where cleanup(x) cannot execute on success, this must not go into finally
    cleanup(x)
    raise
```
We definitely do want to `cleanup()` irrespective of why `do_something()` failed.  

```python
try:
    do_something(x)
except:
    # Despite the fact we are logging to explain why do_something failed...
    # The condition that we want to log the error is dependent on there being ANY error.
    logger.error("Failed to complete do_something", ex_info=True)
    raise
```
We definitely do want to log _something_ irrespective of why `do_something()` failed.  Yes the log is reporting the error to the log so in that sense it is responding to the specific error.  But the actual code path in every case is the same.


### Desired solution part 1

The simplest thing would be to relax W0702 so that it does not trigger if the exception is immediately re-raised.  I believe a very similar clause applies to warnings associated with `except Exception:`.  Specifically it should not warn where all code paths lead to a bare `raise`.

That is
```python
try:
    something()
except:
    something_else()
```
would still raise the same warning it always has.  But 
```python
try:
    something()
except:
    something_else()
    raise
```
should no.

### Desired solution part 2
_Poking my head above the parapet ... I might be shot at for this_.  I wonder if an inverse warning would be appropriate on this code.  Something along the lines of "cleanup code may not execute in all intended circumstances":

```python
try:
    something()
except Exception:  # Really this is wrong, it should be a bare except
    something_else()
    raise
```

To be clear, that's based on the condition of an `except Exception:` block ending all code-paths with a bare `raise`.

I realise this would be opposed to many other linting tools as well as raising a lot of warnings on code that pylint has already pushed people into writing.  So I sense there may be some hot feelings about this suggestion.

title: Take type annotation into account when inference fails
body:
### Current problem

Right now pylint relies on its own inference engine, which ultimately is somewhat basic. This leads to quite a lot of false positives for deep inspections such as no-member, since what the entire inference is doing is just solving the variables into what values they might represent. In some cases it can properly infer the true value, in other cases we just infer a None or raise an inference error. This leads to quite a few false positives in which we cannot properly deduce the actual types due to the limited inference capabilities.


### Desired solution

While mypy is a fully fledged type checker, in our case we only need some support of type inference for function signatures and assignment signatures as well, in which case we could assume the type passed from the type signature instead of relying on our inference. This could also mean better performance, as we won't have to walk the tree calling the inference to figure out the type. A similar project that works in the same vein is jedi which doesn't need to do full type analysis as mypy is doing.

### Additional context

Taken from the comment from @PCManticore [here](https://github.com/PyCQA/pylint/issues/647#issuecomment-529359236)

```python
    """Case class (group Item)"""

    name: str
    irr: float = 0
    items: List[Item] = field(default_factory=lambda: [])

    def add_item(self, item: Item) -> None:
        """Add an item to the item list."""

        self.items.append(item)

    def find_item(self, description: str) -> Item:
        """Find an item by description"""

        return next((item for item in self.items if item.description == description), None)
```

With version 2.10.2, `pylint` returns:
```
E1101: Instance of 'Field' has no 'append' member (no-member)
```

Is this related to https://github.com/PyCQA/astroid/pull/1126?

Thanks

title: Warn against unicode identifier normalization
body:
### Current problem

Unicode normalization in unicode identifier. c.f.

* [2. Lexical analysis — Python 3.9.6 documentation](https://docs.python.org/3/reference/lexical\_analysis.html#identifiers)
* [Issue 44981: wildcard imports should normalise names in `__all__` - Python tracker](https://bugs.python.org/issue44981?@ok\_message=msg%20400106%20created%0Aissue%2044981%20created&@template=item)
* [python - Identifier normalization: Why is the micro sign converted into the Greek letter mu? - Stack Overflow](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/34097193/identifier-normalization-why-is-the-micro-sign-converted-into-the-greek-letter)

The issue is that while some characters can be used as identifier, internally it is normalized. So when looked up using `__all__` or `getattr`, it would results in an error. (See the 2nd & 3rd link for respective example.)

### Desired solution

pylint can warns about an identifier when normalized is different, and suggested a normalized identifier instead. i.e. it notifies that the identifier will be converted internally silently.

Similarly, pylint can warns those inside `__all__` and `getattr` too, as they can never be looked up due to no identifier would have such an unnormalized string.

### Additional context

Edit: keys of `globals()` & `locals()` also shares the same problem:

* [Issue 42680: unicode identifiers not accessible or assignable through globals() - Python tracker](https://bugs.python.org/issue42680)

### Keys to check

- [ ] `__all__[key]`
- [ ] `getattr(..., key)`
- [ ] `__getattr__(..., key)`
- [ ] `globals()[key]`
- [ ] `locals()[key]`

title: Separate check for possibly unused arguments if locals() is used
body:
### Current problem

* I have a long list of keywords to a method, that I want to define for type hints
* I need to pass these to another method as a dictionary, using `locals()`
* pylint detects possibly unused variables, but not arguments

### Desired solution

* pylint should extend the logic used around unused argument and variable definitions to consider arguments as potentially unused as well

### Additional context

Here is an example function that makes use of local scope variables and arguments.
```python
def example(arg_a='a', arg_b='b'):  # unused-argument
    """An example function."""
    var_c = 'c'  # potentially-unused-variable
    return locals()  # {'arg_a': 'a', 'arg_b': 'b', 'var_c': 'c'}
```

title: Raise a warning when a docstring is inherited but the type was changed in the child class
body:
### Current problem

For tools like doc builder `Sphinx` and editors' docstring parser, docstring of method inherited from parent class method (abstract or concrete) is not required. In fact, the tools use the docstring of the parent's method.

At the opposite of static linter (*e.g.* `Pydocstyle`), `Pylint` does not raise a warning of missing docstring if the parent's method has one, and you don't need to add some external package (and configs) to simulate the same for static linters (see [Pydocstyle issue 309](https://github.com/PyCQA/pydocstyle/issues/309)).

I think it is a great idea to keep this non-warning feature. In fact:
- docs builder Sphinx and editors' docstring parser works fine without the repeated docstring in the child method
- it respects the **minimal changes principle** in case of docstring modifications (you have to just change the parent's method docstring)

But I think `Pylint` is **not enough specific**: the need of child's docstring modification is sometimes necessary, and `Pylint` will not raise a warning.

As an example, let suppose docstring covention is Numpy:
```python
from abc import abstractmethod


class ParentA:
    """Parent class A."""


class ChildA(ParentA):
    """Child class A."""


class ParentB:
    """Parent class B: can be concrete or abstract class."""

    def __init__(self):
        self._attr: ParentA = ParentA()

    @abstractmethod
    def attr(self) -> ParentA:
        """Return the attribute.

        Returns
        -------
        ParentA
            The attribute attr
        """
        raise NotImplementedError


class ChildB(ParentB):
    """Concrete class ChildB."""

    def __init__(self):
        self._attr: ChildA = ChildA()

    def attr(self) -> ChildA:
        # Pylint will not raise any warning here because docstring of ParentB.attr exists
        return self._attr
```

So the docstring in Sphinx of `ChildB.attr` method will be the one of `ParentB.attr`
But the two methods don't return the same object type: the first `ChildA` type, the second `ParentA` type.


### Desired solution

I suggest `Pylint` raises a warning in that case like:
> "Return type in child method docstring is not enough specific"

So that it will force the user to re-write docstring of the child method like this:

```python
def attr(self) -> ChildA:
  """Return the attribute.

  Returns
  -----------
  ChildA
      The attribute attr
  """
  return self._attr
```

**But** `Pylint` should make sure that *the docstring elements of the parent and child methods that should not change remain the same*.

To conclude, this is what I suggest (with python color, just because it is better than text):
```python
if 'no docstring':
  if 'method is not overriding any method':
    # Warning: missing doc
  else:
    if 'type signatures of parameters or returns have changed':
      # Warning: you have to update overriding method's docstring
else:
  if 'type signatures of parameters or returns have not changed in overriding method':
    # Warning: unnecessary repeated docstring
  else:
    if 'dosctring of overriding and overridden methods are the same':
      # Warning: signatures not enough specific
```

### Additional context

- Some changes may modify the similar warning and errors table between linters in [Pylint issue 3512](https://github.com/PyCQA/pylint/issues/3512)

Versions
----------
- Python 3.9
- Pylint 3.0.0
- Pydocstyle 6.1.1
- Sphinx-build 4.1.2

### Pylint extension

```ini
load-plugins =
    pylint.extensions.emptystring,
    pylint.extensions.comparetozero,
    pylint.extensions.bad_builtin,
    pylint.extensions.docstyle,
    pylint.extensions.redefined_variable_type,
    pylint.extensions.overlapping_exceptions,
    pylint.extensions.docparams
```

Thank you for your amazing works !

title: False positive 'not-an-iterable' when using deferred initialization in a property
body:
### Bug description

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python
# File: pylint_not_an_iterable.py
"""
Demonstrate the false positive error 'not-an-iterable' in Pylint.

Pylint 2.10.2 raises the error with astroid 2.7.3 and 2.7.2.

In the original code, the error was raised only with astroid 2.7.3 and not
with astroid 2.7.2. This simplified code here raises the error with both
astroid versions.

The deferred initialization in NamedObject.names() is key for causing the
Pylint error to be raised; when performing the initialization in
NamedObject.__init__(), Pylint does not raise the error.
"""

# pylint: disable=missing-class-docstring,missing-function-docstring
# pylint: disable=useless-object-inheritance,too-few-public-methods
# pylint: disable=raise-missing-from,invalid-name

from __future__ import print_function


class NamedObject(object):

    def __init__(self, names):
        self._org_names = names
        self._names = None

    def _determine_names(self):
        self._names = [n.lower() for n in self._org_names]

    @property
    def names(self):
        # Deferred initialization
        if self._names is None:
            self._determine_names()
        return self._names


obj1 = NamedObject(['Bla'])
names1 = getattr(obj1, 'names')

# Pylint raises error 'not-an-iterable' for names1 on the following line:
names1_u = [n.upper() for n in names1]

print(names1_u)
```


### Configuration

_No response_

### Command used

```shell
pylint pylint_not_an_iterable.py  # with no config file present
```


### Pylint output

```shell
On Python 3.9 with astroid 2.7.2 and with 2.7.3:
************* Module pylint_not_an_iterable
pylint_not_an_iterable.py:45:31: E1133: Non-iterable value names1 is used in an iterating context (not-an-iterable)
```


### Expected behavior

Since the value returned from the `names` property is an iterable, Pylint should not raise the "not-an-iterable" error.

Pylint probably does not figure out that the initialization in `NamedObject.names()` is deferred. When initializing in the init method instead, Pylint does not raise the error.

Also, there seems to be a potential dependency to the astroid version: The original code did not cause this error to be raised when using astroid 2.7.2 on Python 3.8 and 3.9 (see [these test runs from 8/29](https://github.com/pywbem/pywbem/actions/runs/1178348129)), but did cause this error to be raised with astroid 2.7.3 on Python 3.8 and 3.9 (see [these test runs from 9/5](https://github.com/pywbem/pywbem/actions/runs/1202131339)). astroid 2.7.3 was released on 8/30, i.e. in between these two sets of runs. However, the simplified code shown above causes the error to be raised for both astroid versions on Python 3.9. I did not dig deeper into the reasons for what triggers the version dependency.

### Pylint version

```shell
On Python 3.9 with astroid 2.7.3:
pylint 2.10.2
astroid 2.7.3  # and also with 2.7.2, see above.
Python 3.9.6 (default, Jun 29 2021, 06:20:32) 
[Clang 12.0.0 (clang-1200.0.32.29)]
```


### OS / Environment

macOS 10.15.7

### Additional dependencies

Dependencies of Pylint:

On Python 3.9 with astroid 2.7.3:
$ pipdeptree --python $(which python) -p pylint
pylint==2.10.2
  - astroid [required: >=2.7.2,<2.8, installed: 2.7.3]
    - lazy-object-proxy [required: >=1.4.0, installed: 1.6.0]
    - setuptools [required: >=20.0, installed: 57.5.0]
    - wrapt [required: >=1.11,<1.13, installed: 1.12.1]
  - isort [required: >=4.2.5,<6, installed: 5.9.3]
  - mccabe [required: >=0.6,<0.7, installed: 0.6.1]
  - platformdirs [required: >=2.2.0, installed: 2.3.0]
  - toml [required: >=0.7.1, installed: 0.10.2]

On Python 3.9 with astroid 2.7.2:
$ pipdeptree --python $(which python) -p pylint
pylint==2.10.2
  - astroid [required: >=2.7.2,<2.8, installed: 2.7.2]
    - lazy-object-proxy [required: >=1.4.0, installed: 1.6.0]

title: Incorrect report "unused-import" when assigning to class attribute with the same name as the imported module.
body:
### Bug description

Pylint incorrectly reports that a module was imported but not used, when the module is only used in a type annotation of a class that has a `@property` decorated function with the same name as the imported module.

```python

import gettext


class C:
    def __init__(self, translation: gettext.NullTranslations) -> None:
        self.translation = translation

    @property
    def gettext(self) -> None:
        return None
```

If the `gettext` module is used elsewhere, the error won't be reported, e.g.:


```python
import gettext


class C:
    def __init__(self, translation: gettext.NullTranslations) -> None:
        self.translation: gettext.NullTranslations = translation

    @property
    def gettext(self) -> None:
        return None
```

If the property is in a different class, the error won't be reported either, e.g.:


```python
import gettext


class C:
    def __init__(self, translation: gettext.NullTranslations) -> None:
        self.translation = translation


class B:
    @property
    def gettext(self) -> None:
        return None
```

This is not specific to the `gettext` module either, e.g.:

```python
import decimal


class C:
    def __init__(self, d: decimal.Decimal) -> None:
        pass

    @property
    def decimal(self) -> None:
        return None
```


### Configuration

_No response_

### Command used

```shell
pylint a.py
```


### Pylint output

```shell
************* Module a
a.py:1:0: C0114: Missing module docstring (missing-module-docstring)
a.py:4:0: C0103: Class name "C" doesn't conform to PascalCase naming style (invalid-name)
a.py:4:0: C0115: Missing class docstring (missing-class-docstring)
a.py:9:4: C0116: Missing function or method docstring (missing-function-docstring)
a.py:4:0: R0903: Too few public methods (1/2) (too-few-public-methods)
a.py:1:0: W0611: Unused import gettext (unused-import)
```


### Expected behavior

The error `W0611` should not be reported, since the `gettext` module is used.

### Pylint version

```shell
pylint 2.7.2
astroid 2.5.1
Python 3.9.2 (default, Feb 28 2021, 17:03:44) 
[GCC 10.2.1 20210110]
```


### OS / Environment

_No response_

_No response_

title: Improve docs for `py-version`
body:
Followup to #5019 and #5024

The current documentation around the `py-version` setting introduced in `2.11` could be improved.
* Conflicting default values -> Default is autogenerated from `sys.version_info`. Likely requires an exception to not show a default value #5069
* Explain best practices
  * Most users should default to running `pylint` with the oldest Python version their project supports
  * In some cases it's useful to set `py-version` to allow running `pylint` with newer versions. E.g. when `pylint` is used together with a pre-commit hook and  most development happens on a newer Python version.
  * Setting `py-version` will only hide suggestions for features that aren't included in all (by a project supported) Python versions.

title: Negation and None

title: E1507 (invalid-envvar-value) does not accept `builtins.str` as a valid type for `os.getenv` argument
body:
### Bug description

If the input argument of `os.getenv` is of type `builtins.str` pylint complains with the error message `os.getenv does not support builtins.str type argument (invalid-envvar-value)`. This should not be the case since `builtins.str` is a string. Which is the expected type like mentioned [here](https://vald-phoenix.github.io/pylint-errors/plerr/errors/stdlib/E1507): "Env manipulation functions support only string type arguments.".

This happens when the key value of the environment variable comes from a data class attribute. Example:

```python
import os
from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Optional


@dataclass
class EnvVar:
    """Environment variable to be set."""

    name: str
    _initial_value: Optional[str] = None

    def __post_init__(self):
        """Save current env var value in `self._initial_value`."""

        self._initial_value = os.getenv(self.name)
```

And the warning is related to `os.getenv(self.name)`.

### Command used

```shell
pylint env.py
```


### Pylint output

```shell
env.py:11:30 E1507 os.getenv does not support builtins.str type argument (invalid-envvar-value)
```


### Expected behavior

No warning raised when the input argument of `os.getenv` is `builtins.str`.

### Pylint version

```shell
pylint 2.11.1
astroid 2.8.0
Python 3.9.4
```


### OS / Environment

mascOS Big Sur (Versin 11.5.2)

title: Refactor so ``Pylinter`` do not need to be a ``BaseChecker`` anymore
body:
### Current problem

The Pylinter class is very big and does a lot of thing. There's also some meta checks that need information from Pylinter and other management class so they can't be a ``BaseChecker`` as they need meta-information not available outside of Pylinter. Another design could separate the coupling between those meta-warning and Pylinter. 



### Desired solution

 Use composition instead of inheritance ? Create a ``MetaChecker`` class for those checker ? Find a proper design to decouple this.

### Additional context

Meta warning are:
> all message that are added with add_message in ``Pylinter``, ``MessageHandlerMixin`` or ``OptionManagerMixin`` instead of being their own Checker class (ie exhaustive list if you search for add_message in those classes's files).

See [discussion](https://github.com/PyCQA/pylint/pull/5136#discussion_r728410412).

title: Add dataclass tests
body:
### Current problem

```py
from dataclasses import dataclass, field

@dataclass
class Expandable:
    _api: field()
    var = []
```

### Desired solution

Detect invalid or problematic usage in dataclasses.

**Some ideas**
- [ ] Invalid `field` call. It should be the value of the assignment
- [ ] Missing type annotation
- [ ] Missing use of `field` -> `default_factory` for mutable types. Careful of `ClassVars` as it might be intended for them.
- [ ] Unsupported-version check as dataclasses require Python 3.7

--
It might be best to create a new checker class for it.

title: Catch `undefined-variable` for variables with only type assignment and `NamedExpr` calls
body:
#5163 added the following test to the test suite for `undefined-variable`:

```python
# pylint: disable-next=fixme
# TODO: With better control-flow inference related to NamedExpr we should see that the
# NamedExpr is never called here and the second call to `var` is thus undefined
def typing_and_incorrect_assignment_expression():
    """The variable gets assigned in an assignment expression which is never called"""
    var: int
    if False:
        if (var := 1 ** 2):
            print(var)
    else:
        print(var) # This should raise
```

Basically, we should be able to recognise when `NamedExpr`s have been called. In this case it clearly hasn't and thus `var` remains undefined.

title: Suggest limit checks instead of bool `in range` with step 1/-1
body:
### Current problem

I noticed a handful of occurrences of using `in range(...)` with the range's `step` being left out (i.e. the default 1 being used) in boolean context in the Home Assistant codebase.

This and a few other cases could be replaced with some more performant and explicit lower and upper limit checks, and it would be nice if pylint could warn about/suggest that.

For example:
```shellsession
$ python3 -m timeit 'x = 999; x in range(0, 1000)'
1000000 loops, best of 5: 265 nsec per loop
$ python3 -m timeit 'x = 999; 0 <= x < 1000'
5000000 loops, best of 5: 49.2 nsec per loop
```

In addition to the performance gain which in most cases would likely not be noticeable, explicit limit checks avoid the pitfall from misremembering whether and which one of `range` args are inclusive/exclusive.

### Desired solution

Flag uses of `in range` where the range's `step` is 1, -1, or left out (defaults to 1) in boolean context, suggest using limits instead:

- `x in range(lo, hi)` -> `lo <= x < hi`
- `x in range(lo, hi, 1)` -> `lo <= x < hi`
- `x in range(hi, lo, -1)` -> `hi >= x > lo`

### Additional context

Seems CPython's [`range` containment operation](https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/main/Objects/rangeobject.c#L428) is not naive for most cases, but reduces to using limit checks, so it doesn't matter much at all how large the range is or where in the range the tested item is. I guess the price is in that case paid for the range object creation. But this means the same price is paid for smaller ranges as well which might seem counterintuitive unless one is aware of the under the hood implementation.

title: Inconsistent behaviour with attribute-defined-outside-init
body:
### Bug description
There seems to be an inconsistency in the way pylint treats attributes that have been defined in `__init__` and a helper function called by `__init__`.

I would expect the two assignments to var1 would either both trigger the linter or both be OK. Instead the first is OK and the second isn't.

```python

"""Module docstring"""

class TestLint:
    """Test doc string"""

    def __init__(self):
        self.init_helper()
        self.var2 = True

    def init_helper(self):
        """Helper doc string"""
        self.var1 = True # no lint error

    def other_func(self):
        """Other func doc string"""
        self.var1 = False # lint error
        self.var2 = False # no lint error
```


### Configuration

_No response_

### Command used

```shell
pylint test.py
```


### Pylint output

```shell
test.py:16:8: W0201: Attribute 'var1' defined outside __init__ (attribute-defined-outside-init)
```


### Expected behavior

I am not sure which of the two options is correct but the current behavior with differing behavior is wrong.

```shell

astroid 2.8.4
```

title: Support an Option to Disable sys.path patching
body:
### Current problem

I want an argument/config option to disable sys.path patching. Pylint currently modifies sys.path based on `__init__.py` in folders of the linted files. sys.path patching is messy to get right and current logic for it here has a bug for implicit namespace packages that has been open for [years](https://github.com/PyCQA/pylint/issues/3944).  As a side effect a simple package structure of,

```
foo/
--> A.py

bar/
--> foo/
------> A.py
```

where bar is a namespace package breaks if you try to lint both foo.A and bar.foo.A together.

While making implicit namespace packages work with sys.path patching is probably possible, sys.path patching means files are linted in a way different than actual execution of those imports in interpreter. You can have a folder pass because of patching, but running script would fail from that location if uninstalled.

A related issue is importing files in two separate packages sharing a name like in this issue is incompatible with current sys.path [patching](https://github.com/PyCQA/pylint/issues/2966). Any solution for that issue will involve either not patching sys.path or patching more selectively. 

### Desired solution

Support a config argument/command line argument to disable patching. The current patch logic is only done in two places [here](https://github.com/PyCQA/pylint/blob/main/pylint/lint/pylinter.py#L987) and [here](https://github.com/PyCQA/pylint/blob/main/pylint/lint/pylinter.py#L987). An option to disable it entirely would be a small patch to pylint. I'd be happy to make the patch if this change would be supported. 

### Additional context

_No response_

title: "Unable to init server" warning with GTK import
body:
### Bug description

When running Pylint on code which imports any GTK or GDK stuff via the GObject mechanism, the warning `Unable to init server: Could not connect: Connection refused` is printed out.

Here's a complete reproducer (running in a fresh Fedora 35 container):

    # dnf install --refresh -y python3-pylint python3-gobject pygobject3-devel gtk3-devel
    # cd /tmp ; cat > test.py <<EOF
    import sys
    print(len(sys.argv))
    EOF

`test.py` is a simple Python script with no complicated logic. Pylint goes through it just fine:

    # pylint-3 -d C0114,W0611 test.py
    
    ------------------------------------
    Your code has been rated at 10.00/10

Now let's add the GTK import and see what happens:

    # sed -ie '1a import gi.repository.Gtk' test.py
    # pylint-3 -d C0114,W0611 test.py
    Unable to init server: Could not connect: Connection refused
    Unable to init server: Could not connect: Connection refused
    
    --------------------------------------------------------------------
    Your code has been rated at 10.00/10 (previous run: 10.00/10, +0.00)

Via some magical mechanism, this also causes our Pylint pipeline to time out, see, for example, [this recent run](https://github.com/abrt/gnome-abrt/runs/4090912194?check_suite_focus=true).

I've found a related bug, #2649, but its outcome is not satisfactory.

### Expected behavior

I would expect Pylint to lint the file without emitting any unrelated warnings and not to time out when being run as part of a GitHub workflow.

### Pylint version

```shell
pylint 2.11.1
astroid 2.8.4
Python 3.10.0 (default, Oct  5 2021, 00:00:00) [GCC 11.2.1 20210728 (Red Hat 11.2.1-1)]
```

### OS / Environment

Fedora 35 (inside a container)

### Additional dependencies

_No response_

title: Enforcing justification for disabled messages
body:
### Current problem

Currently we can suppress warnings in code with comments like this:
\# pylint:disable=warning-type

What happens often in codebases I work on is that people add this without specifying any justification, hence I'd like to have a way to enforce it.



### Desired solution

Add an option that would switch on the "justification enforcing". With this option, every suppression would have to be followed with another line:
\# pylint:justification="Why we really need this"

So the final format would be as follows:
\# pylint:disable=warning-type
\# pylint:justification="Why we really need this"

If the option is not set, the behavior would not change.

### Additional context

I'd like to implement it myself, however It would be good to get some form of green light first.

title: Multiline disable for long list of disabled messages
body:
### Current problem

Sometime disable need to span multiple lines :

```python
# pylint: disable=invalid-name,too-many-public-methods,attribute-defined-outside-init
# pylint: disable= useless-object-inheritance,too-few-public-methods
```

Or contrived example with ``disable-next`` or inline comment (how many message can you want to disable on one line ?)
```python
# pylint: disable-next=invalid-name,too-many-public-methods,attribute-defined-outside-init, useless-object-inheritance,too-few-public-methods
actual_problem = 0
```

```python
actual_problem = 0 # pylint: disable=invalid-name,too-many-public-methods,attribute-defined-outside-init, useless-object-inheritance,too-few-public-methods
```

### Desired solution

It would be nice to be able to do:

```python
# pylint: disable=invalid-name,too-many-public-methods,attribute-defined-outside-init
# pylint: useless-object-inheritance,too-few-public-methods
```
or
```python
# pylint: disable-next=invalid-name,too-many-public-methods,attribute-defined-outside-init
# pylint: useless-object-inheritance,too-few-public-methods
actual_problem = 0
```
or
```python
actual_problem = 0 # pylint: disable=invalid-name,too-many-public-methods, attribute-defined-outside-init,
# pylint: useless-object-inheritance,too-few-public-methods
```

### Additional context

This is required in order to add enforced justification in disable : https://github.com/PyCQA/pylint/issues/5253

title: False-positive `signature-differs` / `arguments-differ` with `typing.overload`
body:
### Bug description

Noticed that one while working on https://github.com/PyCQA/astroid/pull/1217 with @DanielNoord.

The logic for `signature-differs` / `arguments-differ` can't handle function redefinitions as its common with `typing.overload`. In particular I narrowed it down to two issue:
1. All function definitions seem to be check, even those decorated with `overload`.
2. The point of comparison is always the first definition, even if that isn't the true signature.

--
The examples require **Python 3.8** (due to the use of `typing.Literal`). Not that they aren'T necessarily typed correctly. Just for illustration proposes.

**Example 1**
```py
# pylint: disable=unused-argument,no-self-use,missing-docstring
from typing import Literal, Union, overload

class Parent:
    @overload
    def statement(self) -> int:
        ...

    @overload
    def statement(self, future: Literal[True]) -> str:
        ...

    def statement(self, future: Literal[None, True] = None) -> Union[int, str]:
        pass


class Child(Parent):
    # Signature comparison happens with first definition - the overload
    # Should use the actual function instead!
    def statement(self, future=None):  # arguments-differ (false-positive)
        pass
```

**Example 2**
```py
# pylint: disable=unused-argument,no-self-use,missing-docstring
from typing import Literal, Union, overload

class Parent2:
    def statement(self, future: Literal[None, True] = None) -> Union[int, str]:
        pass

class Child2(Parent2):
    @overload
    def statement(self, future: Literal[None] = ...) -> int:
        ...

    @overload
    def statement(self, future: Literal[True]) -> str:  # signature-differs (false-positive)
        # Even overloads are checked although these aren't required
        # to have the correct signature
        ...

    def statement(self, future: Literal[None, True] = None) -> Union[int, str]:
        pass
```

### Configuration

_No response_

### Command used

```shell
pylint test.py
```


### Pylint output

```shell
W0221: Number of parameters was 1 in 'Parent.statement' and is now 2 in overridden 'Child.statement' method (arguments-differ)

W0222: Signature differs from overridden 'statement' method (signature-differs)
```


### Expected behavior

_no errors_

### Pylint version

```shell
2.11.2-dev0
```

_No response_

_No response_

title: Override existing checks with a plugin
body:
### Current problem

Hey guys,

I use [pyinvoke](https://www.pyinvoke.org/) a lot, but pylint doesn't like some stuff a lot.
In invoke so called tasks are declared like this

```python
@task
def my_task(context):
  print("Hello World")
```

Please note the `context` argument: this argument is mandatory for each task and must be named like this. Pylint detects completely a right an `unused-argument`.

For my project I wrote a small pylint plugin, which finds all functions decorated with `task` and an `context` argument and ignores the `unused-argument`. However, this is done by patching `VariablesChecker.add_message`. This doesn't look nice to me:

```python
def register(linter):
    original = VariablesChecker.add_message

    def _new_add_message(
        self, msgid, line = None, node = None, args= None, confidence= None, col_offset= None,
    ):
        if (
            msgid == "unused-argument"
            and isinstance(node, AssignName)
            and node.name == "context"
            and isinstance(node.parent, Arguments)
            and isinstance(node.parent.parent, FunctionDef)
            and "invoke.tasks.task" in node.parent.parent.decoratornames()
        ):
            return
        original(self, msgid, line, node, args, confidence, col_offset)

    VariablesChecker.add_message = _new_add_message
```


### Desired solution

A way to improve certain existing checks, e.g. by override

### Additional context

[Pylint-pytest](https://pypi.org/project/pylint-pytest/) uses a similar way as I do

title: Allow five digit message id's (and possibly disallow four digit id's)
body:
### Current problem

`pylint` only allows message `id`'s up to `9999` since we are limiting ourselves to four digit `id`'s.
If we are going to limit each 2 digit prefix to an individual checker and keep the 51-99 prefixes for external plugins we might run out of prefixes. Therefore, we should probably increase the max length to be 5 digits.

For reference:

> Another thing, if each checker gets its own prefix, we are essential limited to `100`. That might work for us, but what about `plugins`? Should we consider 5 digits? For existing checks, we could add a leading `0`. We need to keep in mind though that is might also be a breaking change. So I suggest `3.0` for it, too.

> _Originally posted by @cdce8p in https://github.com/PyCQA/pylint/issues/5255#issuecomment-960897436_
 

### Desired solution

We will need to find a good way to resolve the current id's to follow the new scheme.

I think we can still consider the **last** two digits to be specific to each message while the **first** three digits can be considered the prefix. If a checker handles 99+ messages it might be time to separate it into a new class for easier maintainability, so limiting each checker to 99 messages should be fine.

Then we need a way to resolve the messages which currently have prefixes 1-50 and 51-99. There are two options:
1. keep accepting four digit `id`'s and append a leading zero when we encounter them or 
2. force extension maintainers (and ourselves) to add the leading zero and only accept five digit `id`'s.

Option 2 obviously has the benefit that it will be much easier to maintain and I think would only require minimal work from maintainers, they can just do `find all + replace`. However, for users that are disabling based on `id`'s this might become more problematic. However, I wonder how large the group of users is that is upgrading to semversion breaking changes and is using `id`'s for disables. The `docs` are quite clear about preferring message names for disables.

A second thing to discuss is which prefixes we will reserve for external plugins. Again, I see two options:
A. 1-500 for `pylint` and 501-999 for plugins
B. 1-50 for `pylint` and 51-99 for plugins, 100-150 for `pylint` and 151-199 for plugins, etc.
Here I think the option A might be best, as it is more in line with the current division.

### Additional context

_No response_

title: signature-mutators should also handle "arguments-differ"
body:
### Current problem

I have a decorator which completely changes the signature of a method, which then overloads an ABC. It does this by changing the return type, and types and number of args the method receives. When I do this, I get

`X,Y,warning,arguments-differ:Number of parameters was 2 in 'Base.Method' and is now 3 in overridden 'Concrete.Method' method`

If I set `signature-mutators` to include the decorator used here, I still see this

### Desired solution

It sounds like signature-mutators already disables 2 or 3 lints, it should include arguments-differ too, or maybe it should understand these types of decorators better -- both my method and decorator are fully type annotated.

### Additional context

pylint 2.11.1
astroid 2.8.4
Python 3.8.7

title: ``consider-using-f-string`` and ``logging-fstring-interpolation`` are mutually exclusive
body:
### Bug description

Enabling both C0209 (consider-using-f-string) and W1203(logging-fstring-interpolation) causes conflict issues.
If logging is using % formatting, then C0209 error message is throwing on the logging string. Is there a way to avoid this conflict?

### Configuration

```ini
# Maximum number of nested blocks for function / method body
max-nested-blocks=5

# Disable the message, report, category or checker with the given id(s). You
# can either give multiple identifiers separated by comma (,) or put this
# option multiple times (only on the command line, not in the configuration
# file where it should appear only once).You can also use "--disable=all" to
# disable everything first and then reenable specific checks. For example, if
# you want to run only the similarities checker, you can use "--disable=all
# --enable=similarities". If you want to run only the classes checker, but have
# no Warning level messages displayed, use"--disable=all --enable=classes
# --disable=W"
# Example:
# disable=E1608,W1627,E1601
# we are disabling all warnings as it would create a lot of noise
# we will enable only selected warnings when needed.
disable=all

# Enable the message, report, category or checker with the given id(s). You can

enable=C0114,C0206,C0209
    ,E0102,E1111,E1120,E1121,E1123,E1305,E1606,E1601
    ,E9004,E9005,E9006,E9007,E9008,E9009,E9010,E9011

```

title: Detect improvable code with open + pathlib.Path
body:
### Current problem

In the python world. developers tend to use both the old `os.path` based system and `pathlib` in parallel. This can lead to some confusion.
Recently I've seen some code like this:
```python
from pathlib import Path
path = Path("foo")
with open(str(path)) as handle:
  pass
```

### Desired solution

Since `open` can consume a `Path` object, the above code can be improved to `open(path)` or `path.open()`

### Additional context

I'm not 100% sure if this is in the scope of pylint, but calling `str` over and over again will lead to a worse performance (even if not too much). Therefore I see such a check as a benefit.
I'm also happy to open a PR, if the idea is accepted.

title: Check tempfile.TemporaryFile() and siblings for unspecified-encoding (W1514)
body:
### Current problem

Currently `tempfile.TemporaryFile()` and siblings don't generate an `unspecified-encoding` warning when no explicit encoding is supplied. Presumably it should, in similar fashion to `open()`, `pathlib.Path().write_text()` etc.

### Desired solution

Check uses of the relevant `tempfile` classes for lack of explicit encodings.

### Additional context

_No response_

title: Provide ``pre-commit`` hooks even without having to use a system hook
body:
### Current problem

Pylint need the code dependencies to be installed to be able to analyze the code. So it need to be aware of the dependencies it needs if we want to be able to install it in an isolated virtualenv environment for pre-commit. 

Right now this is not the case so the pre-commit hook file that is in the repository is miseleading.

### Desired solution

Find a way to make the dependencies known to pylint (new option ?) so it's installed by pre-commit in the isolated venv. Or make pylint detect the dependencies from the setup.py / setup.cfg / pyproject.toml and install them ? I'm not really comfortable with installing dependencies at run time, and I can't think of a better design myself, so I'm open to suggestions.

### Additional context

https://github.com/PyCQA/pylint/pull/5292, and #4505

title: option to enforce messages on `assert` statements
body:
### Current problem

it's easy to write an `assert` statement that results in a useless error message:

```py
assert expected_value == actual_value
```
if this assertion fails, it will give you a useless error message like `AssertionError` with no other information.

### Desired solution

a pylint rule that enforces a message argument to the assert statement, ie:
```py
assert expected_value == actual_value, f"{expected_value=} did not match {actual_value=}"
```

### Additional context

_No response_

title: False positive `consider-using-with` for context managers passed to `AsyncExitStack`
body:
### Bug description

The following code:
```py
# pylint: disable=missing-docstring

import contextlib

async def bug() -> None:
    async with contextlib.AsyncExitStack() as stack:
        stack.enter_context(open("/tmp/x", "wb"))
```
triggers a false-positive:
```
$ pylint a.py 
************* Module a
a.py:7:28: R1732: Consider using 'with' for resource-allocating operations (consider-using-with)
```
https://github.com/PyCQA/pylint/pull/4665 was supposed to fix that but for some reason the fix doesn't work for `AsyncExitStack` (it works with `ExitStack`).

title: Provide an auto-upgrade option / migration tool for pylint configurations
body:
### Current problem

There are breaking changes that could impact the configuration file and make our live as maintainer easier. Asking the user to upgrade their configuration files make most breaking chance impossible. I we can upgrade the configuration file automatically it's easier to make the configuration consistent and easier to maintain. For example a migration to pyproject.toml only instead of maintaining setup.cfg, pylintrc (ini) and pyrpoejct.toml.

### Desired solution

New subcommand ``pylint --auto-upgrade-conf`` to upgrade the configuration file.

### Additional context

https://github.com/PyCQA/pylint/issues/4741#issuecomment-984887726

The new framework for configuration file would help to implement this greatly, we would add an auto-upgraded expected result.

title: Auto-upgrade the ``whitelist`` and ``blacklist`` options in configuration
body:
### Current problem

In `.pylintrc` we have the following attribute

`extension-pkg-whitelist=`

For context, here is a description of that 
```
# A comma-separated list of package or module names from where C extensions may
# be loaded. Extensions are loading into the active Python interpreter and may
# run arbitrary code. (This is an alternative name to extension-pkg-allow-list
# for backward compatibility.)
```

At what point do we end backwards compatibility on this term? It is worth deprecating that language at some point.

### Desired solution

We should deprecate `extension-pkg-whitelist` in favor of `extension-pkg-allow-list` for all references

### Additional context

Lots of different inclusivity guides out there, but here's one
https://developers.google.com/style/inclusive-documentation

title: Emit `arguments-differ` if function definition overwritten to have different argument count
body:
### Current problem

Overwriting a function definition with another definition having a different argument count is a possible anti-pattern.

```python
def noop(x):  # pylint: disable=invalid-name
    """Return value unchanged"""
    return x


def add(x, y):  # pylint: disable=invalid-name
    """Add two values"""
    return x + y


def main(param):
    """Should not emit too-many-function-args"""
    tmp = noop
    if param == 0:
        tmp = add   # I'm proposing this emit arguments-differ
    return tmp(1, 1.01)
```

This code sample will become `tests/functional/t/too/too_many_function_args.py` in #5409.

### Desired solution

Emit `arguments-differ` in the marked line above.

### Additional context

Discussed in https://github.com/PyCQA/pylint/pull/5409/files/13ebc62da361bd4856af9b7c1daccc69b7210e15#r762428183.

title: Warn if module dunders are below imports
body:
### Current problem

The PEP-8 guidelines state that module level dunders are supposed to be [above](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0008/#module-level-dunder-names) imports. This is something pylint does not warn about if the code does not follow it.



### Desired solution

Pylint should raise a warning if the above is not followed.



### Additional context

Similar to #965, but also add a warning for it

title: Disable `assignment-from-none` for methods
body:
```py
# pylint: disable=no-self-use

class Parent:
    def func(self) -> int | None:
        return None

    def serialize(self) -> str:
        x = self.func()  # assignment-from-none
        return f"Result: {x if x else ''}"

class Child(Parent):
    def func(self) -> int:
        return 42
```

In the example above, `assignment-from-none` is emitted on a method call even though `func()` could be overwritten and not return `None`.

**Proposal**
Limit the `assignment-from-none` check to functions. Methods should only be considered if marked with `@typing.final`. It might also work to check the return type itself to see if it's only `-> None`.

**Reason**
We can't reliably determine if a method is ever overwritten and thus return something else then `None`.

title: [cell-var-from-loop] false positive for lambda used as keyword arg
body:
### Bug description

Hi,

I'm little confused by this warning which `pylint 2.12.2` found in the following `example.py`:

```python
"""cell-var-from-loop example."""

grouped_messages = {
    "category_a": [
        {"key_a": 3},
        {"key_a": 1},
        {"key_a": 2},
    ],
    "category_b": [
        {"key_b": 5},
        {"key_b": 3},
        {"key_b": 8},
    ],
}
sorting_keys = {
    "category_a": "key_a",
    "category_b": "key_b",
}

sorted_messages = {}

for category, messages in grouped_messages.items():
    sorted_messages[category] = sorted(
        (message for message in messages),
        key=lambda msg: msg[sorting_keys[category]]
    )

print(sorted_messages)
```

I got rid of this warning by replacing `key=lambda msg: msg[sorting_keys[category]]` with `key=operator.itemgetter(sorting_keys[category])`.
TBH I'm not sure why pylint complains about `category` being defined in loop.
Especially, because both versions of the code, give exactly the same result:

```shell
python3 example.py 
{'category_a': [{'key_a': 1}, {'key_a': 2}, {'key_a': 3}], 'category_b': [{'key_b': 3}, {'key_b': 5}, {'key_b': 8}]}
```

This might be a continuation of: https://github.com/PyCQA/pylint/pull/4827

Thanks

_No response_

### Command used

```shell
pylint example.py
```


### Pylint output

```shell

example.py:25:41: W0640: Cell variable category defined in loop (cell-var-from-loop)

------------------------------------------------------------------
Your code has been rated at 8.33/10 (previous run: 8.33/10, +0.00)
```


### Expected behavior

No warnings

title: ``no-member`` false negative if member conditionally defined in parent class
body:
I also wonder what happens when the assignment in the base class is conditional.
Something like:
```python
class ClassWithMember:
     """Member defined in superclass."""
     def __init__(self):
         if False:
             self.member = True

class AssignMemberFromSuper(ClassWithMember):
     """This assignment is valid due to inheritance."""
     def __init__(self):
         super().__init__()
         self.member = self.member
```
That would require control flow, but would we emit the message with the current code? Or do we assume the assignment succeeded?
If we still emit we might want to open an issue about it to track future progress.

_Originally posted by @DanielNoord in https://github.com/PyCQA/pylint/pull/5544#r771456956_

title: Suggest replacing `Tuple[X]` with `Tuple[X, ...]` or `Sequence[X]`
body:
### Current problem

When `Tuple[X]` is used as a type annotation, it means "a `tuple` of length 1, whose (sole) element is of type `X`". However, since there are not very many situations in which one would genuinely want to specify something as being a 1-tuple, the vast majority of the time that someone writes `Tuple[X]`, they are doing so out of a mistaken belief that `Tuple[X]` is analogous to `List[X]` ("a `list` of `X`s"). The syntax for "a `tuple` of `X`s of any length" is `Tuple[X, ...]`.

### Desired solution

It would be great if pylint were to flag uses of `Tuple[X]` and inform the user of the likely misunderstanding. In most cases, the user will likely be trying to express the notion "an (immutable) sequence of `X`s", so the most appropriate suggestion would probably be `Sequence[X]`. However, the pylint message should also inform the user that if they do, indeed, want to be specific about the object being a `tuple` (of unknown length), then the correct syntax would be `Tuple[X, ...]`.

title: Next step in pylint's gamification and user experience
body:
This is a follow up to #2399 and relate to #5403. It was discussed in detail in https://github.com/PyCQA/pylint/pull/4538#issuecomment-853355683

Right now when starting to use pylint the score can be really bad and we might be dumping thousands of warning on users. This is demoralizing, even for code quality oriented persons and hurting adoption for legacy projects.

> The experience I have right now in starting to use pylint on a code base is overwhelming, I have to use ``pylint .|grep -o "([a-z\-]*)$"|cut -d "(" -f2|sort|uniq|sed s/\)/,/`` to disable all the existing warning and start from somewhere. Then I need to think hard before enabling them again to know where to start. The way I see it the pylint guide would add the disable automatically, maybe in pylintrc, maybe in code directly, then it would rank the warning and tell you exactly what to do next and provide a feeling of progression with level up and "encouragements". Congratulation you do not have any error level warning, Congratulation the code follow SOLID principles, Congratulation we do not have any performance improvement to provide, Congratulation your code did not regress after upgrading to pylint 2.10.1, you were already that good, Congratulation you've got a perfect code according to pylint's opinion etc. A lot of variables to follow that could be in the pylintrc.

The first step is Implementing baseline so only new message are raised : #5403. This way pylint can be adopted easily on a legacy codebase.

Then I think we could create a pylint "guide". What I mean by that is upgrading the pylintrc step by step to fix the legacy messages starting by the more significant one (error, then vicious warning, then warning, then important convention, then opinionated convention, then adding extensions...). This way there would be a clear path to clean code and nothing overwhelming along the way. This would permit to add "levels" and "achievements" and improve gamification. The score do not need to be bad, it can be a good score for this level (i.e. good jobs there are no errors, do you want to increase the quality of your code ?). Pylint would be friendlier and it would help user to increase their code base quality.

title: Refactor the message handling to use a smaller data structure
body:
### Current problem

The message store use message definition, which are big data structure only required when we need to create the messages or explains the message. This is minor as it's probably a small performance gain. Also, we dynamically check and generate the data structure from our code which is always the same for a version.

### Desired solution

We could use the msgid internally and simplify the code (and even improve performance). A translation from symbol to msgid when a symbol is used need to be done upfront. We could use typing to enforce that as msgid are string starting with a char betwee E W C or R and then 4 to 5 digits. , We could also generate an optimized data structure for Pylint's own message and only check the message for user plugin and extension.

### Additional context

#5606 #5605

title: Pylint catches missing method in some situations but not in others
body:
### Bug description

Pylint ignores a `no-member` error when invoking a non-existing method from a Spark Context class if the class is returned from another method, but correctly recognizes the method does not exist if I call it on a `SparkContext` object directly.

I don't know if this is an issue with spark/pyspark specifically or if it's a more general issue, or if it's something that I should be doing differently.

```python
from pyspark import SparkConf, SparkContext
from pyspark.sql import SparkSession


class SparkInit:
    def __init__(self):
        self.active_spark_session = None

    @property
    def spark(self) -> SparkSession:
        if self.active_spark_session is None:
            conf = SparkConf().setAppName("hi")
            self.active_spark_session = SparkSession.builder.config(conf=conf).getOrCreate()
        return self.active_spark_session

    @property
    def context(self) -> SparkContext:
        return self.spark.sparkContext


def main():
    # this passes
    spark_context = SparkInit().context
    spark_context.blabla()

    # this fails
    spark_context = SparkContext()
    spark_context.blabla()
```

```ini
(no .pylintrc configuration)
```

```shell
python -m pylint -E example.py
```

### Pylint output

title: option to treat `TYPE_CHECKING` as `True`, or add a separate variable
Pylint is not using a src/ folder
body:
### Bug description

Pylint does not have a src folder

Led me into problems developing and test at least from time to time.

Here an exmplaination stolen why this a good idea:

## Why does PyScaffold ≥ 3 have a ``src`` folder which holds the actual Python package?
   This avoids quite many problems compared to the case when the actual Python package resides in the same folder as
   your configuration and test files.
   A nice [blog post by Ionel](https://blog.ionelmc.ro/2014/05/25/python-packaging/#the-structure) gives a thorough explanation why this is so. In a nutshell, the most severe
   problem comes from the fact that Python imports a package by first looking at the current working directory and then
   into the ``PYTHONPATH`` environment variable. If your current working directory is the root of your project directory
   you are thus not testing the installation of your package but the local package directly. Eventually, this always
   leads to huge confusion (*"But the unit tests ran perfectly on my machine!"*).

   Moreover, having a dedicated ``src`` directory to store the package files, makes it easy to comply with recent standards
   in the Python community (for example [`PEP 420`](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0420/)).

   Please notice that PyScaffold assumes all the files inside ``src`` are meant to be part of the package.


### Configuration

_No response_

### Command used

```shell
cd <other pylint dev folder> && pytest
```


### Pylint output

```shell
some strange stuff, as pytests test the pylint in the other folder while I have also pylint installed as -e from the other -venv
```

### Expected behavior

no problem because src folder prevents them

title: Performance improvement with reusing checks for attribute validity
body:
### Current problem

While triaging #4562 I noticed two occurrences of `obj.value` causes two calls to `typecheck.visit_attribute` which is a fairly substantial method.

```
val = 1 + obj.value
print(obj.value)
```

These two `visit_attribute` calls should produce the same result from the same scope, so it seems natural to cache the result.

I did a quick implementation of the general concept in the linked PR and there are some promising results.

Profiling on `pylint` current:
```
        1    0.000    0.000   22.432   22.432 __init__.py:21(run_pylint)
    12172    0.035    0.000    0.654    0.000 typecheck.py:1007(visit_attribute)
    85204    0.016    0.000    0.027    0.000 typecheck.py:1016(<genexpr>)
    12149    0.004    0.000    0.004    0.000 typecheck.py:1031(<listcomp>)
    20852    0.003    0.000    0.005    0.000 typecheck.py:1052(<genexpr>)
     5159    0.003    0.000    0.008    0.000 typecheck.py:1058(<listcomp>)
```

```
        1    0.000    0.000   22.297   22.297 __init__.py:21(run_pylint)
    12185    0.027    0.000    0.325    0.000 typecheck.py:1009(visit_attribute)
    29687    0.006    0.000    0.010    0.000 typecheck.py:1032(<genexpr>)
     4223    0.001    0.000    0.002    0.000 typecheck.py:1047(<listcomp>)
     8128    0.001    0.000    0.002    0.000 typecheck.py:1068(<genexpr>)
     1984    0.001    0.000    0.004    0.000 typecheck.py:1074(<listcomp>)
```

1.4% less run time. About 2/3 of calls are saved.

Django has a lot of attributes. Profiling Django before:
```
        1    0.000    0.000  229.853  229.853 __init__.py:21(run_pylint)
   189671    0.656    0.000   23.609    0.000 typecheck.py:1007(visit_attribute)
  1327697    0.262    0.000    0.434    0.000 typecheck.py:1016(<genexpr>)
   169909    0.052    0.000    0.061    0.000 typecheck.py:1031(<listcomp>)
   393684    0.063    0.000    0.105    0.000 typecheck.py:1052(<genexpr>)
    46297    0.030    0.000    0.066    0.000 typecheck.py:1058(<listcomp>)
     1812    0.002    0.000    3.504    0.002 typecheck.py:1113(_get_nomember_msgid_hint)
```

Profiling Django after:
```
        1    0.000    0.000  215.938  215.938 __init__.py:21(run_pylint)
   189671    0.408    0.000    7.123    0.000 typecheck.py:1009(visit_attribute)
   322385    0.070    0.000    0.115    0.000 typecheck.py:1032(<genexpr>)
    44869    0.015    0.000    0.018    0.000 typecheck.py:1047(<listcomp>)
   118548    0.020    0.000    0.033    0.000 typecheck.py:1068(<genexpr>)
     9157    0.006    0.000    0.014    0.000 typecheck.py:1074(<listcomp>)
      605    0.001    0.000    0.957    0.002 typecheck.py:1130(_get_nomember_msgid_hint)
```

7.1% less runtime. Again about 2/3 of the calls hit the cache.

The [primer test results](https://github.com/PyCQA/pylint/actions/workflows/primer-test.yaml?query=is%3Asuccess) look promising, with the most recent run at 20 minutes compared to an average closer to 23 but there's a lot of variance. A few previous commits also clocked in at ~20 minutes. Will be worth keeping an eye on as it gets run on future commits.


### Desired solution

Only one functional test, `no_member_dataclasses` fails with the mock-up implementation of this improvement because we define the same name twice across two different classes.

If this change looks sensible, we:

* Have the cache store and check against the scope to prevent this test failure. (This will probably reduce the performance improvement slightly as cache lookups become more expensive.)
* Add additional tests to try and break this cache

title: Should  ``pylint`` be equivalent to ``pylint .`` ?
body:
### Current problem

Right now pylint is displaying the help if called without argument. We could also default to the current directory i.e. ``pylint`` would be equivalent to ``pylint .``.

### Desired solution

If you want ``pylint`` to be equivalent to ``pylint .`` then react to this post with 👍, if not react with 👎. Most popular solution makes it to pylint 3.0.

### Additional context

Tools like ``flake8`` or ``pytest`` default to current directory, ``mypy`` or ``isort`` do not.

More generally, if a tool can work without argument like ``ls`` or ``pwd`` it does, if it can't because it makes no sense without arguments then it shows the help like ``mv``, or ``scp``, so I'm personally in favor as I think linting the current directory make sense, but I did not find the rational for mypy or isort to not use the current directory as default.

Original proposition done in https://github.com/PyCQA/pylint/issues/352#issuecomment-381300520, created because of https://github.com/PyCQA/pylint/pull/5682#discussion_r785763817

title: False E0237 for __slots__ and collections.abc.Iterable and Container and subtypes using TypeVar specialization.
body:
### Bug description

Consider the following example:

```python
from collections import abc
from typing import TypeVar

_T = TypeVar("_T")

class Data(abc.Iterable[_T]):
    """The _data is in slots."""

    __slots__ = {"_data"}

    def __init__(self, elements):
        """Initialize with `Data` instances."""
        self._data = list(elements)  # E0237: Assigning to attribute '_data' not defined in class slots (assigning-non-slot

class Data(abc.Iterable[int]):
    """The _data is in slots."""

    __slots__ = {"_data"}

    def __init__(self, elements):
        """Initialize with `Data` instances."""
        self._data = list(elements)

class Data(abc.Container[_T]):
    """The _data is in slots."""

    __slots__ = {"_data"}

    def __init__(self, elements):
        """Initialize with `Data` instances."""
        self._data = list(elements)  # E0237: Assigning to attribute '_data' not defined in class slots (assigning-non-slot)

class Data(abc.Container[int]):
    """The _data is in slots."""

    __slots__ = {"_data"}

    def __init__(self, elements):
        """Initialize with `Data` instances."""
        self._data = list(elements)
```

False E0237 are issued for the valid code (except that the subtypes still have abstract methods).
The false error messages are only issued when the `abc` types are specialized with a `TypeVar`. Using explicit (in this case built-in) types raises no error. Any `abc` types inheriting from `Container` and `Iterable` are also affected.

### Command used

```shell
pylint test.py
```

### Pylint output

```shell
E0237: Assigning to attribute '_data' not defined in class slots (assigning-non-slot)
E0237: Assigning to attribute '_data' not defined in class slots (assigning-non-slot)
```

### Expected behavior

No E0237 errors.

### Pylint version

```shell
pylint 2.13.0-dev0
astroid 2.9.3
Python 3.10.1 (main, Dec 18 2021, 23:53:45) [GCC 11.1.0]
```


### OS / Environment

linux 5.16.0-arch1-1

body:

```python
Foo.__foo
```

title: False positive 'undefined-variable' with assignment expression in decorator
body:
### Bug description

When using an assignment expression in a decorator, a false positive 'undefined-variable' and 'unused-variable' may be raised.

### Reproducible Example

```python
# example.py
def preprocess(bar: str) -> str:
    return bar + "xyz"

def condition1(foo):
    return foo.startswith("abc")

def condition2(foo):
    return "bcdef" in foo

def postprocess(foo):
    return foo

data = ["abcdef", "qwerty"]

def decorator(arg):
    print(f"{arg=}")
    return lambda x: x

@decorator(
    [postprocess(foo) for string in data if condition1(foo := preprocess(string)) and condition2(foo)],
)
def decorated() -> None:
    pass
```

### Pylint output

```shell
$ pylint example.py
************* Module example
tmp2.py:32:17: E0602: Undefined variable 'foo' (undefined-variable)
tmp2.py:32:97: E0602: Undefined variable 'foo' (undefined-variable)
tmp2.py:32:55: W0612: Unused variable 'foo' (unused-variable)

------------------------------------------------------------------
Your code has been rated at 3.12/10 (previous run: 3.12/10, +0.00)

$ # Demonstrating that the python code runs as expected:
$ python example.py
arg=['abcdefxyz']
```


### Expected behavior

No errors

title: False positive invalid-overridden-method for async generators overriding AsyncIterable
body:
### Bug description

For classes 

    class Foo(Protocol):
        def bar() -> AsyncIterable[str]:
            pass

    class Foo2(Foo):
        async def bar() -> AsyncIterable[str]:
            yield "hello"

Pylint will give a false positive:

```
W0236: Method 'bar' was expected to be 'non-async', found it instead as 'async' (invalid-overridden-method)
```

That's incorrect because the `yield` has a subtly different effect for async methods.  An async generator method (such as `Foo2.bar()`) are not coroutines.  If you call them without `await` you get an `AsyncIterable` not a `Coroutine`.

That is, to use an async generator you call:

```python
foo = Foo2()
async for b in foo.bar():
    # ...
```

You do not call:

```python
foo = Foo2()
async for b in await foo.bar():
    # ...
```

### Configuration

_No response_

### Command used

```shell
pylint source
```


### Pylint output

```shell
W0236: Method 'bar' was expected to be 'non-async', found it instead as 'async' (invalid-overridden-method)
```


### Expected behavior

(If at all possible) pylint should detect the `yield` in an async method and interpret it as a a non-async method.

### Pylint version

```shell
pylint 2.12.2
astroid 2.9.3
Python 3.9.6 (v3.9.6:db3ff76da1, Jun 28 2021, 11:49:53)
```


### OS / Environment

_No response_

### Additional dependencies

_No response_

title: feature suggestion for `is` compare between different types
body:
### Current problem

Comparing two different types using the `is` operator is always `False`.

For example for this code:

```
A = 5
STR1 = ‘5’
if A is STR1:
	pass
```

`A` is of type `int` and `STR1` is of type `str`.
`is` compare between those types is always `False`.

The user should be alert about it, and consider modifying his code.

As far as I checked there is no checker that checks that..


### Desired solution

I suggest a new checker (or extension of an existing one) that raises a message for this case.

message name suggestion: `is-different-types` or `different-types-is-expression`

message type: `Warning`

message content suggestion:

`An 'is' comparison between different types always fails, consider using casting`

### Additional context

The check should be general to any comparison/expression, not necessarily an `if` comparison.
I think we should raise a message even for cases like:

`condition = '5' is 4`

I thought about extending the checker to a `==` operator, but I am not really fond of the idea and I will explain why.

1. The `__eq__` of Python classes can be modified to return `True` for different comparison values.

2. There is a (stupid) case where `==` actually returns `True` for different types. it happens for `int` and `float`, e.g. `1.0==1`.
[note that the statement `1.0 is 1` returns `False`, as `1.0` is of type `float` and `1` is of type `int`]
[also note that this rare case only happens for small numbers]

title: False negative `used-before-assignment` after name defined and used in except
body:
### Bug description

This doesn't warn as it should:
```python
try:
  pass
except ValueError:
  msg = 'ha'
  print(msg)
```

But this warns just fine:
```python
try:
  pass
except ValueError:

print(msg)  # [used-before-assignment]
```

So it's an issue with "consuming" the name too early in the variables checker when used by the first "print(msg)".

title: False positive for ``unexpected-keyword-arg`` for decorators with inner inner function
body:
### Bug description

Just tried this out with the latest version of the main branch (any version after bfeca4c) and there seems to be a case where #5547 is still raising a false positive unexpected-keyword-arg error.

```python
def example_decorator(*new_func_args, **new_func_kwargs):
    def decorator(func):
        def wrapper(*args, **kwargs):
            print_me = kwargs.pop("print_me", None)
            def inner(*inner_args, **inner_kwargs):
                print(print_me)
                return func(*inner_args, **inner_kwargs)

            return inner(*args, **kwargs)

        return wrapper

    if len(new_func_args) == 1 and len(new_func_kwargs) == 0 and callable(new_func_args[0]):
        return decorator(new_func_args[0])
    return decorator

@example_decorator
def example_func(num):
    return num ** 2

example_func(6, print_me="hello")
```

(Note that no error is raised by pylint if we remove the top-level function (`example_decorator`). The reason for someone to define the decorator this way is so that it's possible to use the bare `@example_decorator`, as well as with arguments `@example_decorator(foo=1)`.)

Using pylint on commit hash e75e37a2908ef1c003ea5816c1e234523bdd38be, this is the error that's printed out:

```
E1123: Unexpected keyword argument 'print_me' in function call (unexpected-keyword-arg)
```

Calling `example_func` works fine:

```
>>> example_task(6, print_me="hello")
hello
36
```

### Command used

```shell
pylint a.py
```


### Pylint output

```shell
************* Module a
E1123: Unexpected keyword argument 'print_me' in function call (unexpected-keyword-arg)
```


### Expected behavior

No `unexpected-keyword-arg` error raised

### Pylint version

```shell
current pylint version in the `main` branch (set for 2.13.0)
```

title: Emit a message for reliance on variable annotations never initialized in `__init__` or `__new__`
body:
### Current problem

This pattern emits no messages:
```python
class PossibleNoMember:
    def __init__(self):
        self.member: bool

    def set_member(self, member):
        self.member = member  # this should be set in `__init__` or `__new__`

    def depend_on_member(self):
        print(self.member)
```

### Desired solution

Should raise either `maybe-no-member` if we're being cautious, or if we can actually check `__init__` _and_ `__new__`, then we might actually raise `no-member`. EDIT: ah, but a static analyzer is going to have a _rough go of it_ trying to do the latter. For example, in the above, put a call to `set_member()` under `__init__()`.

### Additional context

See discussion at

https://github.com/PyCQA/pylint/pull/5544#issuecomment-1038768505
#2945
#3167 (fixed by #4194)

From PEP 526:
"In particular, the value-less notation a: int allows one to annotate instance variables that should be initialized in __init__ or __new__."

If a user feels strongly about initializing these variables outside `__init__` or `__new__`, I think a disable is proper. The question being whether to create a new message they could disable globally. (EDIT: probably a new message.)

title: How to fix pylint E1134: Non-mapping value X is used in a mapping context (not-a-mapping)
body:
### Question

How to fix pylint E1134: Non-mapping value X is used in a mapping context (not-a-mapping)

```python
from dataclasses import dataclass
@dataclass
class One:
    f_one: str
    f_two: str

@dataclass
class Two:
    f_three: str
    f_four: One
    def __post_init__(self):
        """Initialise nested dataclass fields from a dictionary"""
        self.f_four = One(**self.f_four)

data = {'f_three': 'three', 'f_four': {'f_one': 'one', 'f_two': 'two'}}

print(Two(**data)) # this line is where it complaints
```

### Documentation for future user

Maybe an example in the documentation since this isnt that obvious in my opinion and I was using 2.9.3 and upgraded to 2.12.2 and this suddenly became an error.

### Additional context

For more details please see 


https://stackoverflow.com/questions/71118967/how-to-fix-pylint-e1134-non-mapping-value-x-is-used-in-a-mapping-context-not-a

title: Check for circular comparisons and other comparison improvements
body:
### Current problem

```python
a = 1
b = 2

if a > b and b > a:
    pass
```

`compare.py:4:3: R1716: Simplify chained comparison between the operands (chained-comparison)`

While it's nice some message that points to the error, it's not quite correct.

### Desired solution

Detect circular comparisons which simplify to `False`.

This is a graph pattern is solved with a topological sort. The main risks are the performance overhead for building the graph, which should be small because the number of statements in any given `if` is usually low. There is also the complexity risk of having a graph and associated algorithms implementation embedded in the code.

Having the graph could open up the following:

Could see if we can suggest simplifying `if a >= b and b <= a:` to `if a == b:` but this could be more difficult. There might also be something we can do with `or` statements.

Building the graph would also allow us to pick up situations like 
```
    elif a > 1 and a > 10:
        pass
    elif a < 100 and a < 10:
        pass
```
which currently do not emit.

This issue came up while thinking about #5800. It I'm not immediately sure how to suggest the refactor with the current implementation. Building this graph might more readily suggests the refactor.

We could modify `chained-comparison` to detect comparison cycles and do #5800. But unless there are objections I want to try the graph implementation because of the benefits described above.

title: super-with-arguments should not be shown for dataclasses with slots
body:
### Bug description

Python 3.10 adds `slots=True` to dataclass decorators. The documentation describes it as such (emphasis mine):
> slots: If true (the default is False), `__slots__` attribute will be generated and **new class will be returned instead of the original one**. If `__slots__` is already defined in the class, then TypeError is raised.

The fact that it returns a new class breaks argument-less super, as shown in this example:

```python
"""Quick test showing that dataclasses with slots break argument-less super()
and hence pylint should not suggest using it."""
from dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass(slots=True)
class _Animal:
    """Base class for animals."""
    name: str
    num_arms: int
    num_legs: int

    def greet(self):
        """Simple method to use all fields in this dataclass."""
        print(f'Hi, my name is {self.name} and I have {self.num_arms} arms '
              f'and {self.num_legs} legs.')

@dataclass(slots=True)
class Dog(_Animal):
    """Dataclass implementing a dog."""
    num_arms: int = 0
    num_legs: int = 4

    def greet(self):
        print('Woof!')
        # This works
        super(Dog, self).greet()

@dataclass(slots=True)
class Bird(_Animal):
    """Dataclass implementing a bird."""
    num_arms: int = 0
    num_legs: int = 2

    def greet(self):
        print('Chirp!')
        # This does *not* work
        super().greet()

Dog('Snoopy').greet()
# This will raise a TypeError, see Bird.greet above
Bird('Blu').greet()
```

`Bird` implements pylint's suggestion to use argument-less super,  `Dog` does not. Running the file:
```
$ python dc_slots.py
Woof!
Hi, my name is Snoopy and I have 0 arms and 4 legs.
Chirp!
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/tmp/dc_slots.py", line 40, in <module>
    Bird('Blu').greet()
  File "/tmp/dc_slots.py", line 37, in greet
    super().greet()
TypeError: super(type, obj): obj must be an instance or subtype of type
```

### Configuration

_No response_

### Command used

```shell
python -m pylint dc_slots.py
```


### Pylint output

```shell
************* Module dc_slots
dc_slots.py:26:8: R1725: Consider using Python 3 style super() without arguments (super-with-arguments)

------------------------------------------------------------------
Your code has been rated at 9.52/10 (previous run: 9.52/10, +0.00)
```


### Expected behavior

pylint should not be suggesting an argument-less super call in dataclasses that have `slots=True`.

### Pylint version
