```

title: Improved handling of fixme
body:
### Current problem

A common policy is that TODO comments are not allowed in code unless they reference an existing issue. Now to fully validate this is out of scope, but it should be _relatively_ easy to implement a check using a regex. However, the implementation today is not obvious and kind of a hack.

For example, if a valid TODO is
`# TODO (Issue 40): This bird is no more`
And an invalid TODO is
 `# TODO: This bird is no more`

Today you would implement this similarly to

```yaml
[tool.pylint.miscellaneous]
# List of note tags to take in consideration, separated by a comma
notes=["SomethingThatWillNotBeFound"]

# Regular expression of note tags to take in consideration.
notes-rgx="TODO(?! \\(Issue \\d+\\))"
```

`notes` has to have something that won't be found because, if it is left empty, it won't find anything and if left as the default, it will flag all the TODO lines.



### Desired solution

I'm not sure what the ideal solution is. I can think of 3 possible solutions:

1. When `notes` is empty, don't use it to build the regex
2. When `notes-rgx` is given, ignore `notes`
3. Add an additional regex or setting that can be used to to detect this specific case. Ex: `ignore-notes-with-issue`, `notes-issue-rgx`

Clearly, `1` is the easiest to implement and should probably be done regardless.
`2` is a breaking change, but would probably be more intuitive.
`3` really depends on how common a use case this is. For me, it's been the policy at most places I've worked and we ended up disabling this check and rely on code reviews to catch it. It was only recently I thought about tweaking the check.

### Additional context

_No response_

title: Missing Member for Pydantic Model
body:
### Bug description

hey team, I'm running into:
```E1101: Instance of 'Response' has no 'versionInfo' member (no-member)```
Provided the following code:
```
☁  test_py  cat main.py 
from response import Response
from run import f

r = f()
r.versionInfo = r.versionInfo | {"hi":"bye"}
print(r)%                                                                                                                                                                                     ☁  test_py  cat response.py 
from pydantic import BaseModel


class Response(BaseModel):
    versionInfo: dict[str, str]
    fm: dict[int, float]
☁  test_py  cat run.py 
from response import Response

def f() -> Response:
  return Response(verionInfo={}, fm={})%
```
Running pylint on `main.py` I get the following:
```main.py:5:16: E1101: Instance of 'Response' has no 'versionInfo' member (no-member)```

However, `versionInfo` is defined in the `Response` class.

I've got a theory for what's wrong and a work around. Specifically, I think it has something to do with how the `BaseModel` in pydantic isn't defining the fields using self / something more python native. When I replace the `BaseModel` with a normal class and define the fields with `self` linting passes.

Curious what the team thought should be done here. It's not strictly a bug given it's related to a separate dependency in pydantic.

title: run_pylint signature
body:
### Question

The `run_pylint` function [is documented](https://pylint.readthedocs.io/en/v2.17.4/development_guide/api/index.html) with a variadic signature:

```python
run_pylint("--disable=C", "myfile.py")
```

But the function actually [takes one argument](https://github.com/pylint-dev/pylint/blob/v2.17.4/pylint/__init__.py#L28).


### Documentation for future user

It should say:

```python
run_pylint(["--disable=C", "myfile.py"])
```

### Additional context

_No response_

title: No warning about using * operator on sequence types containing objects
body:
### Bug description

This is not so much a bug but a missing check, perhaps.

Given the following file called 'test.py'

```
a = [[]]*2
print(a[0] is a[1])
b = [[],[]]
print(b[0] is b[1])
```

The output is:

```
$ python test.py 
True
False
```

I recently had a bug which because I did not realise that using `*2` would create a copy of the pointer to the initial sub-list, instead of creating a new list. Pylint does not detect this.

I'm not sure what this error message would be called or the exact phrasing.

### Configuration

_No response_

### Command used

```shell
pylint test.py
```


### Pylint output

```shell
************* Module test
test.py:5:0: C0305: Trailing newlines (trailing-newlines)
test.py:1:0: C0114: Missing module docstring (missing-module-docstring)

------------------------------------------------------------------
Your code has been rated at 5.00/10 (previous run: 0.00/10, +5.00)
```


### Expected behavior

Ideally, pylint would warn that using `*` on a sequence type containing objects does not create new objects but instead refers to the same object. In [the documentation](https://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html#sequence-types-list-tuple-range):

`s * n or n * s` is "equivalent to adding s to itself n times" - which significantly means the list has the same element added to itself multiple times, This does not matter for primitives but for objects it does.

### Pylint version

```shell
$ pylint --version
pylint 2.17.4
astroid 2.15.5
Python 3.11.0 (main, Oct 25 2022, 13:54:35) [GCC 9.4.0]
```


### OS / Environment

_No response_

_No response_

title: logging-fstring-interpolation not raised on function parameter hinted as logging.Logger
body:
### Bug description

If I log to the root logger directly or if within a scope I call `logging.getLogger` and then log to that `Logger`, I will get the `logging-fstring-interpolation` warning, but if I have a function parameter that is hinted as a `logging.Logger`, I will not get the `logging-fstring-interpolation` warning on that logger:

```python
"""A demonstration of a missing logging-fstring-interpolation warning."""
import logging

MSG = "we used an f-string in a logging statement"
logging.error(f"Something bad happened: {MSG}")

file_logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)
file_logger.error(f"Something bad happened: {MSG}")


def my_function(logger: logging.Logger) -> None:
    """A function that accepts a logger."""
    logging.error(f"Something bad happened: {MSG}")

    function_logger = logging.getLogger(my_function.__name__)
    function_logger.error(f"Something bad happened: {MSG}")

    logger.error(f"Something bad happened: {MSG}")  # no logging-fstring-interpolation warning


class MyClass:  # pylint: disable=too-few-public-methods
    """A class with a method that accepts a logger."""

    def __init__(self, logger: logging.Logger) -> None:
        logging.error(f"Something bad happened: {MSG}")

        class_logger = logging.getLogger(MyClass.__name__)
        class_logger.error(f"Something bad happened: {MSG}")

        logger.error(f"Something bad happened: {MSG}")  # no logging-fstring-interpolation warning

```

### Configuration

N/A

title: Use inference to determine if **kwargs is missing a named parameter
body:
I would expect these two lines to raise the same set of messages. We don't use inference to analyze whether **kwargs contains the missing named parameter `x`. We could.

```python
import copy
import os

copy.copy(**{"y": os.environ})  # [unexpected-keyword-arg]
copy.copy(y=os.environ)  # [no-value-for-parameter, unexpected-keyword-arg]
```

False negative for `no-value-for-parameter`.

title: Add configurable suppressions for classes based on inheritance (for use with `typing.Protocol`, `typing.NamedTuple`, `dataclass`, etc.)
body:
## Why?

It would resolve high priority issue #3732, among many others. Pylint complaints on classes that are primarily used with type hinting or with data containers are not valid. It is very tedious to disable multiple checks such as too few public methods manually on all of these classes.  (Not to mention preferences with docstrings for code meant just for typing and that should occupy as little screen real estate as possible).

Opening up this more generic issue because the other one is too specific, but there are more uses for inheritance-relationship or class-name based suppression.

## Request

Add a way to configure disables for a category of classes based on inheritance-relationship or class-name. 

### Features 

Ideally, the classes to customize can be specified by name, metaclass, and parent class.


See original comment by @earonesty : 
        
> Configurable suppressions for classes would be generally useful:
> 
> Simple config override in .pylintrc for classes that:
> 
> \# [CLASSRULES.<CUSTOM_NAME>]
> \# metaclass_match=<metaclass_regex>
> \# parent_match=<parent_regex>
> \# disable=list,of,disables
> 
> Would solve a lot of these issues.

_Originally posted by @earonesty in https://github.com/pylint-dev/pylint/issues/3732#issuecomment-751374533_

```python

```

### Desired solution

A new rule `consider-universal-pathing` that detects when someone forgets to use `pathlib` or `os.path.join` for pathing.

title: ``singleton comparison`` - ==/is true message improvement
body:
### Current problem

I was wondering about the ``singleton-comparison`` checker I worked on a bit a while ago.
it suggests you to compare singleton using `is` instead of `==` which is fine, for example for `none`.
But why should we use it with `True` and `False`? since comparison returns a Boolean anyway.
 

### Desired solution

For the following code:

`if expr == True` or `if expr is True`

The checker should suggest to modify it to `if expr`.

for false, both `if expr == False` and `if expr is False`  should be modified to `if not expr`.

### Additional context

There is some controversy about  this matter as can be seen in this thread:
https://stackoverflow.com/questions/27276610/boolean-identity-true-vs-is-true

Since it assumes an object has its `__bool__` implemented although I believe that this is most probably the case in any class, since it comes with a default method that returns `True`.
See this thread for some (maybe engineered) corner cases:
https://stackoverflow.com/questions/64913140/is-there-a-way-to-remove-bool-method-from-a-class

title: `no-member` emitted for all `zipimport` names
body:
### Bug description

example.py:
```python
import zipimport

print(zipimport.ZipImportError)
```

[zipimport source](https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/0e24499129f3917b199a6d46fa33eeedd2c447fc/Lib/zipimport.py). 

Compare to [zipapp.ZipAppError](https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/0e24499129f3917b199a6d46fa33eeedd2c447fc/Lib/zipapp.py#L33) which has a similar definition to ZipImportError but is working fine:

```python
import zipapp
print(zipapp.ZipAppError)
```

### Configuration

_No response_

### Command used

```shell
pylint example.py
```


### Pylint output

```shell
example.py:3:6: E1101: Module 'zipimport' has no 'ZipImportError' member (no-member)
```


### Expected behavior

No error message emitted.

### Pylint version

```shell
pylint 3.0.0b1
astroid 3.0.0a6-dev0
Python 3.10.4
```


### OS / Environment

_No response_

### Additional dependencies

_No response_

title: `disable=too-many-statements` sometimes not working
body:
### Bug description

The `too-many-statements` error can't be disabled in **Line 27**, though this error is disabled in whole file and inline. 
This error is correctly suppressed in **Line 35**.
I've read #7625 and #6042, but I can't find a solution for this.

``` python
def load_arguments(self, _):    # pylint: disable=too-many-statements, too-many-locals
```

This error happens when upgrade pylint from 2.11 to 2.17.

Related issue: https://github.com/Azure/azure-cli/pull/26685

### Configuration

_No response_

### Command used

```shell
git clone --depth=1 --branch 2.50.0 https://github.com/Azure/azure-cli  && cd azure-cli
pylint ./src/azure-cli/azure/cli/command_modules/rdbms/_params.py --disable R,C,W,E --enable=R0915

(Sorry, I can't find an easy way to repro this error in new file)
```


### Pylint output

```shell
************* Module azure.cli.command_modules.rdbms._params
src/azure-cli/azure/cli/command_modules/rdbms/_params.py:27:0: R0915: Too many statements (434/50) (too-many-statements)

------------------------------------------------------------------
Your code has been rated at 9.98/10 (previous run: 9.42/10, +0.55)
```


### Expected behavior

Line27 does not raise error

### Pylint version

```shell
pylint 2.17.4
astroid 2.15.5
Python 3.10.8 (tags/v3.10.8:aaaf517, Oct 11 2022, 16:50:30) [MSC v.1933 64 bit (AMD64)]
```


### OS / Environment

Windows 11

### Additional dependencies

_No response_

title: unused-import false positive when using type union in annotations
body:
### Bug description

When using string annotation, the string should encompass the whole annotation, which is needed for forward referencing. I have not found a workaround over this issue.

Example code:
```py
# a.py

# pylint: disable=missing-docstring
from typing import Literal


def false_unused_imports() -> "int | Literal[True]":
    return True


# def annotation_error() -> int | "Literal[True]":
#     return True
```

### Configuration

_No response_

### Command used

```shell
pylint a.py
```


### Pylint output

```shell
************* Module a.py:4:0: W0611: Unused Literal imported from typing (unused-import)

------------------------------------------------------------------
Your code has been rated at 6.67/10 (previous run: 6.67/10, +0.00)
```


### Expected behavior

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Your code has been rated at 10.00/10 (previous run: 10.00/10, +0.00)

`(no error is found)`

### Pylint version

```shell
pylint 2.17.4
astroid 2.15.5
Python 3.11.4 (tags/v3.11.4:d2340ef, Jun  7 2023, 05:45:37) [MSC v.1934 64 bit (AMD64)]
```

title: wrong-import-order fails on one system, passes in other

### Configuration

Configuration is identical in both systems:


```ini

ignore = "CVS"
ignore-patterns = "kedro/templates/*"
load-plugins = [
    "pylint.extensions.docparams",

]
extension-pkg-whitelist = "cv2"
unsafe-load-any-extension = false
[tool.pylint.messages_control]
disable = [
    "ungrouped-imports",
    "duplicate-code"
]
enable = ["useless-suppression"]
[tool.pylint.refactoring]
max-nested-blocks = 5
[tool.pylint.format]
indent-after-paren=4
indent-string="    "

]

title: Option to warn when logging format string is not a string literal
body:
### Current problem

One may write working code, something akin to

```py
errors = f"{sys.argv[1]} some-error %s"
logging.error(errors, context)
```

but if argv[1] ever contains a `%s` of its own (e.g. `file%s.bar`), a runtime exception is raised:

```
TypeError: not enough arguments for format string
Call stack:
    logging.error(errors, context)
Message: 'one%s some-error %s'
```

The code could be written as

```py
logging.error("%s some-error %s, sys.argv[1], context)
```

or

```py
errors = f"{sys.argv[1]} some-error {context}"
logging.error(errors)
```

or (better?)

```py
errors = f"{sys.argv[1]} some-error {context}"
logging.error("%s", errors)
```

There may be a debate about whether the second example should _also_ trip a lint. `errors` contains format specifiers, but if `logging.error` is invoked with only a single argument, it seems "safe": no TypeError is raised in this case. However, gcc's `-Wformat-nonliteral` (which this would be similar to) would certainly complain.

So the case where logging methods are called with only a single argument may be exempt from this lint, or opt-in -- seems more of a stylistic preference.

But if that's the case, the example is admittedly a little contrived.. It's odd to separate the format specifiers into the `errors` variable. And once the substitutions move to the argument to `logging.error`, they will trip the `logging-not-lazy` lint which already exists.

So maybe nothing is needed. Or maybe it's worth encouraging `logging.error("%s", errors)` over `logging.error(errors)` for the single-argument case for consistency.

### Desired solution

A new lint. E.g., `logging-format-not-literal`

### Additional context

_No response_

title: False positive `R0903: Too few public methods (0/1)` when using generics and shim imports
body:
### Bug description

My module does a conditional import on another package for backwards compatibility reasons:

```python
try:
    from package.main import Class_A as ClassA
except ImportError:
    from package.main import ClassA
```

and then creates a derived class:

```python
class ClassB(ClassA[int]):
    """Derived class"""
```

and this produces an error (see below).

I notice that when not attempting to use the generics part everything works fine:

```python
class ClassB(ClassA):
    """Derived class"""
```

Complete source code: https://gist.github.com/astrojuanlu/421e78b189a78504e827aac5f2a4c92e

```
> tree -a -L 1
.
├── .git
├── .gitignore
├── .pylintrc
├── .venv
└── package
> tree 
.
└── package
    ├── __init__.py
    ├── derived.py
    └── main.py

2 directories, 3 files
```

### Configuration

```ini
[DESIGN]
min-public-methods=1
```


### Command used

```shell
pylint package
```


### Pylint output

```shell
************* Module package.derived
package/derived.py:11:0: R0903: Too few public methods (0/1) (too-few-public-methods)

-------------------------------------------------------------------
Your code has been rated at 9.17/10 (previous run: 10.00/10, -0.83)

title: R6003 does not apply to "quick" top-level type definition
body:
### Bug description

For Python 3.9,

code
```py
# pylint: disable=consider-alternative-union-syntax
PlotlyColorScale = Union[list[str], list[tuple[Union[float, int], str]]]
```

Used as return type
```py
def _get_coloring() -> tuple[PlotlyColorScale, float | None]:
```

Suggestion https://pylint.readthedocs.io/en/stable/user_guide/messages/refactor/consider-alternative-union-syntax.html

interpreted as
```py
PlotlyColorScale: (list[str], list[tuple[(float, int), str]])
PlotlyColorScale = (list[str], list[tuple[(float, int), str]])
```

Examples should be updated, or the warning silenced

title: missing await and/or unused coroutine
body:
### Current problem

Currently pylint does not warn if there is a missing `await` / unused coroutine, eg:
```python
import asyncio

async def nested() -> None:
    print("first()")

async def fn() -> None:
    nested()  # <-  could this issue a warning please
    await asyncio.create_task(nested())
    await asyncio.gather(nested())
    await asyncio.ensure_future(nested())

async def main() -> None:
    await fn()

asyncio.run(main())
```

Slightly related to https://github.com/pylint-dev/pylint/issues/654

### Desired solution

It would be really cool if a call on awaitable without using future triggered a warning.

### Additional context


MS pyright: `error: Result of async function call is not used; use "await" or assign result to variable (reportUnusedCoroutine)`

mypy: 
 `error: Value of type "Coroutine[Any, Any, None]" must be used  [unused-coroutine]`
`note: Are you missing an await?`

title: False positive `E1101` when inheriting from tuple and using sorted function inside __new__ dunder
body:
### Bug description

When inheriting from tuple and using `sorted` function, any added attributes aren't recognized anymore by pylint
```py
# pylint: disable=missing-module-docstring
# pylint: disable=missing-class-docstring
# pylint: disable=missing-function-docstring


class Foo(tuple):

    def __new__(cls, iterable) -> 'Foo':
        instance = super().__new__(cls, iterable)

        instance._x = 0
        instance.y = 1
        return instance

    @property
    def x(self):
        return self._x


my_foo = Foo(("c", "b", "a"))
print(my_foo.x, my_foo.y)


class Bar(tuple):

    def __new__(cls, iterable) -> 'Bar':
        instance = super().__new__(cls, sorted(iterable))

        instance._x = 0
        instance.y = 1
        return instance

    @property
    def x(self):
        return self._x


my_bar = Bar(("c", "b", "a"))
print(my_bar.x, my_bar.y)

```


### Configuration

_No response_

### Command used

```shell
pylint test.py
```


### Pylint output

```shell
************* Module test
test.py:35:15: E1101: Instance of 'Bar' has no '_x' member; maybe 'x'? (no-member)
test.py:39:16: E1101: Instance of 'Bar' has no 'y' member; maybe 'x'? (no-member)

------------------------------------------------------------------
Your code has been rated at 5.00/10 (previous run: 5.00/10, +0.00)
```


### Expected behavior

No errors should be reported

title: Issues with `invalid-name`
body:
### Bug description

I believe that if I disable pylint warnings for `invalid-name` where I declare a class level attribute, that should suppress errors where I use that attribute. 
```python

"""
This class shows a bug in pylint.
"""


class Base:
    """
    This is where the invalid-name field is defined.
    """

    # pylint: disable=invalid-name
    thisIsABadField: str
    # pylint: enable=invalid-name

    def __init__(self, greeting: str):
        print(greeting)

    def do_something(self):
        self.thisIsABadField = "hello"

    def do_another(self):
        self.thisIsABadField = "hello again"
```


### Configuration

```ini
[BASIC]
class-attribute-naming-style=snake_case
```


### Command used

```shell
pylint path/to/file.py
```


### Pylint output

```shell
************* Module example.test
example/test.py:19:8: C0103: Attribute name "thisIsABadField" doesn't conform to snake_case naming style (invalid-name)
```


### Expected behavior

I believe that if I disable pylint warnings for `invalid-name` where I declare a class level attribute, that should suppress all errors where I use that attribute. In this case I am provided an error for accessing the attribute in my `do_something()`, however I am not provided an error in my `do_another()`, which seems oddly strange because if the error is already getting shown for one of these it probably should have been for both. Regardless, I would expect no errors to be shown if I disable the invalid name error where I declared the attribute.

### Pylint version

```shell
pylint 2.17.4
astroid 2.15.6
Python 3.10.12 (main, Jun  7 2023, 12:45:35) [GCC 9.4.0]
```


### OS / Environment

Ubuntu 20.04

### Additional dependencies

_No response_

title: Redefined outer name not detected across modules when parallelization used
body:
### Bug description

When I enable parallelization (using the `-j 0` command line option), pylint no longer emits a `redefined-outer-name` warning. It will emit the warning if parallelization is disabled or if `-j 1` is used.

The files below demonstrate the issue. In this case, `test_demo()` defines `demo.some_redefined_variable`, but the variable is shadowed in `demo.main()`.

demo.py:

```
"""Module that redefines a name defined
in an outer scope
"""

def main():
    """This function redefines some_redefined_variable"""
    some_redefined_variable = "something else"
    print(f"{some_redefined_variable}")
```

test_demo.py:

```
"""Test module that defines a name that will be redefined
in the demo module.
"""
import demo

def test_demo():
    """Test function that defines the outer name"""
    demo.some_redefined_variable = "something"
    demo.main()
```

The two conflicting outputs are below:

```
$ pylint -j 1 demo.py test_demo.py 
************* Module demo
demo.py:7:4: W0621: Redefining name 'some_redefined_variable' from outer scope (line 8) (redefined-outer-name)

-------------------------------------------------------------------
Your code has been rated at 8.57/10 (previous run: 10.00/10, -1.43)

$ pylint -j 0 demo.py test_demo.py 

-------------------------------------------------------------------
Your code has been rated at 10.00/10 (previous run: 8.57/10, +1.43)
```

### Configuration

_No response_

### Command used

```shell
pylint -j 0 demo.py test_demo.py
```


### Pylint output

```shell
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Your code has been rated at 10.00/10 (previous run: 10.00/10, +0.00)
```


### Expected behavior

I expect the warning to be emitted, as with the non-parallelized (`-j 1`) case. Specifically:

```
************* Module demo
demo.py:7:4: W0621: Redefining name 'some_redefined_variable' from outer scope (line 8) (redefined-outer-name)
```

### Pylint version

```shell
pylint 2.17.4
astroid 2.15.6
Python 3.10.6 (main, May 29 2023, 11:10:38) [GCC 11.3.0]
```


### OS / Environment

Ubuntu 22.04

### Additional dependencies

_No response_

title: Improve similarities checker by considering relative indentation of code blocks rather than absolute indentation
body:
### Current problem

Currently, the following is not caught as duplicate code.  This is sanitized from a production codebase I work on.

```
if cond:
    stmt_1
    ...
    stmt_n
    return
stmt_1
...
stmt_n
```

### Desired solution

I would expect R0801 to be flagged for these two code segments.

### Additional context

Distinct but possibly related to https://github.com/pylint-dev/pylint/issues/1457 .  This seems to be an issue with the fact that for comparing code fragments, it does so using the absolute indentation level, and not converting each code segment to use a relative indentation level.  Now, in order to make this change it does cause a considerable increase in algorithmic complexity and would require the current hash based approach to actually use a number of different hashes per line, one per indentation level.  However, I have found it much easier to argue for better coding when a tool says that code should be changed than when I say it.

title: Unignorable "invalid-name" when accessing "private" members
body:
### Bug description

Given are two files main.py and server.py in a module "mod".

test.py contains the unit tests for server.py and accesses a "private" member of the class Server.
This access leads to "invalid-name" but can not be ignored by adding disable statements:

test.py:

```python
#pylint: disable=protected-access, missing-module-docstring, missing-function-docstring
#pylint: disable=missing-class-docstring, too-few-public-methods
import mod.server

class TestServer():

    def test_server(self):
        # pylint: disable=invalid-name
        server = mod.server.Server()
        server._Server__dlt_socket = 1 # pylint: disable=invalid-name

```

server.py:
```python
#pylint: disable=missing-module-docstring, missing-function-docstring
#pylint: disable=missing-class-docstring, too-few-public-methods, unused-private-member

class Server():
    def __init__(self):
        self.__dlt_socket = False

```
### Command used

```shell
pylint mod/
```


### Pylint output

```shell
************* Module mod.test
mod\test.py:9:8: C0103: Attribute name "_Server__dlt_socket" doesn't conform to snake_case naming style (invalid-name)
```


### Expected behavior

Expected behavior is to at least be able to ignore the message.

### Pylint version

```shell
pylint 2.17.4
astroid 2.15.6
Python 3.10.6 (tags/v3.10.6:9c7b4bd, Aug  1 2022, 21:53:49) [MSC v.1932 64 bit (AMD64)]

title: False positive: unneeded not
body:
### Bug description

```python
a = {1: 1}
b = {2: 2}
if not a.keys() < b.keys():
    print("!")
```

### Configuration

_No response_

### Command used

```shell
pylint a.py
```


### Pylint output

```shell
************* Module a
a.py:3:3: C0113: Consider changing "not a.keys() < b.keys()" to "a.keys() >= b.keys()" (unneeded-not)

title: false positive `no-member` warnings in pydantic 2.0
body:
### Bug description

### Description

I'm using `pydantic.v1` to transition our codebase to pydantic 2.* but doing so has resulted in many pylint warnings due to the use of `pydantic.v1.Field()` in defining the fields of my class.

```
x : int = pydantic.v1.Field(...)`
```

it seems that pylint interprets `self.x` as a `ModelField` instead of an `int` and therefore creates many warnings throughout the entire codebase.

Note that this doesn't occur if I leave out the `pd.Field()` definition.

```
x : int
```

Any thoughts on how to get around this issue would be really appreciated! Code example below showing the issue.  

(I am running pylint-2.17.5 and Python 3.10.9)

Thanks

### Code Snippet

```py
""" module docstring """
import pydantic.v1 as pydantic


class ClassA(pydantic.BaseModel):
    """Class A"""

    field_a : float


class ClassB(pydantic.BaseModel):
    """Class B"""

    # doesnt trigger pylint
    obj_a: ClassA

    # triggers Instance of 'FieldInfo' has no 'field_a' member (no-member) from below method
    obj_a: ClassA = pydantic.Field(...)

    def get_field_a(self):
        """Grab the 'field_a' from 'self.obj_a'."""
        return self.obj_a.field_a

# simple test to show that the method works as expected
FIELD_A = 1.0
obj_a = ClassA(field_a=FIELD_A)
obj_b = ClassB(obj_a=obj_a)
assert obj_b.get_field_a() == FIELD_A
```

### Python, Pydantic & OS Version

title: False positive attribute-defined-outside-init when setting variant field
body:
### Bug description

```python
class MessageVariant:
    message: str

    def __init__(self, message: str) -> None:
        self.message = message


class OtherVariant:
    pass


def parse(operation: str) -> MessageVariant | OtherVariant:
    if operation != "":
        return MessageVariant(operation)
    return OtherVariant()


def edit_messages(operation: str) -> None:
    variant = parse(operation)
    if isinstance(variant, MessageVariant):
        # W0201: Attribute 'message' defined outside __init__ (attribute-defined-outside-init)
        variant.message = ""
```

title: `E1101 (no-member)` for wrong class with for partially implemented abstract `classmethod`?
body:
### Bug description

Hi, I feel like this a false-positive from Pylint when accessing a property from a class inheriting from an abstract class with a partially implemented abstract `classmethod`. Specifically, I think it's something to do with the `cls()` call in the abstract implementation of `from_dict`.

```py
"""pylint test"""

import abc


class Animal(abc.ABC):
    """Animal class"""

    @property
    def name(self) -> str:
        """Animal name"""

    @classmethod
    @abc.abstractmethod
    def from_dict(cls, animal_dict: dict):
        """Parse an Animal object from a dict"""
        return cls()


class Dog(Animal):
    """Dog class"""

    name = "dog"
    barks = False

    @classmethod
    def from_dict(cls, animal_dict):
        new_dog = super().from_dict(animal_dict)
        new_dog.barks = animal_dict.get("barks")

        return new_dog


dog = Dog.from_dict({"barks": True})

print(dog.name)
print(dog.barks)

```

`python test.py`
```
dog
True
```

title: Potential lazy regex in default value if badly edited in the future + useless part
body:
### Bug description

In the default config file, for the `ignore-long-lines` regex, where the default value is `^\s*(# )?<?https?://\S+>?$`.

If someone decide to modifie this value, it is really easy to create a lazy regex expression by removing the last `?` and adding anything between the `>` and the `$`.

Also, the last `>` is useless, because the `\S+` will always match this character already. 

Last but not least, this regex is only useful on comments. I think this should be more open to any url in general, as even basic one could be really long quickly.

### Configuration

_No response_

### Command used

```shell
n\a
```


### Pylint output

```shell
n\a
```


### Expected behavior

Recognize that a simple url that is simple in is construction should be accepted.

And get an extra boost of performance by skipping one token (big perf....). 

### Pylint version

```shell
pylint 2.17.4
astroid 2.15.4
Python 3.11.4 (tags/v3.11.4:d2340ef, Jun  7 2023, 05:45:37) [MSC v.1934 64 bit (AMD64)]
```

title: False negative ``redefined-outer-name`` when an import aliased to ``_`` is redefined in a function
body:
### Bug description
```python
#/usr/bin/env python

import math as _


class MyClass:
    def mymeth(self):
        _ = 123
        print(_.abs(-123))
```

Notice that  the sample Python redefines the global `_` inside the method. This should throw a [W0621](https://pylint.readthedocs.io/en/latest/user_guide/messages/warning/redefined-outer-name.html) error, but does not.

body:
### Bug description

I think I get false positives when I use a 

error.py:
```python
from abc import ABCMeta, abstractmethod
import asyncio
from contextlib import AbstractAsyncContextManager, asynccontextmanager

class Base(metaclass=ABCMeta):
    @abstractmethod
    def get(self) -> AbstractAsyncContextManager[str]:
        pass

class Implementation(Base):
    @asynccontextmanager
    async def get(self):
        yield "hello"

async def main():
    async with Implementation().get() as res:
        print(res)

if __name__ == "__main__":

```

title: Weird redefined-variable-type warning for IntEnum
body:
### Bug description

`redefined-variable-type` shows weir warning for `enum.IntEnum`:

```python
import enum

class Transition(enum.IntEnum):
    ONE_ONE     = 1
    ONE_TWO     = 2
    ONE_THREE   = 3
    TWO_TWO     = 4
    TWO_THREE   = 5
    THREE_THREE = 6

class State(enum.IntEnum):
    ONE   = 1
    TWO   = 2
    THREE = 3

state1 = State.ONE
state2 = State.ONE
transition = None
if state1 == State.ONE:
    if state2 == State.ONE:
        transition = Transition.ONE_ONE
    elif state2 == State.TWO:
        transition = Transition.ONE_TWO
    elif state2 == State.THREE:
        transition = Transition.ONE_THREE
    else:
        pass
elif state1 == State.TWO:
    if state2 == State.ONE:
        transition = Transition.ONE_TWO  # [redefined-variable-type]
    elif state2 == State.TWO:
        transition = Transition.TWO_TWO
    elif state2 == State.THREE:
        transition = Transition.TWO_THREE
    else:
        pass
```

### Command used

```shell
pylint --load-plugins=pylint.extensions.redefined_variable_type --enable=all pylint_test.py
```


### Pylint output

```shell
************* Module pylint_test
pylint_test.py:1:0: C0114: Missing module docstring (missing-module-docstring)
pylint_test.py:3:0: C0115: Missing class docstring (missing-class-docstring)
pylint_test.py:11:0: C0115: Missing class docstring (missing-class-docstring)
pylint_test.py:18:0: C0103: Constant name "transition" doesn't conform to UPPER_CASE naming style (invalid-name)
pylint_test.py:30:8: R0204: Redefinition of transition type from test.Transition.ONE_THREE to pylint_test.Transition.ONE_TWO (redefined-variable-type)

-------------------------------------------------------------------
Your code has been rated at 8.39/10 (previous run: 10.00/10, -1.61)
```


### Expected behavior

If check meant redefinition from `None` to `Transition`, such warnings should be shown for every assignment. If not, warning should not be there.

### Pylint version

```shell
pylint 2.17.4
astroid 2.15.5
Python 3.11.4 (main, Jun 26 2023, 16:57:25) [GCC 7.5.0]
```

title: W1404:  implicit-str-concat is not working with f strings
body:
### Current problem

W1404 doesn't work for implicit concatenation of f strings.

Example: 
`raise ValueError(f"{a} = " f"{b}.")`

It correctly raises error for: 
`raise ValueError("a =" "b.")`

### Desired solution

`raise ValueError(f"{a} =" f"{b}.") `
should raise 
`Implicit string concatenation found in %s`

### Additional context

These weird f string breaks can be generated when migrating to Black formater.

title: Extend consider-using-f-string to string concatenations
body:
### Current problem

It'll be great to extend `consider-using-f-string` to string concatenations:

For example:

```
value1 = 'test'
value2 = 'Add ' + value1 + ' to other value'  # [consider-using-f-string]
value3 = 0
value4 = ''
value4 += 'Add ' + str(value3) + ' to yet other value'  # [consider-using-f-string]
```

### Desired solution

Show warning for code with string concatenations. Such expressions, especially with multiple variables, may be long and better expressed with f-string.

_No response_

title: False positive unnecessary-dunder-call for __iadd__
body:
### Bug description

the following code generates an `unnecessary-dunder-call` message
```python
"https://github.com/pylint-dev/pylint/issues/8978"

class Incrementable:
    "class with an incrementable field"
    def __init__(self, a):
        self.a = a
    def __iadd__(self, other):
        if isinstance(other, Incrementable):
            self.a += other.a
            return self
        return NotImplemented

d = {}
d.setdefault("a", Incrementable(6)).__iadd__(Incrementable(7))
```

I see 2 work-arounds, both pollute the namespace:
1. define an intermediate variable:
```
x = d.setdefault("a", Incrementable(6))
x += Incrementable(7)
```

2. Import [`operator`](https://docs.python.org/3/library/operator.html):
```
import operator
operator.iadd(d.setdefault("a", Incrementable(6)), Incrementable(7))
```

Using [`defaultdict`](https://docs.python.org/3/library/collections.html#defaultdict)  is even worse - I will have to convert it back to the ordinary dict for serialization...

### Configuration

_No response_

### Command used

```shell
pylint z.py
```


### Pylint output

```shell
************* Module z
z.py:14:0: C2801: Unnecessarily calls dunder method __iadd__. Use += operator. (unnecessary-dunder-call)
```


### Expected behavior

no diagnostics - or better one.
since `+=`  requires an extra variable, it is not an acceptable recommendation.

### Pylint version

```shell
pylint 2.17.5
astroid 2.15.6
Python 3.10.12 (main, Jun 11 2023, 05:26:28) [GCC 11.4.0]
```

windows, linux

_No response_

title: False positive `protected-access` and `unused-private-member` with Singleton Pattern
body:
### Bug description

```python
from __future__ import annotations
from tomllib import load
from typing import Optional, Any

class Config:
    __shared: Optional[Config] = None

    def __init__(self, config_path: str) -> None:
        with open(config_path, 'rb') as config_file:
            self.__dict = load(config_file)

    @staticmethod
    def get(key_path: str) -> Optional[Any]:
        keys = key_path.split('.')
        keys_size = len(keys)
        if not Config.__shared:
            return None
        current_dict = Config.__shared.__dict.copy()
        value: Any | None = None
        for position, key in enumerate(keys):
            if position == keys_size - 1:
                value = current_dict[key] # last element
                break
            current_dict = dict[str, Any](current_dict[key])
        return value

    @staticmethod
    def initialize(config_path: str) -> None:
        if Config.__shared is not None:
            return
        Config.__shared = Config(config_path)
```

title: False positive `access-member-before-definition` (E0203) if member is defined in parent class in different module
body:
### Bug description

Directory structure:

```
pkg
├── __init__.py
├── mod1.py
└── mod2.py
```

`__init__.py`:
```python
from .mod1 import *
```

`mod1.py`:
```python
from . import mod2

class SubClass(mod2.SuperClass):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        self.attr2 = None

    def meth1(self):
        if not self.attr1:
            return
        self.attr1 = "Something"
```

`mod2.py`:
```python
class SuperClass:
    def __init__(self):
        self.attr1 = None
```

Pylint produces both `access-member-before-definition` (`E0203`) and `attribute-defined-outside-init` for the accesses to `attr1` in `meth1`.

Clearly `attr1` is defined in the `SuperClass` constructor (which is called from `SubClass`).

I have noticed that the error and warning disappear for the following small changes:

* If the `__init__` method in `SubClass` is omitted
* If the import in `__init__.py` is removed or changed to importing only specific symbols (anything except `mod2` or `SubClass`) from `mod1` (such a symbol would have to be added to `mod1` first though).

### Configuration

_No response_

### Command used

```shell
pylint --disable=C,R0903 pkg
```


### Pylint output

```shell
************* Module pkg.mod1
pkg/mod1.py:9:15: E0203: Access to member 'attr1' before its definition line 11 (access-member-before-definition)
pkg/mod1.py:11:8: W0201: Attribute 'attr1' defined outside __init__ (attribute-defined-outside-init)

------------------------------------------------------------------
Your code has been rated at 5.38/10 (previous run: 5.38/10, +0.00)
```


### Expected behavior

Pylint should not produce `E0203` or `W0201` for `attr1`.

### Pylint version

```shell
pylint 2.17.5
astroid 2.15.6
Python 3.11.5 (main, Sep  5 2023, 17:44:34) [GCC 12.3.0]
```

This also happens on `main` and I originally found this with pylint 2.4.4, astroid 2.3.3, and python 3.8.5


### OS / Environment

Ubuntu 23.04

### Additional dependencies

Not sure this is needed, but here goes:

title: E0110 False positive when using attrs.field
body:
### Bug description

When defining an abstract class with an abstract property and using `attrs` to define a concrete type, pylint will report a false-positive E0110 on instantiating the concrete type if the property is initialized with `attrs.field()`:

```python
from __future__ import annotations
from abc import ABC, abstractmethod
import attrs


class Abstract(ABC):
    @property
    @abstractmethod
    def my_prop(self) -> int:
        ...


@attrs.define
class Concrete(Abstract):
    my_prop: int


@attrs.define
class Concrete_FalsePositive(Abstract):
    my_prop: int = attrs.field()


conc = Concrete(35)
conc_fp = Concrete_FalsePositive(99)

print(f"conc:{conc.my_prop} fp:{conc_fp.my_prop}")
```

title: Determine if method is abstract using `__isabstractmethod__` instead of `abstractmethod` decorator
body:
### Current problem

I am trying to use my own decorator, with its own implementation, to denote abstract methods.

Currently, it appears we are determining abstraction solely on the [decorator used](https://github.com/pylint-dev/pylint/blob/fbbf7880b2e55673bb0e4ba0b1c6c06ca677d5d6/pylint/checkers/utils.py#L948).

The [decorator must be named](https://github.com/pylint-dev/pylint/blob/fbbf7880b2e55673bb0e4ba0b1c6c06ca677d5d6/pylint/checkers/utils.py#L45-L50) one of the following:

 - abc.abstractproperty
 - abc.abstractmethod
 - abc.abstractclassmethod
 - abc.abstractstaticmethod

### Desired solution

If a function is found to have the property `__isabstractmethod__` set to `True`, then this would suffice in determining if the method is abstract. Based on [Python's source code](https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/b9831e5c98de280870b6d932033b868ef56fa2fa/Lib/abc.py#L7-L25), [this parameter](https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/b9831e5c98de280870b6d932033b868ef56fa2fa/Lib/abc.py#L24) is the only implementation detail from the [`abstractmethod` decorator](https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/b9831e5c98de280870b6d932033b868ef56fa2fa/Lib/abc.py#L7-L25).

Simplify put, if a function is found to have the attribute `__isabstractmethod__` set to `True`, then the pylint check [https://github.com/microsoft/pyright/blob/d58286a459e5a8afdd954e3e6033faa48cab5c0f/docs/configuration.md#reportGeneralTypeIssues](reportGeneralTypeIssues) should be satisfied.

### Additional context

_No response_

title: docstyle doesn't work with --from-stdin
body:
### Bug description

When using --from-stdin, the docstyle plugin still reads the file directly. If the line where the doc string is in stdin happens to match with another string in the file on disk, then it will give an error about an invalid docstring quote.

In a situation where you might be running pylint from a git commit that is not the current working copy, this could be very common.

This patch would fix the issue, but it's not very efficient, doesn't check for exceptions, etc.


```patch
--- pylint/extensions/docstyle.py        2023-09-19 15:31:45.561690710 -0600
+++ pylint/extensions/docstyle-fixed.py  2023-09-19 15:31:55.425789319 -0600
@@ -65,7 +65,11 @@
 
         if docstring:
             lineno = node.fromlineno + 1
-            line = linecache.getline(node.root().file, lineno).lstrip()
+            root = node.root()
+            if root.file_bytes:
+                line = str(root.file_bytes.split(b"\n")[lineno-1]).lstrip()
+            else:
+                line = linecache.getline(root.file, lineno).lstrip()
             if line and line.find('"""') == 0:
                 return
             if line and "'''" in line:
```


### Configuration

_No response_

### Command used

Create 2 files

hello.py
```python
"""This is a module docstring"""


def hello():
    """This is a function docstring"""
    arr = [
        "hello",
        "world"
    ]
    return arr
```

hello_modified.py
```python
"""This is a module docstring"""

# This file has two lines added
# line 2

def hello():
    """This is a function docstring"""
    arr = [
        "hello",
        "world"
    ]
    return arr
```

Run this command: `cat hello_modified.py | pylint --from-stdin --load-plugins=pylint.extensions.docstyle hello.py`


### Pylint output

```python
************* Module hello
hello.py:6:0: C0198: Bad docstring quotes in function, expected """, given " (bad-docstring-quotes)

------------------------------------------------------------------
Your code has been rated at 6.67/10 (previous run: 6.67/10, +0.00)
```


### Expected behavior

I expected that there would be no errors, just as if running pylint directly on hello_modified.py

### Pylint version

```shell
pylint 2.13.8
astroid 2.11.3
Python 3.8.10+chromium.25 (default, Sep 27 2022, 04:18:13) 
[GCC 10.2.1 20210130 (Red Hat 10.2.1-11)]

title: Undeserved unexpected-line-ending-format on Windows
body:
### Bug description

```python
pylint modifies source code that it reads from stdin.

See `...\pylint\lint\pylinter.py`


def _read_stdin() -> str:
    # See https://github.com/python/typeshed/pull/5623 for rationale behind assertion
    assert isinstance(sys.stdin, TextIOWrapper)
    sys.stdin = TextIOWrapper(sys.stdin.detach(), encoding="utf-8")
    return sys.stdin.read()
```

See TextIOWrapper https://docs.python.org/3/library/io.html#io.TextIOWrapper
```
if newline is None, universal newlines mode is enabled. Lines in the input can end in '\n', '\r', or '\r\n', and these are translated into '\n' before being returned to the caller.
```

Please add newline="" to TextIOWrapper constructor.

```
def _read_stdin() -> str:
    # See https://github.com/python/typeshed/pull/5623 for rationale behind assertion
    assert isinstance(sys.stdin, TextIOWrapper)
    sys.stdin = TextIOWrapper(sys.stdin.detach(), encoding="utf-8", newline="")
    return sys.stdin.read()
```

### Configuration

_No response_

### Command used

```shell
@echo off
REM ~ Clean up.
erase some_file.py
rmdir /S /Q .venv
REM ~ Generate a python file with CRLF line ending.
(
  echo """Some docstring"""
)>"some_file.py"
REM ~ Generate environment.
py -3.11-64 -m venv .venv
call .venv\Scripts\activate.bat
python -m pip install pylint > nul
@echo on

REM ~ Run pylint with and without --from-stdin.

REM ~ pylint does issues an unexpected-line-ending-format warning.

title: Do not raise ``too-many-instance-attributes`` on dataclasses or with a higher threshold
body:
### Current problem

Dataclasses sometimes deliberately have a lot of arguments, which is also the purpose of the classes. With normal classes, methods and functions it makes sense if the argument list is not too long. 

### Desired solution

Therefore a conscious distinction between dataclass and normal class would be meaningful with the argument number.

### Additional context

_No response_

title: Ellipsis in Protocol methods not recognized in 'assignment-from-no-return' on methods of dataclass members
body:
### Bug description

```python
from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Protocol

class Blah(Protocol):
    def do_thing(self) -> str:
        ...

@dataclass
class BlahUser:
    blah: Blah
    def do_thing_with_blah(self):
        value = self.blah.do_thing()
        return value
```

Pylint's `assignment-from-no-return` rule issues the following error

> Assigning result of a function call, where the function has no return

But `Blah` is a protocol, there's no expectation it should return.

I can `raise` in the body of `Blah` instead. Not using `dataclass` is another alternative.

### Configuration

_No response_

### Command used

```shell
I received this error through the Pylint VS Code extension
```


### Pylint output

```python
Assigning result of a function call, where the function has no return
```


### Expected behavior

I would expect no error.

### Pylint version

```shell
v2023.7.12711014
```


### OS / Environment

Ubuntu Linux

### Additional dependencies

_No response_

title: False positive E1121 when using multiple inheritance and overriding class names
body:
### Bug description

Originally found this via VSCode while using the google cloud API, tried my best to turn it into a minimal example.

If we have a file `classes.py`:
```python
class A:
    def func(self, arg1, arg2):
        super().func([arg1, arg2])


class B:
    def func(self, args):
        print(args)


class B(A, B):
    pass
```

and a second file `weird.py`:
``` python
from classes import B

obj = B()
obj.func(1, 2)
```
we get an unexpected E1121 on line 4.

The file runs without any errors, and this doesn't happen when the classes are declared in the same file that calls `func`, or if the final class has a different name (e.g. "C"). Also doesn't seem to happen with single inheritance (e.g. declaring a class "A" that subclasses the original "A" and overwrites `func`)

Possibly related to https://github.com/pylint-dev/pylint/issues/1123 ?


### Configuration

_No response_

### Command used

```shell
pylint weird.py
```


### Pylint output

```python
************* Module weird
weird.py:1:0: C0114: Missing module docstring (missing-module-docstring)
weird.py:4:0: E1121: Too many positional arguments for method call (too-many-function-args)
```


### Expected behavior

I would expect no E1121, since the program runs through and we (correctly) get no E1121 when we move the class definition into the main file

### Pylint version

```shell
pylint 2.17.7
astroid 2.15.8
Python 3.11.5 (main, Sep 11 2023, 13:54:46) [GCC 11.2.0]
```


### OS / Environment

_No response_

### Additional dependencies

_No response_

title: Pydantic Field(alias=) is not recognized by Pylint, warns E1123 unexpected-keyword-arg
body:
### Bug description

I'm not entirely sure if this is a bug, but it's unexpected behavior that I hope could be resolved in Pylint. It might be related to https://github.com/pylint-dev/astroid/pull/1178

I'm using the [dataclasses feature](https://docs.pydantic.dev/latest/usage/dataclasses/) in Pydantic v2.4.2 and I have a dataclass with an aliased field, like so:

`example.py`

```python
from pydantic import Field
from pydantic.dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass
class Example:
    number: int = Field(alias='n')


example = Example(n=5)
```

This is valid code and runs just fine, but pylint complains with the following

`E1123: Unexpected keyword argument 'n' in constructor call (unexpected-keyword-arg)`

It's maybe worth noting that pylint does *not* complain about the alias usage when the class is a `BaseModel` subclass rather than a dataclass.

```python
from pydantic import Field, BaseModel


class Example2(BaseModel):
    number: int = Field(alias='n')


example = Example2(n=5)
```

### Configuration

_No response_

### Command used

```shell
pylint example.py
```


### Pylint output

```python
************* Module example
example.py:14:10: E1123: Unexpected keyword argument 'n' in constructor call (unexpected-keyword-arg)
```


### Expected behavior

No warning would be reported because `n` is a valid keyword argument to `__init__()` for the `Example` class.

### Pylint version

```shell
pylint 2.17.5
astroid 2.15.6
Python 3.10.12 (main, Jun 11 2023, 05:26:28) [GCC 11.4.0]
```


### OS / Environment

Ubuntu 22.04.3 LTS

```python
pydantic==2.4.2
```

title: False positive: unpacking-non-sequence after return
body:
### Bug description

```python
class DubDependency(ExternalDependency):
    # dub program and version
    class_dubbin: T.Optional[T.Tuple[ExternalProgram, str]] = None
    class_dubbin_searched = False

    def __init__(self, name: str, environment: 'Environment', kwargs: T.Dict[str, T.Any]):

[...]

        if DubDependency.class_dubbin is None:
            if self.required:

title: Possible used-before-assignment false positive on type hinting identical to func names in class
body:
### Bug description
Consider the following class example:

```python


import json
from typing import Optional


class Message:
    message_type: str
    exchange: Optional[str]
    routing_key: str
    payload: dict | str

    def dict(self):
        return {"message_type": self.message_type, "exchange": self.exchange, "payload": self.payload}

    def str(self):
        return json.dumps(self.dict())
```


### Configuration

```ini
[MESSAGES CONTROL]
disable=R,missing-docstring,redefined-outer-name

[FORMAT]
max-line-length=120

[REPORTS]
reports=no

[MASTER]
extension-pkg-whitelist=pydantic

[TYPECHECK]
disable=
    no-self-argument

[TYPECHECK.messages control]
generated-members=pydantic.*
```


### Command used

```shell
pylint --rcfile=.pylintrc message.py
```


### Pylint output

```python
************* Module message
cronenberg/message.py:6:18: E0601: Using variable 'str' before assignment (used-before-assignment)
cronenberg/message.py:9:13: E0601: Using variable 'dict' before assignment (used-before-assignment)
```


### Expected behavior

This is a new behaviour encountered in `pylint 3.0.0`, which did not occur in `2.17.7`. pylint treats type hints as possible func names that are defined later on within the class 

### Pylint version

```shell
pylint 3.0.0
astroid 3.0.0
Python 3.10.12 (main, Jun 20 2023, 17:00:24) [Clang 14.0.3 (clang-1403.0.22.14.1)]
```

MacOS

title: Usability improvment for ``use-a-generator`` in test assertion
body:
### Current problem

Right now the following code raises a ``use-a-generator`` warning:

```python
def test_use_a_generator():
    assert all([int(i) == i for i in [1, 2, 3, "4"]])
```

Result of ``pytest a.py``:

```python
_____________ test_use_a_generator _____________ 
    def test_use_a_generator():
>       assert all([int(i) == i for i in [1, 2, 3, "4"]])
E       assert False
E        +  where False = all([True, True, True, False])
```

The fix is:
```python
def test_use_a_generator():
    assert all(int(i) == i for i in [1, 2, 3, "4"])
```
But the result of ``pytest a.py`` becomes less clear:

```python
_____________ test_use_a_generator _____________ 
    def test_use_a_generator():
>       assert all(int(i) == i for i in [1, 2, 3, "4"])
E       assert False
E        +  where False = all(<generator object test_use_a_generator.<locals>.<genexpr> at 0x7fd1b67eaf80>)
```
And the expected performance improvement is not easy to defend because most of the time the test should pass so all the values will have to be calculated. When the test fail the first thing to do will be to revert the changes

### Desired solution

When a ``use-a-generator`` should be raised in an assert node (inside a code construct detected as a test by pytest/unittest -- named ``test_`` or test classes?) do not raise  ? ``consider-using-a-generator`` raised instead ? Do nothing to keep it simple and stupid ?

title: used-before-assignment false positive
body:
### Bug description

Code below leads to used-before-assignment false positive:
```python
def function(*args):
    print(args)

def wrapper(arg):
    return lambda *args: function(arg, *args)

if __name__ == "__main__":
    args = None
    func = wrapper(1)
    func(2)
```
W0621 warning here is expected.

title: Pylint 3.0.1 | invalid-field-call | false positive
body:
### Bug description
Using a `Field` in a child class of a dataclass seems to trigger `invalid-field-call`


```python
from dataclasses import dataclass, field

@dataclass(init=True, repr=False, eq=False, order=False, unsafe_hash=False, frozen=True)
class Parent():
    name: str = field(init=False)

class Child(Parent):
    age: int = field(init=False)
```


### Configuration

```ini
disable=all
enable=invalid-field-call
```


### Command used

```shell
pylint test_format.py
```


### Pylint output

```python
************* Module test_format
test_format.py:9:15: E3701: Invalid usage of field(), it should be used within a dataclass or the make_dataclass() function. (invalid-field-call)
```


### Expected behavior

I would expect no errors since it's inside a dataclass by inheritance

### Pylint version

```shell
pylint 3.0.1
astroid 3.0.0
Python 3.10.12 (main, Jul  2 2023, 10:24:23) [GCC 13.1.1 20230429]
```


### OS / Environment

Archlinux

### Additional dependencies

_No response_

title: Pylint false report for used-before-assignment
body:
### Bug description

```python
import typing

from marshmallow import fields
from marshmallow import validate


class AdminCancelOrders:
    order_ids: typing.List[int] = fields.List(fields.Int(), required=True, validate=validate.Length(min=1))

    def validate(self):
        pass
```


### Configuration

_No response_

### Command used

```shell
pylint pylint_bug_test.py
```


### Pylint output

```python
pylint pylint_bug_test.py
************* Module pylint_bug_test
pylint_bug_test.py:8:84: E0601: Using variable 'validate' before assignment (used-before-assignment)
```


### Expected behavior

It should report that validate is used before assignement as it did not in 2.7.x

### Pylint version

```shell
pylint 3.0.1
astroid 3.0.0
Python 3.11.2 (main, Mar 10 2023, 07:05:07) [Clang 14.0.0 (clang-1400.0.29.202
```

MacOSX Ventura

```python
marshmallow==2.21.0.2
```

title: Add pylint-junit reporter class
body:
### Current problem

I'm a user of pylint. I used to use pylint2junit. I found out it has not been working since pylint 2.15. I then found pylint-junit which seems to fix some issues in pylint2junit. But it also have had issues since pylint 2.15. pylint2junit does not seem maintained. pylint-junit seems more active.

I would really like to continue to use pylint and I need junit reporting for my (Azure DevOps) pipelines.

### Desired solution

Add `pylint-junit` reporter class to `pylint` to make the junit support more robust.

### Additional context

Proposed by @Pierre-Sassoulas in https://github.com/pylint-dev/pylint/issues/8368#issuecomment-1759674966

title: False positive no-member when calling class method on Annotated type
body:
### Bug description

```python
When calling a class method on a typevar defined using typing.Annotated, pylint erroneously reports no-member. 

The following code will reproduce the error:


from typing import Annotated

class Foo:
    @classmethod
    def foo(cls):
        print("foo called")

Bar = Annotated[Foo, int]

Bar.foo()
```
```


### Configuration

_No response_

### Command used

```shell
pylint test.py
```


### Pylint output

```python
************* Module test
test.py:10:0: E1101: Class 'Annotated' has no 'foo' member (no-member)
```


### Expected behavior

Pylint should understand the annotated typevar can be treated the same as the type being annotated and is not of type Annotated

### Pylint version

```shell
pylint 3.0.1
astroid 3.0.0
Python 3.12.0 | packaged by conda-forge | (main, Oct  3 2023, 08:36:57) [Clang 15.0.7 ]
```


### OS / Environment

MacOS Ventura 13.5.2
conda 23.7.4


### Additional dependencies

_No response_

title: ``no-member`` check does not honor ``sys.version_info``
body:
### Bug description

Running pylint with Python 3.11 provides the ``no-member`` error even if it is shielded with `sys.version_info` check. The `token.FSTRING_START` is introduced in Python 3.12.


```python
#pylint: disable=missing-module-docstring
import token
import sys

if sys.version_info >= (3, 12):
    print(token.FSTRING_START)
```

Running pylint with Python 3.12 works fine as expected.



### Command used

```shell
pylint x.py
```


### Pylint output

```python
************* Module x
x.py:6:10: E1101: Module 'token' has no 'FSTRING_START' member (no-member)

------------------------------------------------------------------

title: attribute-defined-outside-init false positive for attribute defined on subclass after verifying class type
body:
### Bug description

```python
# pylint: disable=missing-docstring
# pylint: disable=too-few-public-methods

class Super:
    def __init__(self):
        pass


class Sub(Super):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        self.some_value = 0


def get_super(x: bool) -> Super:
    if x:
        return Sub()
    return Super()


obj = get_super(True)
assert isinstance(obj, Sub)
obj.some_value = 27
```


### Configuration

_No response_

### Command used

```shell
pylint test.py
```


### Pylint output

```python
************* Module test
test.py:23:0: W0201: Attribute 'some_value' defined outside __init__ (attribute-defined-outside-init)

------------------------------------------------------------------
Your code has been rated at 9.29/10 (previous run: 7.14/10, +2.14)
```


### Expected behavior

No errors: `some_value` is a member of `Sub`, defined in `Sub.__init__`, and we have by line 23 verified that `obj` is of type `Sub`. 

Note that this error is *not* triggered if `get_super` returns `Sub` unconditionally! It seems the `assert` is being ignored.

### Pylint version

```shell
pylint 3.0.1
astroid 3.0.1
Python 3.11.5 (main, Aug 29 2023, 19:39:22) [GCC 12.3.1 20230526]
```


### OS / Environment

_No response_

### Additional dependencies

_No response_

title: pylint does not support typing.Self when override
body:
### Bug description

The following code in python-3.11 and in previous python using `typing_extensions`:

```python
import typing

class A:
    @classmethod
    def f1(cls) -> typing.Self:
        return cls()

class B(A):
    @classmethod
    def f1(cls) -> typing.Self:
        return super(B, cls).f1()

    def f2(self):
        pass

B.f1().f2()
```

Results in:
```
E1101: Instance of 'A' has no 'f2' member; maybe 'f1'? (no-member)
```

mypy does work correctly.

### Configuration

_No response_

### Command used

```shell
pylint a.py
```

### Pylint output

### Expected behavior

title: import-private-name false positive
body:
### Bug description

**tldr;** importing from the protected submodule does not fire warnings.


```python
Consider the following structure:


src/my_main_package/
├── __init__.py
├── accessor_broken1.py
├── accessor_broken2.py
└── my_package
    ├── __init__.py
    ├── _protected_submodule.py
    └── valid_internal_access.py


`my_package._protected_submodule` contains a class `SomeClass` like this:

```python
"""This submodule is protected and its public members should only be accessed by siblings in the same module."""


class SomeClass:
    """This class is public in a protected module and should be accessible by sibling files, like valid_access.py."""
```

Its siblings `valid_internal_access.py` and the `__init__.py` (both in `my_package`) are the only two files that should be allowed to import from it.

This what `valid_internal_access.py` could look like:

```python
"""This should work and could be used to expose SomeClass in the __init__.py"""
from _protected_submodule import SomeClass

SomeClass()
```

Consider the `__init__.py` in `my_package` does **not** import the protected module.
And therefore no expose it to other external classes.

There are then two broken accessor examples, which both should not be able to import the protected class without warnings:

```python accessor_broken1.py
"""This is an external accessor, which should not access _protected_submodule like this."""
from my_main_package.my_package import _protected_submodule

t2 = _protected_submodule.SomeClass()
```

and variant two:

```python accessor_broken2.py
"""This should not either."""
from my_main_package.my_package._protected_submodule import SomeClass

t = SomeClass()
```


### Configuration

```ini
[MAIN]
load-plugins= """
    pylint.extensions.private_import
    """
```


### Command used

```shell
pylint .
```


### Pylint output

```python
-------------------------------------------------------------------
Your code has been rated at 10.00/10 (previous run: 8.57/10, +1.43)
```


### Expected behavior

From the description of **C2701** I would have expected this to result in two pylint errors.
Am I missing something? Or is this a bug?

> Used when a **private module** or object prefixed with _ is imported.

__https://pylint.pycqa.org/en/stable/user_guide/messages/convention/import-private-name.html__

### Pylint version

```shell
pylint 2.17.4
astroid 2.15.6
Python 3.9.18 (main, Oct 17 2023, 12:33:47) 
[GCC 13.2.1 20230801]
```


### OS / Environment

Archlinux (should not be relevant)

### Additional dependencies

_No response_

title: R1732(consider-using-with): Regression on `contextlib.ExitStack`
body:
### Bug description


This is extremely similar to https://github.com/pylint-dev/pylint/issues/4654, but is somehow still flagged in my case. 

I can see 2 possible distinctions. 

1. I use  `stack.enter_context(tempfile.TemporaryDirectory())` and `stack.enter_context(tempfile.NamedTemporaryFile(...))` instead of `stack.enter_context(open(...))` as in the original issue.

2. The `with contextlib.ExitStack` block is not directly in the same context/scope as where `stack.enter_context` is called. 

For example:

```python
def util_func(stack):
    tmpdir = stack.enter_context(tempfile.TemporaryDirectory())
    # do stuff
    return tmpdir

def test_something():
    with contextlib.ExitStack() as stack:
        tmpdir = util_func(stack)
        stack.enter_context(tempfile.NamedTemporaryFile(dir=tmpdir))
        # ...
```


### Configuration

see https://github.com/crim-ca/weaver/blob/master/.pylintrc

### Command used

```shell
pylint \
    --load-plugins pylint_quotes \
    --rcfile=./.pylintrc \
    --reports y \
    ./weaver ./tests
```


### Pylint output

```python
************* Module tests.functional.test_builtin
tests/functional/test_builtin.py:93:38: R1732: Consider using 'with' for resource-allocating operations (consider-using-with)
tests/functional/test_builtin.py:401:38: R1732: Consider using 'with' for resource-allocating operations (consider-using-with)
tests/functional/test_builtin.py:403:12: R1732: Consider using 'with' for resource-allocating operations (consider-using-with)
```


### Expected behavior

No error for `stack.enter_context(tempfile.TemporaryDirectory())` and `stack.enter_context(tempfile.NamedTemporaryFile(...))`. 


### Pylint version

```shell
Name: pylint
Version: 2.17.4
Summary: python code static checker
Home-page: 
Author: 
Author-email: Python Code Quality Authority <code-quality@python.org>
License: GPL-2.0-or-later
Location: /home/francis/dev/miniconda/envs/weaver-py310/lib/python3.10/site-packages
Requires: astroid, dill, isort, mccabe, platformdirs, tomli, tomlkit
Required-by: pylint-quotes
```

Ubuntu 20.04.6

_No response_

title: Inconsistent behavior with circular import on MacOS
body:
### Bug description

The issue appeared when the code allegedly had a circular dependency. `pylint` did not complained on a MacOS machine, but did complain on Linux. However, I was able to narrow it down to a file-system (???) issue by demonstrating an inconsistent behavior when running inside a container on a local folder versus a mounted folder (when the host is a MacOS).

I added details and how-to reproduce [here](https://github.com/guyarad/pylint-macos-issue)

TL;DR - to reproduce on MacOS run:

```shell
git clone git@github.com:guyarad/pylint-macos-issue.git
cd pylint-macos-issue
make bug_repro
```

### Configuration

_No response_

### Command used

```shell
pylint --recursive=y --disable=W,C0114,C0115,C0116,R0903 -s=n .
```


### Pylint output

Inconsistent - on Linux:

```python
************* Module module1.derived
module1/derived.py:1:0: R0401: Cyclic import (module1 -> module1.derived) (cyclic-import)
```
But on a MacOS it was successful.


### Expected behavior

Consistent behavior when running on a mounted folder and local folder (when all files are identical).

### Pylint version

```shell

title: Better docs required for 2 'design' checkers
body:
Pylint has 2 checkers named `design`:

1. a standard checker;
2. an optional checker which can be loaded by specifying `--load-plugin=pylint.extensions.mccabe`.

That confused me, because I don't want the standard `design` checker, and I thought I was avoiding it by not loading `pylint.extensions.mccabe`, but I was wrong. I suggest making this clearer in the documentation.

There is an additional problem in the documentation. In the [section](https://pylint.readthedocs.io/en/stable/user_guide/configuration/all-options.html#design-checker) for the standard `design` checker, `--max-complexity` is listed, even though it is only available if you load `pylint.extensions.mccabe`. That's clear from the following shell script:

```sh
#!/bin/sh

echo Pylint details:
echo
pylint --version
echo

echo Without plugin:
echo
pylint --help |
  egrep max-complexity
echo

echo With plugin:
echo
pylint --load-plugins=pylint.extensions.mccabe --help |
  egrep max-complexity
```

When I run that, I get:

```
Pylint details:

pylint 3.0.1
astroid 3.0.1
Python 3.12.0 (main, Oct  3 2023, 04:51:45) [GCC 10.2.1 20210110]

Without plugin:


With plugin:

  --max-complexity <int>
```

title: False positive circular dependency detection
body:
### Bug description

The code is given in this [repository](https://github.com/guyarad/pylint-macos-issue).
The detection root cause is the `Step` class definition. However, since we import the class first in the package's `__init__.py` file, the import is not circular by Python's rules. 

We found 2 ways to solve it.

1. Reasonable solution - instead of import the base class from the package level, import directly from the containing module.
2. Unclear solution - adding `__init__.py` at the repository root level. Not sure why this makes the issue disappear.

To summarize - there are 2 related issues here:
1. The circular dependency detection is not accurate. Demonstrated by `make test`.
2. One of the mitigations to the issue is unexpected (if the issue is real, I wouldn't expect adding that file will resolve it).

To reproduce the issue and show the 2 resolutions work, simply run `make verify_resolutions` in [the repository](https://github.com/pylint-dev/pylint/issues/9168).

[Related issue](https://github.com/pylint-dev/pylint/issues/9168)

### Configuration

_No response_

### Command used

```shell
pylint --recursive=y --disable=W,C0114,C0115,C0116,R0903 -s=n .
```


### Pylint output

```python
************* Module module1.derived

title: False positive unsubscriptable-object with MutableMapping / pygtrie
body:
### Bug description

```python
test.py:

from collections.abc import MutableMapping
from typing import cast
import pygtrie

T = MutableMapping[str, dict[str, int]]

if __name__ == '__main__':
    x: T = pygtrie.CharTrie({'a': {'b': 'c'}})
    y: pygtrie.CharTrie[str, dict[str, int]] = pygtrie.CharTrie({'a': {'b': 'c'}})
    z: dict[str, dict[str, int]] = {'a': {'b': 'c'}}

    # x and y print() lines trigger unsubscriptable-object, but z does not:
    print(x['a']['b'])
    print(cast(dict[str, dict[str, int]], x['a'])['b'])
    print(cast(pygtrie.CharTrie[str, dict[str, int]], x['a'])['b'])
    print(y['a']['b'])
    print(cast(T, y['a'])['b'])
    print(cast(dict[str, dict[str, int]], y['a'])['b'])
    print(z['a']['b'])
    print(cast(T, z['a'])['b'])
    print(cast(pygtrie.CharTrie[str, dict[str, int]], z['a'])['b'])
```
```

title: False positive: Class has no '__dataclass_fields__' member (no-member)
body:
### Bug description
The code:

```python
from dataclasses import dataclass


@dataclass
class Youpi:
    yapi: int


print(Youpi.__dataclass_fields__)
```


### Configuration

_No response_

### Command used

```shell
pylint repro.py
```


### Pylint output

```python
$ pylint repro.py 
************* Module repro
repro.py:1:0: C0114: Missing module docstring (missing-module-docstring)
repro.py:5:0: C0115: Missing class docstring (missing-class-docstring)
repro.py:9:6: E1101: Class 'Youpi' has no '__dataclass_fields__' member (no-member)
```


### Expected behavior

Youpi is a dataclass, so it has a `__dataclass_fields__` attribute, yet pylint tells it has not.

At runtime it works.

In the other hand, neither the PEP nor the doc tells about `__dataclass_fields__`, so it could be considered "private" / "not guaranteed to be kept in future versions" / "unsafe to use".

I personally don't think so, by [Hyrum's Law](https://www.hyrumslaw.com/) people already depend on it. Also it's mentionned in typeshed: https://github.com/python/typeshed/blob/40caa050ce66a8dd62a62c4b4631767c4092d998/stdlib/_typeshed/__init__.pyi#L314

### Pylint version

```shell
$ pylint --version

astroid 2.15.6

body:
### Current problem

[`duplicate-code`](https://pylint.readthedocs.io/en/latest/user_guide/messages/refactor/duplicate-code.html
) check is useful, but sometimes for 5-lines of similarity it's overly pedantic.

### Desired solution

The request is to be able to configure the minimum number of duplicated lines that can trigger `duplicate-code`.

And then document it on https://pylint.readthedocs.io/en/latest/user_guide/messages/refactor/duplicate-code.html.

title: --recursive option does not find .py files in sub-directories of packages
body:
### Bug description
When running `pylint --recursive=y` on a directory that contains both a package and a sub-directory, pylint correctly locates and lints all py files, both those in the non-package sub-directory and those in the package.

However, when running `pylint --resursive=y` on a package that contains both a sub-package and a sub-directory, pylint **will not** locate or lint any files in the sub-directory.

Example structure:
```python

- tmp
  - src1
    - my_package
      * __init__.py
      * foo.py
    - scripts
      * bar.py
  - src2
    * __init__.py
    - my_package
      * __init__.py
      * foo.py
    - scripts
      * bar.py
```


The only difference between `src1` and `src2` is that `src2` is a package.

Running `pylint --recursive=y ./tmp/src1` will find/lint both `foo.py` and `bar.py`.
Running `pylint --recursive=y ./tmp/src2` will **not** find/lint `bar.py`


### Configuration

_No response_

### Command used

```shell
pylint --recursive ./src2
```


### Pylint output

```python
************* Module src2.package1.foo
lint-test/src2/package1/foo.py:1:0: C0114: Missing module docstring (missing-module-docstring)
lint-test/src2/package1/foo.py:1:0: C0104: Disallowed name "foo" (disallowed-name)
lint-test/src2/package1/foo.py:2:0: W0107: Unnecessary pass statement (unnecessary-pass)
lint-test/src2/package1/foo.py:1:0: W0611: Unused import sys (unused-import)
```


### Expected behavior

bar.py is linted as intended by #5682

```
************* Module foo
lint-test/src1/scripts/foo.py:1:0: C0114: Missing module docstring (missing-module-docstring)
lint-test/src1/scripts/foo.py:1:0: C0104: Disallowed name "foo" (disallowed-name)
lint-test/src1/scripts/foo.py:2:0: W0107: Unnecessary pass statement (unnecessary-pass)

************* Module package1.foo

lint-test/src1/package1/foo.py:1:0: W0611: Unused import sys (unused-import)

```

```
